title: Sound and the Fury review
subject: machine-learning
contentHtml:
Signet classic -- the appendix was prior to the book and added a lot of detail that I wouldn't have guessed otherwise. The part that I remember the most is how insufferable Jason IV is; he alone has a socially constructed identity. He ends up happy, though. The recollection of personal responsibility throughout the novel is brutal and all-consuming. 

There isn't a ton of interfamilial dynamics outside of Quentin and Caddy: two of the three perspectives taken are from outsiders, Benjy and Quentin. I wish that there was more time spent on the father figure, Jason III: the appendix paints a sympathetic picture of him. 

To understand the Compson's decline better I would have to have a more idealized image of a great Southern family. It seems like Faulkner takes for granted that readers intuit how great life was for them before they stopped owning plantations.
preview: Signet classic -- the appendix was prior to the book

title: The Canon
subject: machine-learning
contentHtml:
If the electrons are to take on any organized, extracurricular activities, they must feel inspired. They must be animated. They must eat.

power plants create electric currents by spinning *copper coils* inside of giant magnets at very high speeds. This hyperexcites the electrons inside the coil and and they are then transmitted along power lines.

Charging is segregating existing electrons so that they can run again.

- covalent bond: two molecules sharing electrons to be satiated.

- molecule: usually refer to covalent bonded substance, but also other bonds: such as sodium chloride (ionically bonded, two words not one?). Condiments, eggshells, household cleaning supplies. Ionic bonds are stiffer and more predictable than covalent bonds.

- Ionic bonds join different elements: bond between ions (charged, negative atoms). ion-plus seeks ion-minus. ==Different elements are prone to being negatively or positively ionized==, but they can only be one. 
	- One or two electrons in outer shell == high chance of negative ionization.
	- Almost full outer shells == high chance of positive ionization. 
	- two factors at play: the fact that elements have equal proton and electron counts, but also desire to fill outer shells with electrons.

Sodium want to give away electron -- 2/8/1.
Chlorine want an extra electron: one short in outer shell.
Sodium Chlorine :  Sodium-Chlorine +

- finally metal bonds: "electron sea", fluid electrons moving from one atom to another

H2O is polarized, as the electrons are closer to the oxygen, so the bottom half is more negatively charged. So the integrity and clinginess of water is formed by hydrogen bonding 
In general hydrogen bonding is when hydrogen is attached to heavier molecules, and the bottom molecule is slightly more negatively charged.

- are there other kinds of bonds?

electron antipathy explains why we don't go through things

Pottery clay is held together by the Van Der Waals force: the mild polarization created by a lot of molecules in the same area: electrons seeking protons.

Also a graphite pencil leaving a mark is the Van der Waals force being disrupted.

ionic solids tend to be brittle. 2/3 of human dry weight is carbon. So organic chemistry (carbons chemistry) is important. Carbon is 4 electrons, four holes. Covalent bond between two carbon atoms is very, very strong.  

Breathing: in oxygen, out carbon dioxide

DNA is zipped up by the hydrogen bond

==Proteins are tangled up and pleated by hydrogen bonding==

Frying an egg destroys hydrogen bonds to make egg whites.


Chemistry! Physics 0070! Electricity!
Be more complex, less centered on sweet and salty experiences and find sour, bitter, umami ones.

chemical reactions produce ==new== things.
baking is endothermic in the sense that the chemical reactions are taking in heat.

fruits breath in oxygen and exhale carbon dioxide

""I do not know what I may appear to the world, but to myself I seem to have been only like a boy playing on the sea-shore, and diverting myself in now and then finding a smoother pebble or a prettier shell than ordinary, whilst the great ocean of truth lay all undiscovered before me."

_Indricotherium_: giraffe rhinocerous

Cell division and maintenance genes are the longest lasting and most shared: we share 1/5 of our dna with yeast

Natural selection is the force that transforms drift and randomness into the gift of extravagance. It takes the doctrinaire sloth of the second law of thermodynamics, the tendency of every system to get frowzier over time, and hammers it into a magic, all-purpose, purpose-making machine that turns around and breaks entropy at the knees.

Whales; artiodactyls: hoofed ungulates: pigs, camels, cows, hippos

echidna, pangolin, anteater: large, sickle-shaped claws, long sticky ribon, elongated muzzle without fur, enlarged salivary glands, strong stomach.

Life functions through the specialized chemistry of carbon and water, and builds largely upon four key families of chemicals: lipids for cell membranes, carbohydrates such as sugars, amino acids for protein metabolism, and nucleic acid DNA and RNA for the mechanisms of heredity. Any successful theory of abiogenesis must explain the origins and interactions of these classes of molecules.


A counter to those who say that modernist architecture is soulless
- good architecture prioritizes function over form: form follows function
	- emphasizing the aspatiality of a building, excontemporaneous
	- but the reality is, functional requirements for a space out in the world are often as arbitrary as formal ones.
- Prioritizing function will work over small scale time periods, prioritizing form works over longer ones. Clearly a sphere is ideal in some sense. For what purposes then should it serve? Ornamentation is the natural consequence of living in a space.

title: beta=10 LRE performance hypernyms - misc
subject: machine-learning
contentHtml:
LRE and then Jacobian. The bias term demonstrates generalization in many cases, e.g. from croissant --> food, cup --> beverage, gasoline --> utility. What do we call hierarchical genrealization? Park reports that "Hierarchically related concepts live in orthogonal subspaces". 

So, in order to generalize, we would expect the bias term to be orthogonal to the original Ws term. Let's test this. Hmm, it didn't work. What does the above statement really mean? It says that a hierarchical concept direction is represented by a vector orthogonal to the original space, so we could look at hypernyms - animal for example. We could use the span "animal", "mammal", and try to interpret the bias vector accordingly.

title: Whitening
subject: machine-learning
contentHtml:
A whitening transformation or sphering transformation is a linear transformation that transforms ==a vector of random variables with a known covariance matrix== into ==a set of new variables whose covariance is the identity matrix, meaning that they are uncorrelated and each have variance 1. ==The transformation is called "whitening" because it changes the input vector into a white noise vector.

![[Screenshot 2024-07-04 at 8.52.09 AM.png]]
preview: A whitening transformation or sphering transformation is a linear transformation

title: Antonym - Binary - Several S_O, ln->full tests

contentHtml:
When using abridged gradients, from the layer norm to the output of each layer,
most of the time the results don't display the kind of variance that we would usually like to see.

With LN  and residuals on each layer:

we have vague results at later 
S_O: 23-24
```
after  before [' the', '\n', ' ', ' a', ' out'] ['\n', '...', ' up', ' out', ' ']
```

S_O: 14-15
We have specific but homogenous results earlier. This gives reason to suspect that this is the right area but need more variation.

```
after  before [' upward', ' out', ' outward', ' outer', ' outside'] ['\n', '...', ' out', ' up', ' ']
ahead  behind [' upward', ' out', ' outward', ' outer', ' outside'] ['\n', '...', ' up', ' out', ' ']
anterior  posterior [' upward', ' out', ' outward', ' outer', ' outside'] ['\n', '...', ' ', '...', ' the']
backward  forward [' upward', ' out', ' outward', ' outer', ' outside'] ['\n', '...', ' ', ' up', ' out']
before  after [' upward', ' out', ' outward', ' outer', ' outside'] ['\n', '...', ' out', ' ', ' up']
```

At this point we use Norm, no residual or layer bias for S_S or O_O as that has worked in the past.

S_O: 20-23:

```
after  before ['\n', ' have', ' Lopez', ' The', ' Liu'] ['\n', '...', ' up', ' out', ' ']
ahead  behind ['\n', ' have', ' Lopez', ' The', ' Liu'] ['\n', '...', ' up', ' out', ' ']
anterior  posterior ['\n', ' have', ' Lopez', ' The', ' Liu'] ['\n', '...', ' ', '...', ' the']
backward  forward ['\n', ' have', ' Lopez', ' The', ' Liu'] ['\n', '...', ' ', ' up', ' out']
before  after ['\n', ' have', ' Lopez', ' The', ' Liu'] ['\n', '...', ' out', ' up', ' ']
S_O_START,20,S_O_END,23,beta,1,clre,0,lre,15,lm,47
```

S_O: 12-15:

```
after  before ['-', ',', ' in', ' (', '_'] ['\n', '...', ' out', ' up', ' ']
ahead  behind [' in', ',', '-', ' (', '\n'] ['\n', '...', ' up', ' out', ' ']
anterior  posterior ['-', ',', ' in', ' (', '_'] ['\n', '...', ' ', '...', ' the']
backward  forward ['-', ',', ' in', ' (', '_'] ['\n', '...', ' ', ' up', ' out']
before  after ['-', ',', ' in', ' (', '_'] ['\n', '...', ' out', ' up', ' ']
S_O_START,12,S_O_END,15,beta,1,clre,3,lre,15,lm,47
```

S_O: 12-13:
```
after  before ['\n', ' The', ' have', 'The', ' was'] ['\n', '...', ' out', ' up', ' ']
ahead  behind ['\n', ' The', ' have', 'The', ' the'] ['\n', '...', ' up', ' out', ' ']
anterior  posterior ['\n', ' The', 'The', ' have', ' was'] ['\n', '...', ' ', '...', ' the']
backward  forward ['\n', ' The', 'The', ' have', ' was'] ['\n', '...', ' ', ' up', ' out']
before  after ['\n', ' The', ' have', 'The', ' was'] ['\n', '...', ' out', ' up', ' ']
S_O_START,12,S_O_END,13,beta,1,clre,0,lre,15,lm,48
```

S_O: 15-16:

```

title: Bender is wrong about factual recall
subject: machine-learning
contentHtml: Factual recall has an explicit general meaning, which is the ability of an entity to reproduce data that it has seen. It really doesn't make sense that the bar is set at "human-like recall".

title: Midterm Report
subject: machine-learning
contentHtml:
How the LRE paper works:

### 1. introduction

### 2. background: relations and their representations
2.1 Representations of knowledge in language models
2.2 Neural representations of relations

### 3. finding and validating LREs
3.1 Finding LREs
3.2 Evaluating LREs

### 4. Experiments
4.1 Are LREs faithful to relations?
4.2 Do LREs casually characterize model predictions?
4.3 Where in the network do representations exhibit LREs?

### 5. Application: The Attribute Lens

### 6. Related Work
Representation Probes
Knowledge Representation
Knowledge Extraction

### 7. Conclusion

### ethics statement
### reproducibility statement

### acknowledgements

### references





### related works
### problem statement

My research objective is twofold: (1) determine the relation classes for which LRE is a suboptimal approximator and (2) develop alternative techniques which outperform LRE on both these relation classes, and as a whole. Thus, my work hopes to achieve a better understanding of how transformers model knowledge, and have potential applications in knowledge editing in LLMs.

### approach

### conclusion

title: Layer Normalization
subject: machine-learning
contentHtml:
In this paper, we transpose batch normalization into layer normalization by computing the mean and variance used for normalization from all of the summed inputs to the neurons in a layer on a ==single training case.==

- Alternative to batch normalization
	- distribution of the summed inputs over a minibatch of cases
- Distribution of inputs over each layer per training sample.
- uses same $\mu, \sigma$ across layers for single training case.
- invariant to scaling and shift of weight matrix, but not of weight vectors

title: WeightedLRE or LoRE
subject: machine-learning
contentHtml:
- The LoRE (Linearly oriented Relational Embedding) is suitable for quickly calculating semantic relations with multiple senses. 

- This has implications for tinyML as the operation is easy to run. It should be just a weight and bias, but also needs to hold the input subj. representations for calculation. So does that mean that I need to pass the subject up to that point into the model? maybe

- For example, the English plural can take:
	- y -> ies
	- is -> es
	- ss -> sses
	- fe -> ves
- etc.

- It works by weighting Jacobian inputs by similarity to the input, and calculating a linear approximation for the function accordingly

- It should outperform the original LRE and other methods and just be really good overall.

title: An orphanage
subject: machine-learning
contentHtml:
An orphanage lies among the ruins of solemn-faced storefronts, far in the the desert. Nobody finds it on their own, everyone comes from somewhere inside. They are taught the basic methods of scavenging by those who came before them. The sun is always very hot.  The children go out in the mornings, before the heat warms the ruined asphalt. They look for wreckage, aircraft mostly, small vessels and flyers that have been grounded forever. They find tasty snacks with dog-earred wrappings, blankets, bottles, bags. Japanese tools for trimming and scoring and marking, paper mostly, sometimes skin. Sometimes they find dark and damp spaces where things grow. They do not know to grow things. They know how long the walk is and the last time they ate.

They get their water from a large still underground reservoir. They climb down drains, covers like doors, descend eight iron rungs, three flights of concrete stairs, navigating by touch and instinct. If one brought a light down, clasped between their teeth, they would see a perfect figure eight, almost two rings, lined with concrete. It is a place meant for thousands; but it is also not meant to be used at all. At the very least not in the way they do, cupping hands and sipping straight from the surface.

They are artistically inclined. They find pieces of detritus and bind them with rope and glue to the rooftops of their dwellings. What do they make? They have little use for maps. They are fascinated by animals, birds and lizards and mice. They add to the vertebrae of birds metal wings, put long plastic streamers under dead frogs.

They are interested in fashion. They sort through bins and racks between half-walls and shaded alcoves. Clothing for every possible purpose, each one speaking to a series of future events. Parkas, ski coats, cargo pants. Summer outfits, dresses, swimwear, cocktail attire. Slips and shirts with proclamations of being fixed in time and space. Identities created by an eliminatory process which mean nothing to them now, because there is nothing to take away.

They ignore the conversations in lights during the nighttime from the stars. They ignore the flashing, pleading papers found frequently among the remains of the dead.

The worst time was when they found a plane that crashed behind the grass yards. The lights extinguishing in the night like an ember . But the pilot was still breathing, the emergency beacon tapping out a steady red-black signal to the sky. That pilot woke to find them searching through the scattered packages in the back. He turned off the engine lights and

title: The Pile -- Notes
subject: machine-learning
contentHtml:
We introduce new datasets derived from the fol-
lowing sources: PubMed Central, ArXiv, GitHub, the FreeLaw Project, Stack Exchange, the US Patent and Trademark Office, PubMed, Ubuntu IRC, HackerNews, YouTube, PhilPapers, and NIH ExPorter. In addition, we incorporate several existing high-quality datasets: Books3 (Presser, 2020), Project Gutenberg (PG-19) (Rae et al., 2019), Open Subtitles (Tiedemann, 2016), English Wikipedia, DM Mathematics (Saxton et al., 2019), EuroParl (Koehn, 2005), and the Enron Emails corpus (Klimt
and Yang, 2004).


Pile-CC:  a new filtered subset of Common Crawl

Books3: Bibliotik private tracker (Presser 2020)

Bits per Byte:
LT /LB is 0.29335 GPT-2 tokens/byte across the Pile

![[Screenshot 2024-06-10 at 12.48.22 PM.png]]

The two most comparable publicly available datasets to the Pile are CC-100 (Wenzek et al., 2019) and C4/mC4 (Raffel et al., 2019). C4 is comparably-sized to the Pile, while mC4 and CC- 100 are larger, multilingual datasets. However, C4/mC4 require immense computational resources to preprocess the data, with its maintainers even recommending the use of a distributed cloud service,17 setting a high bar of entry to using these datasets. CC-100 is directly downloadable and pre-cleaned; however, its English portion is much smaller than the Pile.

title: ngrams
subject: machine-learning
contentHtml:
---
aliases:
  - n-grams
---
### perplexity
The inverse geometric mean of an unseen set of test words:

$\sqrt[N]{\frac{1}{P(w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n)}}$

Perplexity-plagiarism tradeoff

Using N-Grams for modeling long-tail distributions is ... not a good idea.
- common words will be overrepresented and rare words will be nonexistent

Sparseness is the big problem with N-grams

Laplace smoothing:
- add 1 to (bigram) matrix

Kneser-Ney smoothing

Counting frequencies stores SEMANTIC and SYNTACTIC and DISCOURSE information...

Linear interpolation:
- Weighted combination of unigram/bigram/trigram

title: Pip vs Conda
subject: machine-learning
contentHtml:
- **Conda installs binary packages, which means the packages include compiled code**. This can make the installation process faster and more reliable, especially for packages with complex dependencies. Pip, by contrast, often installs packages from source, which means the code is compiled during the installation process.Aug 8, 2023

- Conda is a packaging tool and installer that aims to do more than what `pip` does; handle library dependencies _outside_ of the Python packages as well as the Python packages themselves. Conda also creates a virtual environment, like `virtualenv` does.


Install = build + copy to system path (with tracking by package manager)

- Building means compiling the source code to binary in a sandbox location where it won't affect your system if something goes wrong, like a `build` subdirectory inside the source code directory.

- Install means copying the built binaries from the `build` subdirectory to a place in your system path, where they become easily accessible. This is rarely done by a straight copy command, and it's often done by some package manager that can track the files created and easily uninstall them later.

![[Screenshot 2024-06-04 at 8.40.09 AM.png]]

This paper delivers a novel approach to automated priority assessment of bug reports in open 068 bug repositories. First, we create a number of topic models and assign each report to one of them since our approach is based on NLP. This step is based on the work of Xia et al. (Xia et al., 2017). Second, we train a text classifier for each topic, which can predict the priority level of a new bug report that belongs to that topic. For the latter, we deploy BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers)

By predicting the destiny of bug reports in terms of whether they will be eventually resolved as fixed or perhaps labeled as won’t-fix, invalid, or works-for-me, we aim to in- form bug triage and developers in advance and help them set the potentially difficult or problematic bug reports aside. Alternatively, triagers may decide to assign them to more senior developers working on the project, who are expected to have a higher level of expertise and familiarity with the code base. This will save developers time and, consequently, the project’s resources.

title: Modernity and Self Identity
subject: machine-learning
contentHtml:
Anthony Giddens

- "unembodied self": acting out many or all routines
- Staring at yourself in a hall of mirrors

- The feeling that all social interactions are staged --> the desire to not take part in social interactions

- Food <> sexual <> dress regimes
	- Gratification and deprivation, periodicity

- All competent agents routinely 'keep in touch' (totem) with the grounds of their behavior as an aspect of producing, reproducing behavior. 

#### guilt and shame
- Some argue that guilt is a private anxiety-state, and shame is public one
	- I am much more attuned to the shame axis than I am of guilt
	- But shame depends on feelings of personal insufficiency which may not need to be publicly observed
		- Guilt Axis: discrete acts relating to violation of codes or taboos, cumulative processes in which autonomy is developed by surmounting repression, wrongdoing towards loved or respected others, trust based on absence of betrayal

		- Shame Axis: overall tissue of self-identity, insight into the nature of the narrative of self-identity, inadequate for loved or respected others, trust based on being "known" by the other, where self-revelation does not incur anxieties over exposure
	- "overt" shame and "bypassed" shame
		- overt shame is humiliation
		- bypassed shame is unconsciously experienced anxieties about inadequacies of self

- pride: a biography being justified and unitary

- (the only person who can change yourself is yourself)
- time allows for a life to be lived, not a finite quantity that is running out.
	- the best way to live is not to count.
- stress is correlated with the onset of physical disease
- You have three years left to live. 
	- How would you spend them? 
		- Much in the way I am now
	- Where would you spend them?
		- Here
	- Do you want to work and study?
		- Yes, but on more personal items

- technological skill can be an extension of self, but then it is more archival
-

title: MEMIT
subject: machine-learning
contentHtml:
[[Temporal Knowledge editing in LLMs]]

### scalable knowledge bases
Scalable knowledge bases. The representation of world knowledge is a core problem in artificial
intelligence (Richens, 1956; Minsky, 1974), classically tackled by constructing knowledge bases of real-world concepts. Pioneering hand-curated efforts (Lenat, 1995; Miller, 1995) have been followed by web-powered knowledge graphs (Auer et al., 2007; Bollacker et al., 2007; Suchanek et al., 2007; Havasi et al., 2007; Carlson et al., 2010; Dong et al., 2014; Vrandeˇci´c & Krötzsch, 2014; Bosselut et al., 2019) that extract knowledge from large-scale sources. Structured knowledge bases can be precisely queried, measured, and updated (Davis et al., 1993), but they are limited by sparse coverage of uncatalogued knowledge, such as commonsense facts (Weikum, 2021).



What do we lose when we treat linguistic units as non-atomic?

![[Screenshot 2024-05-25 at 12.45.25 PM.png]]

a) Language models can be viewed as knowledge bases containing memorized tuples (s, r, o), each connecting some subject s to an object o via a relation r, e.g., (s = Michael Jordan, r = plays sport, o = basketball). (b) MEMIT modifies transformer weights to edit memories, e.g., “Michael Jordan now plays the sport baseball,” while (c) maintaining generalization, specificity, and fluency at scales beyond other methods. As Section 5.2.2 details, editing score is the harmonic mean of efficacy, generalization, and specificity metrics.

LLMs are promising because they scale well and are unconstrained by a fixed schema (Safavi & Koutra, 2021). In this paper, we take on the update problem, asking how the implicit knowledge encoded within model parameters can be mass-edited.

memory can be considered edited effectively if the model assigns a higher probability to the statement "Michael Jordan plays the sport of baseball” than to the original prediction (basketball)". Is this really true? What about reworded prompts?

In GPT-2 XL, we found that there is a sequence of critical MLP layers R that mediate factual association recall at the last subject token S (Figure 2). 

MEMIT operates by
 (i) calculating the vector associations we want the critical layers to remember, then
 (ii) storing a portion of the desired memories in each layer l ∈ R.

 (a) first, the early-layer attention modules gather ==subject names== into vector representations at the last subject token S. 
 
 (b) Then MLPs at layers l ∈ R read these encodings and add memories to the residual stream. 
 
 (c) Those hidden states are read by attention to produce the output. (d) MEMIT edits memories by storing vector associations in the critical MLPs.

![[Screenshot 2024-05-25 at 12.02.38 PM.png]]


#### optimal single-layer update
Minimal change semantics!
Look into: linear associative memory, Kohonen, 1972, Anderson 1972.

<s,r,o>: associates a set of input keys $k_i$ ≜ $k^l_i$ (encoding subjects) to corresponding memory values $m_i$ ≜ $m^l_i$ (assumption is these are <r,o> pairs)

![[Screenshot 2024-05-25 at 12.27.44 PM.png]]

$W_0$ is really $W^l_{\text{out}}$, which denotes the output matrix from a single transformer layer MLP.

Can I confirm or integrate any concepts from modal logic into this picture?

- there are certainly instances of necessity and possibility, which should be treated differently than factual statements. 
- We have the intuitive notion of possibility being not necessarily not.


![[Screenshot 2024-05-25 at 12.43.22 PM.png]]

### generalization (across a single figure, not a world state)

To test for generalization, we can rephrase the question: “What is Michael Jordan’s sport? What
sport does he play professionally?” If the modification of G is superficial and overfitted to the
specific memorized prompt, such predictions will fail to recall the edited memory, “baseball.”

### specificity

Conversely, to test for specificity, we can ask about similar subjects for which memories should
not change: “What sport does Kobe Bryant play? What does Magic Johnson play?” These tests
will fail if the updated G indiscriminately regurgitates “baseball” for subjects that were not edited.

### fluency

When making changes to a model, we must also monitor fluency. If the updated model generates disfluent text such as “baseball baseball baseball baseball,” we should count that as a failure.

### editing success metrics

#### efficacy success

New object $o_i$, true real-world object $o_i^c$.

$\mathbb{E}_i[\mathbb{P}_{G}[o_i \vert p(s_i, r_i)] > \mathbb{P}_{G}[o^{c}_{i} \vert p(s_i, r_i)]]$

title: Preproposal
subject: machine-learning
contentHtml:
Transformer Linearity 
### introduction

### related works
Use work primarily from reputable conferences.
### problem statement
Neural language models are known to contain many real-world facts involving a subject, object, and a relation (for example, "Miles Davis plays the trumpet"). Affine linear relational embeddings have been shown to be a good approximation for propositional statements for a subset of contexts, such as bias, commonsense, factual, and linguistic relations (Hernandez et. al. 2024). However, the factors which determine relations for which LRE approximation is successful remain unclear. I aim to determine relationship classes for which LRE is suboptimal and develop alternative approximations which outperform LRE. Thus, I hope to discover new insights as to how relational knowledge in LLMs is encoded, with potential applications in LLM knowledge editing.

My research objective is twofold: (1) determine the relation classes for which LRE is a suboptimal approximator and (2) develop alternative techniques which outperform LRE on both these relation classes, and as a whole. Thus, my work hopes to achieve a better understanding of how transformers model knowledge, and have potential applications in knowledge editing in LLMs.
### approach
Building on the foundation of the LRE approximation as

But experimenting with different approaches, my summer research will model relations with linear approximations for transformer models, utilizing internal transformer subject representations as inputs to predict output tokens. In doing so, I hope to provide evidence for linearly separable subdomains within language.

- factual relations
- commonsense relations
- linguistic relations
- mathematical relations
	- is_divisible_by(#,#)
	- 
- transformer models in languages with with <S,O,R> order
	- For example, Japanese:
		"Miles Davis wa toranpetto o ensō suru"

title: ALL IDEAS
subject: machine-learning
contentHtml:
This is pure research
1. topic: I am studying relationships between internal representations of transformer-based LLMs
2. because I want to find out how LLMs represent relations
3. To further our understanding of probabilistic models of the mind

[[REU general notes]]

Approach this from a problem-solving perspective and be humble about the work that you can actually do

Ideally we want to do something that can be extended or is relevant to Ellie's lab, 
- which is mostly carrying out behavioral psychology type studies on LLMs. W
- working with state space models? 
- Working with RNNs? 
Honestly as long as it could be submitted to a NLP journal it should be fine. So that's not a real constraint. 

Try to pick 2-3 ideas before you decide on the one. It’s a 10-week program, and there is no best topic. If you are good in linguistics along with CS, focus on something that brings in a linguistic issue you find interesting into the project by mixing it up with programming/deep learning/LLMs.

I also prefer projects where you develop an algorithm or an architecture or something you can claim to be yours, not just prompt ChatGPT to respond and analyze results.

![[Screenshot 2024-05-25 at 12.57.26 PM.png]]


### Robust Rewording Prompt Generation
- pros: easy to understand, well defined metrics
- cons: 
	- what problem are you solving? reproducibility?
	- Fantastically Ordered Prompts and Where to Find Them: Overcoming Few-Shot Prompt Order Sensitivity: https://arxiv.org/abs/2104.08786


### structured complex task decomposition (SCTD)
- This is super cool and general, treats task planning as DAG
- Task generation strategies:
	- [[In-context learning]]
	- Multiple sequence: we deduplicate the steps and combine the remaining steps to obtain the final set of steps.
- we first train a separate model that scores the sequences generated by the ICL approach. Then, we generate multiple sequences of steps and select the one with the highest score according to the trained model. (Sample & Filter)
- Soft Prompting: [[Soft Prompting]]: We learn the prompt embedding based on our training data and decide the size of the prompt based on performance on our validation set.

- Our results indicate that LLMs are strong in task decomposition==. For predicting temporal dependencies, however, while they are able to produce a good sequence of steps with the right order of steps, their ability in predicting pairwise temporal dependencies still lags behind.== Future work can find ways of improving the temporal dependency understanding of LLMs, or develop new approaches to improve LLM-based SCTD, e.g. by generating the entire task graph at once (see Sakaguchi et al. (2021); Madaan et al. (2022)) or by breaking the complex tasks into simpler tasks and then solving those simpler tasks (see Zhou et al. (2022a); Kazemi et al. (2023b)).

of a new topic and start a new chunk."

title: contrastive learning
subject: machine-learning
contentHtml:
Contrastive learning is **an approach that focuses on extracting meaningful representations by contrasting positive and negative pairs of instances**. It leverages the assumption that similar instances should be closer in a learned embedding space while dissimilar instances should be farther apart.

title: BERT
subject: machine-learning
contentHtml:
==WRONG==


The two approaches ([[elmo]] and gpt) share the same objective function during pre-training, where unidirectional language models learn general language representations.

Second, rather than standard language modeling, BERT uses two learning objectives: a masked language modeling (MLM) objective and a next sentence prediction (NSP) objective. MLM is essentially a cloze task in which the goal is to predict a word given its left and right context—for instance, The [MASK] purred loudly → cat. The NSP task aims to predict the next sentence given the current one.

subject: machine-learning
contentHtml:
Mistborn Era 2 review

What I really like about Brandon Sanderson is how consistent he is at presenting his ideas, again and again and again until you start to feel their weight. In a certain sense this is a talent for world-building, but in my opinion his worlds are not super lucidly presented or particularly beautiful in their own right, there are plenty of more vividly imaginative and less compelling writers out there. 

His characters are almost boringly realistic individuals, and you get to encounter them again and again from different internal monologues that nearly inevitably have the same message. Redundancy is employed here really well. Compared to other novels at times its comparing a japanese ink painting to paint splatters. There isn't a real plot arc over the course of these particular novels but instead a series of plot points at a very rapid pace. 

The things that I have been reminded of. The most powerful pulling forces on me have always been fantastical ones that dare me to achieve impossible things. 

Reading the first mistborn series now

title: Scope Disambiguation
subject: machine-learning
contentHtml:
---
aliases:
  - Scope Ambiguities in LLMs
---
TODO
- Read Pavlick et al. 2022: [[Semantic Structure in Deep Learning]]
- Read Anderbois et al. 2012 on linear order, grammatical roles, and lexical effects.


![[Screenshot 2024-05-25 at 10.32.23 PM.png]]

"The models with higher accuracy scores, however, see more severe drop-offs in the
control setting, most notably with GPT-4, which sees its accuracy drop to 75% in the control setting." This indicates that the model is truly evaluating the statement.

![[Screenshot 2024-05-25 at 10.41.33 PM.png]]



"Every farmer owns a donkey"
- surface scope: $\forall y [\text{farmer}(y) \rightarrow \exists x \text{donkey(x)} \land \text{owns}(y,x)]$
	- for each farmer, there exists a donkey that the farmer owns.
- equivalent to scope: $\forall y \text{farmer}(y) \rightarrow \exists x \text{donkey(x)} \land \text{owns}(y,x)$
	- For each farmer, there exists a donkey that the farmer owns.
	- but this is ill-formed (the issue is that $y$ is not bound in the consequent)

- inverse scope: $\exists x [\text{donkey}(x) \land \forall y \text{farmer}(y) \rightarrow \text{owns}(y,x)$.
	- every farmer owns the same donkey.
	- harder to access 

 - Built upon the quantifier scope dataset presented by AnderBois
	- 1700 sentences and phrases from LSAT
	- filtered for interacting quantifiers, and human evaluated to 75% golden cutoff
- 41 had inverse scope reading, 112 had surface scope


### part 2: are LLMs sensitive to the existence of scope ambiguity?

We therefore construct a small-scale dataset, consisting of 38 manually handcrafted datapoints, where each datapoint includes a scope ambiguous sentence (S), a matching non scope-ambiguous control sentence (Sc), and two follow-up phrases (F1 and F2), yielding a total of 152 sentence-continuation pairs.

title: Quantum Interference Model for Semantic Biases of Glosses in Word Sense Disambiguation
subject: machine-learning
contentHtml:
we try to use multiple representations to characterize a word sense to simultaneously reveal the distribution of the representations, and then refer to the distribution information to improve the accuracy of word sense recognition. To this end, we adopt the superposition state (Nielsen and Chuang 2002) in quantum mechanics to formalize representations from different glosses of the same word sense, and then reveal their distribution.


[[GlossBERT]]

title: RoBERTa
subject: machine-learning
contentHtml:
---
aliases:
  - BERT
  - Masked Language Modeling
---
Used 2.2 trillion tokens to train on. 

Basically BERT (used 137 billion tokens) with better training.

We present a replication study of BERT pretraining (Devlin et al., 2019) that carefully measures the impact of many key hyperparameters and training data size. We find that BERT was significantly undertrained, an;d can match or exceed the performance of every model published after it. Our best model achieves state-of-the-art results on GLUE, RACE and SQuAD.

(1) training the model longer, with bigger batches, over more data; (2) removing the next sentence prediction objective; (3) training on longer sequences; and (4) dynamically changing the masking pattern applied to the training data.  

We also collect a large new dataset (CC-NEWS) of comparable size to other privately used datasets, to better control for training set size effects. When controlling for training data, our improved training procedure improves upon the published BERT results on both GLUE and SQuAD. When trained for longer over additional data, our model achieves a score of 88.5 on the public GLUE leaderboard, matching the 88.4 reported by Yang et al. (2019). Our model establishes a new state-of-the-art on 4/9 of the GLUE tasks: MNLI, QNLI, RTE and STS-B. We also match state-of-the-art results on SQuAD and RACE. Overall, we re-establish that BERT’s masked language model training objective is competitive with other recently proposed training objectives such as perturbed autoregressive language modeling.

title: t5
subject: machine-learning
contentHtml:
---
aliases:
  - T5
  - text-to-text transformer
---
Uses [[SentencePiece]] to encode text as WordPiece tokens. Uses a vocabulary of 32,000 tokens. 

Tested in  [[Uncontrolled Lexical Exposure Leads to Overestimation of Compositional Generalization in Pretrained Models]] for overgeneralization.

It is a transformer architecture which treats all language tasks (Coreference CoQA, NLI, summarization, translation etc.) as "text-to-text"

- This is also masked span prediction like [[RoBERTa|BERT]].

![[Screenshot 2024-05-25 at 1.37.32 PM.png]]

- This is not seq2seq. A seq2seq model is [[RoBERTa]]. 
-  effectively recasts the STS-B regression problem as a 21-class classification problem.
- [[winograd]]

"The city councilmen refused the demonstrators a permit because \*they\* feared violence.”

the model would be trained to predict the target text 

“The city councilmen”

- Note that the choice of text prefix used for a given task is essentially a hyperparameter; we found that changing the exact wording of the prefix had limited impact and so did not perform extensive experiments into different prefix choice.

### attention mask patterns

"causal" is used by transformers

![[Screenshot 2024-05-25 at 1.40.54 PM.png]]

> A fundamental and frequently cited drawback of using a language model in the text-
to-text setting is that causal masking forces the model’s representation of the ith entry of
the input sequence to only depend on the entries up until i.


>In the English to German translation example mentioned above,
fully-visible masking would be applied to the prefix “translate English to German: That is
good. target:” and causal masking would be used during training for predicting the target
“Das ist gut.” 
- casual masking is intuitive.
- casual masking reduces training time?

> the main difference between a prefix LM and the BERT architecture is
that the classifier is simply integrated into the output layer of the Transformer decoder in
the prefix LM.

For models that ingest a prefix before making predictions (the encoder-decoder model and prefix LM), we sample a span of text from our unlabeled data set and choose a random point to split it into prefix and target portions. For the standard language model, we train the model to predict the entire span from beginning to end.

title: Temporal Knowledge editing in LLMs
subject: machine-learning
contentHtml:
We firstly query the language model with the cloze statement of current knowledge to extract the knowledge under model time, and then edit the model using both of them with timestamps, and also use the model to make knowledge time predictions so that the model can reinforce both historical and new knowledge, and improve the awareness of time of knowledge.


Since the knowledge inside a model becomes progressively outdated, knowledge editing (KE), a convenient way to edit the knowledge, has received increasing attention and some methods have been proposed. Zhu et al. (2020) propose the constrained finetuning approach on modified facts to solve the problem. Sotoudeh and Thakur (2019) utilize symbolic representations and gen- eralized RELU networks. (Hahnloser et al. 2000) to cor- rect model with small patches. Cao, Aziz, and Titov (2021) introduce the method which corrects knowledge and im- proves predictions using a hyper-network for targeted modifications. Dai et al. (2022) explore knowledge neurons in models and attempt to leverage them to edit specific factual knowledge. ROME (Meng et al. 2022a) and MEMIT (Meng et al. 2022b) take the approach of locating and then edit- ing. Mitchell et al. (2022a) a collection of small auxiliary editing networks that use a single desired input-output pair to make fast, local edits. In addition, MEMIT-CSK (Gupta et al. 2023) is designed to edit commonsense knowledge.

title: transformer review
subject: machine-learning
contentHtml:
At each layer of a transformer, to compute the representation of a word i we combine information from the representation of i at the previous layer with information from the representations of the neighboring words. The goal is to produce a contextualized representation for each word at each position.

As with the overall transformer, a self-attention layer maps input sequences (x1, ..., xn) to output sequences of the same length (a1, ..., an) (words). When processing each item in the input, the model has access to all of the inputs up to and including the one under consideration, but no access to information about inputs beyond the current one. 

We have K, Q,  V for each input vector. The score between a current focus of attention, xi, and
an element in the preceding context, x j, consists of a dot product between its query
vector qi and the preceding element’s key vectors k j (how much attention to pay to each key?). We divide the scores by d_k and then softmax, to get weights for each original value vector. The sum is the output of self-attention, and represents the influence of *context* on the input vector.


![[Screenshot 2024-05-21 at 8.36.44 PM.png]]



### encoder-decoder

With just the decoder, we are doing next-word prediction. At each output timestep, a token is sampled from the model’s predicted distribution and the sample is fed back into the model to produce a prediction for the next output timestep, and so on. A Transformer decoder (without an encoder) can be used as a language model (LM). 

Radford et al. 2019: Language models are unsupervised multitask learners

seeing examples of word usage in the CCCC allow large transformer models to fake compositional generalization with the said word in different syntactic roles.

This was tested for by substituting items in the lexicon with novel character sequences, feeding the model the COGS data, and testing for generalizability.

TODO: research COGS benchmark
[[lexical generalization]]
- ( Weißenhorn et al. 2022) "lexical generalization is essentially a solved problem for seq2seq models"


It could be memorization all the way down:

> Imagine that the famous wug test (Berko, 1958) was in the form of This is a slug. Now there are two of them. There are two __., with the real word slug in place of wug. Even if a subject produces the expected ending /z/, this result cannot serve as evidence for the existence of an abstract pluralization rule, because prior observations of slugs could have been retrieved from memory. The same analogy applies to using real words as context-controlled lexical items in compositional generalization benchmarks when pretrained models are being tested.


> Using the COGS benchmark of Kim and Linzen (2020), wetest two modified evaluation setups that control for this issue: (1) substituting context-controlled lexical items with novel character sequences, and (2) substituting them with special tokens represented by novel embed-dings. We find that both of these setups lead to lower generalization performance in T5(Raffel et al., 2020), suggesting that previously reported results have been overestimated due to uncontrolled lexical exposure during 

> Our results support the conclusion that the previously reported generalization performance of pretrained models has been substantially overestimated, and furthermore highlight the surprising sensitivity of the models’ generalization behavior
to the choice of the type of the context-controlled lexical items.

title: RST-LoRA
subject: machine-learning
contentHtml:
Integrates semantic structure into transformer architecture as text embedding. Achieves SoTA results on domain specific summarization tasks.


![[Screenshot 2024-05-24 at 5.49.38 PM.png]]

-the integration of rhetorical structure theory (RST)
into parameter-efficient fine-tuning strategies
for long document summarization remains un-
explored. 

[[LoRA]]

Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT)
PEFT strategies face challenges in distinguishing latent
text relations and determining the importance level
of different sentences during fine-tuning. This issue
arises because such distinctions are not a primary
focus in PEFT’s learning process and are not explicitly represented in the input data. However, this is essential for the task of long document summarization since generating a good summary often requires natural language generation (NLG) models to have the ability to discern salient information
within the text and comprehend the intricate interrelations among different text components.

RST delineates a set of coherence relations between text
segments, known as Elementary Discourse Units
(EDUs), at the document level (e.g., one segment
might provide clarification for another, or con-
versely, two segments could present contrasting
viewpoints)

Kikuchi et al. (2014) boosted the summarization performance of the RNN-based model by constructing RST trees, where satellite EDUs were pruned to retain only the nucleus EDUs, thus focusing on the document’s key content. 

A limitation observed with LoRA and other PEFT
methods during the fine-tuning phase is that, while
their primary function is to act as a low-rank approximation of the weight matrices in LLMs, they do not adequately capture textual context relations.

### four RST matrix distributins:
A binary, label-agnostic representation collapsing probabilities into a simple 1-or-0 regarding discourse connections

an extension of the binary distribution that includes relation labels, enriching the binary decisions with relational types

A probabilistic representation that omits labels, focusing instead on the probabilities to express uncertainty in discourse connections.

The most granular representation, retain-
ing both types of discourse relations and their
probabilistic weights for a full-fledged represen-
tation of discourse nuances.

title: BERTScore
subject: machine-learning
contentHtml:
Analogously to common metrics, BERTScore computes a similarity score for each token in the candidate sentence with each token in the reference sentence. However, instead of exact matches, we compute token similarity using contextual embeddings.

Uses contextualized word embeddings to calculate similarity, making it an improvement to systems like [[BLEU]] which are string similarity measures.

title: collaborative filtering
subject: machine-learning
contentHtml: collaborative filtering is a method of making automatic predictions (filtering) about the interests of a user by collecting preferences or taste information from many users (collaborating). The underlying assumption of the collaborative filtering approach is that if a person _A_ has the same opinion as a person _B_ on an issue, A is more likely to have B's opinion on a different issue than that of a randomly chosen person.

In a term-document matrix, each row represents a word in the vocabulary and each column represents a document from some collection of documents. This is really just [[tf-idf]] | | As You Like It
| Twelfth Night | Julius Caesar | Henry V | | | ------ | ------------------- | ------------- | ------------- | ------- | --- | | battle | 1 | 0 | 7 | 13 | | | good | 114 | | | | | | fool | 36 | | | | | As You like it: [1, 114, 36] The vectors for the comedies As You Like It [1,114,36,20] and Twelfth Night [0,80,58,15] look a lot more like each other (more fools and wit than battles) than they look like Julius Caesar [7,62,1,2] or Henry V [13,89,4,3]. Doing IR will also require efficient ways to store and manipulate these vectors by making use of the convenient fact that these vectors are sparse, i.e., mostly zeros. We also have ### word semantics We can view the above table as a list of words with the corresponding documents giving the semantic intepretation. ### term-term matrix term-term matrix, also called the word-word matrix or the term-context matrix, in which the columns are labeled by words rather than documents. This matrix is thus of dimensionality |V |×|V | and each cell records the number of times the row (target) word and the column (context) word co-occur in some context in some training corpus.

title: tf-idf
subject: machine-learning
contentHtml:
"embedding" method
In the popular tf-idf scheme (Salton and McGill, 1983), a basic vocabulary of “words” or “terms” is chosen, and, for each document in the corpus, a count is formed of the number of occurrences of each word. After suitable normalization, this term frequency count is
compared to an inverse document frequency count, which measures the number of occurrences of a word in the entire corpus (generally on a log scale, and again suitably normalized). The end result is a term-by-document matrix X whose columns contain the tf-idf values for each of the documents in the corpus. Thus the tf-idf scheme reduces documents of arbitrary length to fixed-length lists of numbers.

title: Eastview Guatemala 2018 census
subject: nlp
contentHtml: Official population and housing data for Guatemala from the 2018 census. This census is provided at the Municipio administrative level (ADM2) and includes 340 polygons with 294 attribute variables. Attribute descriptions retain source-language names and unique identifier codes. All variable names were translated from source language (Spanish). Field name prefixes, originally written with coded titles, were renamed to create unique names across the geodatabase (e.g. Original variable of “Total de personas” was updated to D001 and given the alias “Total Population [Total de personas]”, the letter D was given as it relates to the Demographic theme table. D = Demographic, E = Economic, H = Housing and S = Social). All variables from the original data were reviewed for completeness and organized into their relevant themes of Demographic, Economic, Housing and Social. The geodatabase was built to hold these specific tables with all geographic names and codes represented as text fields and census data represented as doubles or long integers. Automated tools were run to apply aliases in the format “English translation field description [Source language field description]”. Vector data was analyzed for accuracy and compared with national boundaries.

title: sem_nl
subject: nlp
contentHtml:
### Semantics of Natural Language 
#### Donald Davidson, Gilbert Harman

The first essay in the collection is by Charles J. Fillmore, over subjects, speakers and roles.

- The very first thing we need to do in sentence analysis is divide it: into the **subject** and the **predicate**.
- The **subject** is the person or thing about whom / which a statement is made or question asked.
- The **predictate** is the statement or the question asked.
- In Chomsky's formal grammars, these parts are the nounphrase (NP) and verbphrase (VP).

Similarly, to Chomsky there is a real, deep, structure and a surface structure. The following example:

1a. Pianists play piano
1b. Pianos are played by pianists

Has the same deep structure, but a different NP as subject. There's an argument that 1a is analytic (breakdown, giving definition, saying nothing new) while 1b is synthetic (combining, making statement, saying something new). The first feels more redundant than the second.

Author believes the semantic relevance of the subject/predicate distinction should be sought only in the deep structure.
#### subject semantics
The semantic role of the subject is not always the same. The boy in each of these:

"The boy slapped the girl". 
"The boy fell down".
"The boy received a blow". 
"The boy has blue eyes". 

Appears not to have a common notional property of "subjectness" which semantic descriptions can exploit. If we would like to think the **subject** defined in the configurational way (as above) must be relevant to the semantics of the sentence, we have to think some more.

First we note something. There are verbs which have both intransitive and transitive uses, and show the same semantic role regarding the S and DO respectively. For example: rotate, break:

2a. The cylinder (S) rotated
2b. Fred rotated the cylinder (DO).

3a. The lens broke.
3b. Fred broke the lens.

One popular proposal is that the transitive sentences contain the intransitive sentences embedded to CAUSE.

2c. Fred cause (the cylinder rotate)
3c. Fred cause (the lens break)

The appearance of underlying intransitive verbs in transitive-verb positions is a "matter of surface detail".
In effect, we can think of transitives as S cause (verb).

4a. Peter put the beer in the icebox.
4b. Peter cause (beer be (in icebox)). 

This makes it possible to modify the sentence like:

4c. Peter cause (beer be (in icebox)) (for three hours) ==  Peter cause (beer be (in icebox) for three hours)

#### direct object semantics
The semantic role of the direct object is not always the same. 

"I smashed the pumpkin"
"I grew the pumpkin"
"I like the pumpkin"


There are illocutionary statements:

Your threats don't bother me 

To deal with illocutionary force, we must deal with the participants: person deixis (speaker and addressee), place deixis, time deixis, discourse deixis (anaphora?), honorific systems. 

> In anaphora, the referent is an element of the current discourse itself, whereas in deixis, the referent is outside the discourse in its spatiotemporal surroundings
preview: ### Semantics of Natural Language #### Donald Davidson, Gilbert Harman

title: LexSem_final
subject: linguistics
contentHtml:
1. Terry says:

> "Oh I disagree. I realize it was warm here this past winter, but not every city had a
warm winter last year."

Terry uses "not every city had a warm winter last year" ($\lnot \forall x$ s.t. $P(x)$) to imply that "there exist cities that did not have a warm winter last year" ($\exists$ x s.t. $\lnot P(x)$). Rhetorically, the $\lnot \forall P$ statement Terry employs is stronger than $\exists \lnot P$ because it highlights the negative nature of the statement.
    
When Terry says "not every city had a warm winter last year", he is comparing the previous year's winter in their city (indexed by "here") to the respective previous year's winters in the set of all cities. It is possible that Terry does not consider them to be within a city, but the implication is that they are.

Warm is a scalar adjective. Terry is using the positive form of warm, understood to be warm for that individual city's winter. Clearly, a warm winter in Juneau is not the same warm winter in Orlando. Akin to "all of my children are tall (for their age)", this should be interpreted "not every city had a warm winter (for their context)". 

A positive adjective requires a comparison class $C$ and a standard $d_C$. Roughly, the context for a city's last year's winter warmness is the climate, but strictly speaking (and especially in context of climate change) the context is the previous winters in that city. [[POS warm]] describes the set

$$\{w \mid w_{\text{warmness}} > d_{C}\}$$


Here, $w$ is a winter and $w_{\text{warmness}}$ is it's degree of warmness. $C$ is the set of all past winters for that city, and $d_{C}$ is a vague standard of warmness determined by the warmnesses of all winters in $C$, $\{c \in C: c_\text{warmness} \}$. $d_C$ here is dependent on the speaker: perhaps Terry believes it is the average, five degrees above the average, or even (somewhat logically unsound) a constant degree ("It's not really a warm winter unless its 70F or above").

We have $F(x)$ as the Boolean function of a city having a warm winter last year. 

If $x_w$ is last year's winter for the city $x$, we have

$$F(x) = x_w \in \{w \mid w_{\text{warmness}} > d_{C}\}$$

The indexical "here" references the actual location of a speaker, in this case Terry. Assuming the likely interpretation that "here" references a city $x_0$, the clause "(I realize) it was warm here this past winter" says:
$$F(x_0)$$

The second clause is more complicated. 

The clause "not every city had a warm winter last year" says that for the set of all cities $X$:
$$ \lnot (\forall x \in X: F(x))$$

Thus, all together the statement is saying

$$ F(x_0) \land \lnot (\forall x \in X: x_w \in \{w \mid w_{\text{warmness}} > d_{C}\})$$

Note that $w_x$, $C$, and $d_{C}$ are fixed for individual $x$.
Also note that the vagueness in the statement relating to warmness only applies to the warmness of individual cities previous winters (whether $F(x)$ is true or false) and not to the quantifier "not every". Any vagueness that could be assigned to the broader clause, "not every city had a warm winter last year", has to do with the size of $X$, the set of all cities.

1.

> Lee: Computer science is a lot more interesting than linguistics.

> Sandy: Oh - I disagree. Linguistics is a lot more interesting than CS!

Here, interesting is a predicate of personal taste. As per Lasersohn, statements are true relative to a world, time, speech context, and a judge. Thus, when Lee says that computer science is more interesting, it is true with Lee as judge, and when Sandy says that linguistics is more interesting, it is true with Sandy as jduge.

2.
> Lee: Marvin is tall.

> Sandy: No - I disagree - he isn't tall.

There are two different ways that they can both be disagreeing and both be right. They may have different comparison classes for tall, or they may have different standards within that comparison class. 

In the first case, Lee might be comparing against all men, but Sandy is comparing against all men who are Marvin's weight. Sandy might say:

> No -- I disagree -- he isn't tall. For his weight, five feet eleven isn't tall.

In the second case, Lee and Sandy may be comparing against the same class, men who are Marvin's weight, but have different standards for tallness within that class. Sandy might say:

> No -- I disagree -- he isn't tall. Five feet eleven doesn't count as tall, even for his weight.


3.
> Lee: Toby is an elephant.

> Sandy: Wait, Toby is a reptile!

Based on our assumptions about the world, we would say that they cannot both be right, because being a *mammal* is a definitional property entailed by being an *elephant*. Because we consider mammals and reptiles to (generally) be exclusive classes, this meanst that Toby cannot be a reptile and an elephant at the same time.

While it is possible to discover that elephants are actually reptiles, we think of *elephant* as a natural kind term that refer to things that are fundamentally elephant. Based on our knowledge of the natural world, we believe individuals who are fundamentally elephant are not reptiles.

1. two words that reference other worlds

Two words that reference other worlds include *lucky* and *suprised*. 

*Lucky* says that according to the speaker, world, and time, a favorable event has occurred that is improbable: there are many possible worlds in which it did not occur. "I got lucky winning the lottery" requires reference to other worlds where I had not won the lottery.

*Surprised* says that according to the speaker, world and time, an event has occurred that is improbable: there are many possible worlds in which it did not occur. As above, "I was surprised you won the lottery" requires reference to other worlds where the event did not occur.


Two words that reference times include *undone* and *gone*. 

*undone* requires something to have been done at a previous time, as you cannot undo something that has not been done. "Her hair was undone" implies that at some previous time, her hair had been done. 

When used as a standalone adjective, *gone* means an absence where there something was previously present: "the milk is all gone" implies that at some previous time, the milk was present. 

*gone* also functions as a past participle verb, which almost by definition requires reference to other times in the past. "They've all gone home" says that at some previous time, the individuals indicated went home. 




We find it not surprising because English has Subject Verb Object word order. By Dowty's hypothesis about thematic roles, we have subjects as participants with more "agent-like" properties, and objects as participants with less.

Lee has more agent-like properties. Lee causes the event, and is sentient and volitional. Thus, Lee is the subject.

The books have more patient-like properties. They undergo a change of state (the manner of the action implies they are aligned square against the shelf) and a change of location (moved to a bookshelf). Thus, the books is the object.

If we had a verb like smelf which takes in the patient-like word first and the agent-like word second, it would make sense in an Object Verb Subject language. However, English does not follow this syntax, which is why *smelf* is surprising.

title: final
subject: linguistics
contentHtml:
### Are moral properties natural properties?

We all have our own meanings for words. This makes philosophy accessible, but sometimes infuriating. It is why questions like "are moral properties natural properties?" make sense to me and to someone who has spent their whole life writing about these things. With this disclaimer out of the way, by my definitions of *moral* and *natural*, I believe that they are not identical, but reflective of one another. As a result of this, I believe that moral properties exist.

To me, one of the strongest arguments for them not being the same draws from mathematics. There are formal notions of identity that are weaker than equality. These include isomorphism, equivalence, and others. So the properties can also be viewed in the same way: not equal, but reflective. I believe they are different enough to avoid reductionism of the moral to natural properties. However, I also believe that ethics and science are similar enough to avoid skepticism about their existence of moral knowledge.

### Some Differences

We will begin by looking at some of their differences. R.M.Hare wrote: 
"Let us take that characteristic of “good” which has been called its supervenience. Suppose that we say ‘St. Francis was a good man.’ It is logically impossible to say this and to maintain at the same time that there might have been another man placed exactly in the same circumstances as St. Francis, and who behaved in exactly the same way, but who differed from St. Francis in this respect only, that he was not a good man."

But this does not in fact, follow logically from St. Francis being a good man. In my view, Hare's use of "logically" is purely for rhetorical effect. Hume used the is-ought gap to defend his objective nature of reality (a stark one devoid of *oughts*), but it can also work here. If there does exist another man who shares every natural property with St. Francis, we cannot derive any *ought* from this. We know that St. Francis himself is good; yet we cannot talk about whether his doppleganger is good, because we don't know. Clearly, R.M.Hare's challenge does not pose a problem if the moral and natural are not the same, and the moral does not supervene on the natural.

A similar line of reasoning makes the existence of Moral Twin Earths doubtful. We are asked to imagine an Earth that is exactly like ours in natural properties, but with everyone on the planet as utilitarian, committed to making very different decisions than ours. To me, imagining such a world is inconceivable. We cannot know, with certainty, any moral properties based solely on natural properties. If a world full of utilitarians did exist, I believe that it would be very different than ours.

One other way moral and natural properties are very different is as follows. If you point to a pond and say "this is H2O", what you are claiming is fundamentally different than when you point to teenagers setting fire to a cat and saying "this is wrong". In the first one, you may say things like "this is H2O" even if that is all you know about it. As a seven-year-old, I was very prone to doing these things. In contrast, you cannot say "this is bad" without believing you have the whole picture. The assertion of scientific fact does not require you to be understand why believe you are right, while ethics does. 

A more succinct illustration of my point is as follows. In science, you can be technically correct all the time: like I was as a seven-year-old. In ethics, such a thing is impossible to imagine. 

Observe that the following statement makes sense:
Technically, the largest desert in the world is Antarctica. 

But that this one doesn't:
Technically, polygamy is wrong.

If I were to hear the second statement, I would immediately assume the statement to be in the context of a specific moral system or set of rules, such as Christianity. I would never see the second statement as an expression of genuine belief. It seems to me that the role of *technically* is to distinguish between the *actual* (technical) and the *intended* meanings of a statement. For example, it is *technically* the case that Antarctica receives little rainfall and meets qualifications for a desert. However, the *intended* meaning of "the world's largest desert", that people would find suitable, is something like the Sahara. 

Because you cannot qualify a moral statement with *technically*, it follows that the actual meaning of moral terms like "right" and "good" is exactly the intended one. In, this way, it seems clear to me that moral properties cannot be reduced to natural ones. 

### Some Similarities

Now that I have argued why moral and natural properties are different and non-reductive, we will look at their similarities. Finding these similarities will help defend against arguments by those who do not believe in their existence. 

Many people are skeptical about the existence of moral properties as moral knowledge. Following Hume, my basic point of view is that if you have this skepticism, it should extend not only to moral knowledge, but to all of it. The field of ethics is often compared, aspirationally, to the natural sciences. Yet to me it seems very reasonable to say there are no facts in science. We may reject the hypothesis that there is a correlation between two things, or we may fail to reject it, but we can never accept it. When we deal with the real world, as ethics and natural science clearly do so, we can never fully escape uncertainty.

One experiment which is said to cast doubt on moral knowledge involves comparing two observations. One is a physicist observing a proton in a cloud chamber; the other is a woman observing teenagers setting a cat on fire. In the first, it seems that scientific observation of the physicist is both the cause and effect of their theory about the physical world; the direction of causation goes both ways. in the second, the moral observation of the woman is solely effect of her theory about the moral world: only theory influences observation. If observations cannot change moral judgements, how can moral knowledge exist?

I believe that we are committing a fallacy when we compare the two. To be more precise, we are comparing a scientific observation against a moral aplication, which are not the same at all. The analogue should not be between the skeptical, intrepid scientist testing their hypothesis against their evidence and the woman blindly saying "this is wrong". It should be between the skeptical, intrepid scientist testing their hypothesis and the skeptical, intrepid woman testing their own moral hypothesis.

What might such a moral hypothesis and revision look like? After seeing the cat set on fire, the woman might believe that minding her own business is right; until her own beloved pet is set on fire the next day. She wonders if she should be more assertive in her own neighborhood; having made such a resolution, she stops a robbery the next week.

This series of events certainly seems scientific, with a hypothesis, a test, and revision. However, one objection might be that this kind of hypothesis seems removed from the essence of moral philosophy. The most moral lesson she may have learned is something like "You ought to stop others from endangering you and your community". Surely in the beginning, she still believed that the teenagers did something wrong. Then, is it the case that her moral philosophy did not change at all?

To me, the important thing is that she changed her reaction and would in fact act differently the next time. As she became more aware of her ability to change the outcome of the world, her knowledge of moral properties with respect to herself changed accordingly.

Another possible challenge to the existence of moral properties comes from Mackie, who says: "If there were objective values, then they would be entities or qualities or relations of a very strange sort, utterly different from anything else in the universe". Furthermore, Mackie finds it supremely unsatisfying that the only way in which we may find objects to have moral properties through a "special kind of intuition". 

The notion of objective values is indeed very strange. By their nature, they seem not to exist in the real world. Yet, science does not seem to deal with objective things either, but instead very good approximations of them. George Box says, "all models are wrong, but some of them are useful". Neither scientific or moral theory makes claims towards universal truth. Furthermore, in my view neither really deals primarily with the abstract primitives that *do seem objective*. In the case of physics, these are numbers, in the case of ethics, these are right and wrong, or good and bad.

It's clear that when Mackie's argument is applied to the domain of numbers, these questions become absurd. Numbers are utterly different from anything else in the universe. We are able to count, we have a "special kind of intuition" about what two or three things are, but there is nothing strange about this. There is nothing about the existence of numbers that is crying out to be explained.

We can say that numbers are integral to physics; but we cannot say that it is what physics is *about*. In the same way, right and wrong are integral to ethics, but are not what ethics is *about*. It is very weird for someone to praise "goodness" or "rightness": "I love being good! Being good is the best!" These statements feel vacuous and empty because moral properties are abstract notions that gain meaning only when they have been assigned.

title: desire_reason
subject: linguistics
contentHtml:
### Belief and Desire
Belief in p is *eliminated* in the case of not-p.
Desire in p makes one *revise* not-p to p.

### Motivational skepticism: *reason cannot produce desire*.

But it can. For example, we can rationally convince ourselves to get a shot. 

### Reasoning and desire

Jean wants to avoid the flu.
She believes that if she gets a shot, she will avoid the flu.
But she does not desire to get the shot (or does she?)

Humeans are committed to the motivational power of practical reason. They believe that practical reason can convince people to get shots. 

Well maybe: actually, its not desire.
Well then what is it?

### So reason can produce desire.

Even if Williams is right (there are no external reasons), whether we ever have reasons that aren't grounded in S depends on the context of practical reason. 

Are there any genuine categorical imperatives?

There are doubts. It seems like when you suggest a rule, I can meaningfully ask, "Okay, I understand your rule, but what reason do I have to follow it?"

(???) Your answer usually takes the form, "Here's something you care about. If you follow the rule, it will be helped in some way."

### Lewis Caroll
Logic doesn't *really* work, unless you accept a kind of composition by identity. 

A. If P, then Q.
B. If O, then P.
Z. If O, then Q.
C. If A and B, then Z.

Reasoning is the process of getting new beliefs from old beliefs. The state of mind in accepting a rule is not the same as that when you believe a premise.

Here, the tortoise doesn't want to use the rule.

Accepting and following a M/E rule is not having a desire!
For Humeans, following means/ends rules are Categorical Imperatives! (We are not really humeans)

Analytic: rigid designator
Synthetic: not rigidly designating

If A, then B. A. So B.

Reason is desire + belief

### You CANNOT think in any other way!!

Desire: to A. 

Believe: by Bing, I will A.

THEN YOU MUST Desire: to B.

### The source of all value

We value happiness as an END. 

1. I value the ends I rationally set myself, and take myself to have reason to pursue them. (? the kind of value you have is selfish, why would you want to help other's with their valuing ?)
   
2. But I recognize that their value is only condiitonal: if I did not set them as my ends, I would have no reason to pursue them. 

3. I have a *worth-bestowing* status.

4. I must see myself as having an unconditional, int`rinsic value (?) -- as being an end in myself and the condition of the value of my chosen ends -- in virtue of my capacity to bestow worth on my ends by rationally choosing them.

5. I must similarly accord any other rational being the same unconditional value I accord myself (??)

6. So I should act in a manner that respects this.

title: value
subject: linguistics
contentHtml:
We have a cubic press.

This cubic press has value only because of its ability to produce... ... which makes someone happy.
Therefore, the diamonds must have value.

We have penicillin. 

This penicillin has value only because of its ability to prevent ... ... which makes someone happy.
Therefore, the bacterial infections must have value!

We have ends.
These ends have value only because of their ability ... ... which makes someone happy (?)

??? Things are valuable only because we value them.
This could only be so if *we* have value.

Imagine a person who always says they value the thing for its own sake. 
There's just something arbitrary and dogmatic about valuing many such unrelated,
unsystematic, contingently-chosen ends, without some more fundamental 
explanation for why they matter.

title: what_is_a_non_natural_property
subject: linguistics
contentHtml:
Moore: *Good* is a simple, non-natural property. 
The Open Question Argument (Then is N good? is a reasonable question for all N) shows that good must be "simple". But it is non-natural. Our reasoning might be as follows. Can we imagine "good" as existing by itself in time, and not merely as a property of some natural object? Moore believes that we cannot. For the majority of properties of objects (roundess, redness, squareness) can be imagined independent of the existence of those objects. However, good cannot be; and so it is non-natural property.

These characteristics Moore considers here as natural seem much more like non-natural characteristics, not natural ones. Brownness is certainly not the collection of brown objects (although from a linguistic perspective it might be treated as such).

Moore cannot articulate why he believes good is non-natural. He insists in Principia Ethica that two and four do not *exist* in the saem way that natural things do, but that "two and two are four" means something. This is because he built his lectures around the idea that "being" and "existence" were two separate conditions, which he later retracted.

A weaker and stronger "follows"?
- "Good", "Right"? 

To know the meaning of a sentence is to know what it means to make it true or false. That's not true, it's not even false!

Positivists say:

Mathematics is all tautologies (or contradictions)
There is one god.  (this doesn't mean anything)
Statements of value or not analytic, so they aren't tautologies. They are not in the literal sense significatn, but expressions of emotions which can neither be true or false. A similar thing can be applied to aesthetics.

Your taking the candy from the child was wrong 
--> You toook the canddy from the childd 😒.
