title: proofwriting_final
subject: nlp
contentHtml:
# Space Logic Lemmas

For my proofwriting final, I will introduce some basic proof structures in an intuitive format that you've probably seen before: that of Venn Diagrams! 

While some of the content here is seen in other papers as an supplement for a more advanced theory, I think these results are interesting in their own right. Furthermore, they are self-contained, making them perfect for analysis. Form follows function.


#### Everything*
A blank diagram doesn't actually tell us anything, but it does direct us to something. If it isn't labeled, we can usually assume that it contains everything of interest. It is sometimes called the universal set $U$. In probability theory, it might be the sample space, $\Omega$. In the context of points and lines, it is $\mathbb{R}^2$, in the context of individuals it is everyone, in the context of possible worlds it is every possible world, and so on.

#### A
We draw the set A as a circle. I've tried other approaches but haven't found anything that works as well; for both inclusions and intersections, circles are the most suggestive of a set. Squiggles look like you're trying to call attention to them, triangles, squares, and hexagons fail miserably at suggesting intersection, etc.

#### A intersect B 

Our classic example is set intersection. Given sets $A$ and $B$, their intersection $A \cap B$ is all of the elements in both set $A$ and set $B$. This is depicted as the overlapping area between $A$ and $B$.

#### Direct Proof: A implies B
Here, we have the first way to express a statement, the direct proof $A \rightarrow B$. There is a difference between $\cap$ and $\rightarrow$. The first is a description *within* a world, while the second is a relation *about* a world. In every world we have $A$, we have $B$. $B$ encircles $A$.

#### Not A
Here we have the opposite of $A$, denoted $\sim A$ or $\neg A$.

#### Not B
Here we have the opposite of $B$, denoted $\sim B$ or $\neg B$.

#### Contrapositive: Not B implies not A
Here is an intuitive explanation for the contrapositive proof, ${\sim} B \rightarrow {\sim}A$. If we have the implication $A \rightarrow B$, or B encircling A, it is inevitable that we will also have ${\sim} B \rightarrow {\sim} A$. 

As we can see in the picture, ${\sim}A$ encircles ${\sim}B$, because $B$ encircles $A$. (this is also like downward entailment in semantics).

#### A and B
$\land$ is what we get when we apply $\rightarrow$; like $\cap$, it is a statement in a world. That is, when we apply $A \rightarrow B$, we obtain a world with a circle $A$ and a bigger circle $B$ around it. 


#### A and not B
Armed with our new tool, we investigate a different approach from our direct proof. Assume that $A \rightarrow B$. Then we have $A \land {\sim}B$, a small circle and a bigger hole. Doesn't it look hard to be in both? (it's impossible!)

#### C and not C
What $C \cap {\sim}C$ says is, "be both inside and outside the circle". As we can see, this is impossible. it's quite like $A \cap {\sim}B$, but with A relaxed, widened a bit... exactly to the size of B.

#### Proof by Contradiction: A and not B implies C and not C

Like the contrapositive, proof by contradiction follows the direct proof. As we can see, if we have the direct implication $A \rightarrow B$, we will also have $A \cap {\sim}B \rightarrow C \cap {\sim} C$. That is, if B encircles A, then our original world will be a small circle $A$ and a bigger hole ${\sim}B$. These two pieces then imply $C \land {\sim}C$.


#### A if and only if B
We can say A $\leftrightarrow B$ to mean $A \rightarrow B$ and $B \rightarrow A$ at the same time. If we have both $A \rightarrow B$ and $B \rightarrow A$, we must have B encircling A and A encircling B. This is impossible, unless A and B are the same circle! This is same rationale for proving equality by double set inclusion.

#### Equivalence: A <> B <> C
If we have multiple statements we need to prove equivalence for, we need only to prove $A \rightarrow B$, $B \rightarrow C$, and $C \rightarrow A$ to prove $A \leftrightarrow B \leftrightarrow C$. Below, we can see that the circles are in a donut shape which makes this true.

#### Induction

Hey, we got something fun to play with. It's labeled "inductive step". Looks like a world with a circle $A_{n}$ and a bigger circle $A_{n+1}$ on it. 

Oops, I dropped it and it opened! It's an implication now, with a small circle on one end and a big one on the other. Let's see. If we have a world and just one circle $A_1$, we get...

Woah! A whole sequence of statements $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n$.

title: squib_unalive_etc_revs
subject: linguistics
contentHtml:
## What exactly does the word unalive mean?

Unalive is a very interesting new word in online culture. Although it derives from the word "alive", it is not a noun but a verb. It shares some properties with "kill", and perhaps with "die", but which exactly?

In class, we noted that from a compositional viewpoint, the word *dead* does not mean *not alive*. Rather, it can only apply to individuals who have been alive, e.g.

t_-: e [[alive]]
t_0: e NOT [[alive]]

In this way, Abe Lincoln is dead, but not the Eiffel Tower. 
Here, we will also assume we have die and kill as composed of one another:

die: become [[dead]]
kill: cause [[become [[dead]]]]

Depending on the context, unalive can mean "die", "kill", or "kill myself". Here are representative examples for each (source: reddit)

- (die) "Has anyone gone to the ER wanting to unalive due to insomnia?"

- (kill myself) "I wish I had unalive pills right now".
  
- (kill) "... started growing on a tree where my aunt unalives chickens"

## unalive is a "complete" kill

My hypothesis is that "unalive" is a more complete version of the word "kill": it has the regular transitive usage, and the reflexive use in the intransitive.


A compositional definition for unalive must involve "alive", not in the sense of an opposite ("unemployed"), but in the sense of reversal, as with "undo", "unpack", or "unzip". Someone can be unemployed without ever having been employed, but you cannot undo something that has not been done. In the same way, you must be alive to unalive.

Yet the word "kill" lacks an intransitive version that means "to kill oneself". For the purposes of online communication, this use of kill appears much more frequently than its *true* intransitive, that is "they/he/she kill". However, "they/he/she unalived" has that exact meaning, that is "they/he/she killed herself". 


## slay

It's worth noting that roughly analogous to "kill", there is a popular term "slay" with positive connotations. For example, if a friend gives a presentation in class, it is common for someone to say that they "slayed" or just the exclamative/imperative "slay!". This can probably be traced to the older expression "they/he/she killed it". One possible rationale for the informal "slay" replacing the informal "kill" in the intransitive is that the conventional use of intransitive "slay" is slightly more appropriate than the intransitive form of "kill". Compare:

"Borcs coming left and right all night, they slayed till dawn".
"Borcs coming left and right all night, they killed till dawn".

Let's go back to "unalive". I contend that "unalive" means "kill", but with the same structure as transitive-intransitive words like "wash". In the base case, it can be thought of as an transitive verb with a subject and object. In the intransitive case, null complement anaphora fills in the object. 

"I wash him"
"I wash" (implies "I wash myself")

"I unalive him"
"I unalive" (implies "I unalive myself")

Importantly, "kill" does not have this usage:
"I kill him"
"I kill" 

The most common form we see unalive is "I want to unalive" or "I want to unalive myself". Although in traditional usage we might find "I want to die" and "I want to kill myself" as parallel, it is much more useful to compare against "I want to wash" and "I want to wash myself". 

It is clear they have equivalent meaning. That is, "I want to unalive" does not mean "I want to die". It means "I want to kill" in the reflexive intransitive, or "I want to kill myself". It is an economical way to say "kill myself", that avoids both the negative connotations of "commit suicide" and the passivity of "die".

title: darwinian_dilemma
subject: linguistics
contentHtml:
### Moral Realism and Anti-Realism

Where antirealists part ways with realists is in denying that there are evaluative truths which hold independently of the whole set of evaluative judgements we make or might make upon reflection. According to realists, the truth that Hitler was morally depraved holds independently of any stance that we or Hitler might take towards the truth. There is a real property of morality out there. There are "objectively wrong" stances one can take.

According to the non-naturalist versions of value realism, evaluative facts or truths are not reducible to any kind of natural fact. There are irreducibly normative facts or truths, which can be thought of having a different source of origin than natural fact (Divine intervention? Numbers?) 

Value naturalism holds that evaluative facts are identical to or constituted by certain natural facts, and that evaluative facts ARE the kinds of things that play a role in causal explanations.

one enormous factor in shaping the content of human values have been the forces of natural selection.

The Darwinian Dilemma posits that evaluative tendencies stem from genetic differences

The chance, if there IS an evaluative truth, that our evaluative judgements happen to align with it, seems to be exceedinly small.

...as a matter of sheer chance, some large portion of our evaluative judgments ended up true, due to a happy coincidence between the realist’s independent evaluative truths and the evaluative directions in which natural selection tended to push us, but this would require a fluke of luck that’s not only extremely unlikely, in view of the huge universe of logically possible evaluative judgments and truths, but also astoundingly convenient to the realist.

Does street think that an evolutionary explanation of our color perception undermines our knowledge of the colors of objects? <-> does street think that an evolutionary explanation of morality undermines our knowledge of true moral facts?

why is evaluative "truth" posited as being so antithetical to the darwinian hypothesis? There are no possibilities for what the truth might be other than the moral judgements of those around us.

Why can't it be both?

The greatest good is styrofoam <-> Our notion of coherency is also determined by the darwinian conclusion.

How about: argument for human imagination.

### Undermining Defeat

Some arguments can be "undermined" by challenging axioms of your argument.

We were entitled to take our moral intuitions to be evidence of the MORAL TRUTH... but because the darwinian dilemma posits moral judgements as (mechanical) reactions, we can't.

Different species have "morality" of kinds. Maybe the reason is that humans have generally lived longer than animals on objective metrics.

Some argument from the "reflection" perspective: maybe moral judgements warrant more reflection than judgements of disgusting or tasty things...

There's a big degree of difference between how certain we are in our moral beliefs and the darwinian dilemma -- that our moral beliefs are determined mechanically.

What even is the darwinian dilemma? what does "very unlikely" mean? She agrees with me.

title: error_theory_3
subject: linguistics
contentHtml:
### Elimination, or revision?

Mackie and Street agree that there are no moral facts. Mackie believes that morality is a myth and there is no moral right or wrong. Street believes that it is in fine shape, even though it is simply invented by us. 

Maybe it's like this:

We thought that X's would have F and G.

But there is nothing like that.

But was being F and G built into the concept of X?

Yes: then we have discovered that there are no Xs. This is the elimination, the null hypothesis.
No: then we have discovered that the X's were not as we thought. This is revision, the revised hypothesis.

### NAIVE PHYSICS

Before learning about science, we usually think of a solid object as something that fills the space within its boundaries, continuous, and contains no empty spaces (unlike a sponge). 

But there are no such things.
We are not inclined to say that science has discovered that there are no solid objects. We would rather have a revised (scientific?) version of solidity.

Because we treat solids as a revisable concept! Fundamentally, solids are still intuitively "solid".

### NAIVE WITCHES

A witch is a woman who 
- is socially outcast
- seems odd, eccentric
- can do magic and communes with satan

But we have discovered that there are no such women. Why don't we say, like with solids, that "witches aren't magic or satanic after all"?

Because we treat witches as an eliminable concept! Fundamentally, witches would no longer be intuitively "witch-like".

Okay, anyways...

**Can we have the intrinsic TO-BE-DONENESS without metaphysical profligacy?**

Consider the case of numbers. They
- exist independently of space and time
- necessarily have their properties
- knowable a priori
- casually isolated

Maybe this is a bad way to think about numbers. What is *queer* is the process of reasoning in mathematics... it is very different (how?) than reasoning about the rain, glasses, what to wear...

Moral reasoning is reasoning about what to do (is that true? Perhaps it is also how to react to what others do, or what one has done). Practical reasoning is different from theoretical reasoning, so its objects will look queer against the background of the objects of theoretical reasoning. 

Namely, it has much wider scope ... 

The *degree* and *quality* of disagreement in practical and theoretical affairs is very different.

Is there an aim to which all rational endeavors end? Yes, rationalism! Moral facts are **theorems of practical reason**. 
- Act only on those maxims which you can at the same time will to be universal laws.
- Don't do it to me if you can't accept my doing it to you.
- Derivation from rules built into the structure of practical reasoning.

Okay. So are there any universal laws? Categorical Imperatives? Phillipa Foot.

"You ought to practice your serve"

"*If you want to improve at tennis*, you ought to practice your serve". Essentially exceptions are always saying that you "don't care". Perhaps the real question is, is it possible to have someone who both does not care and also disagrees?

### Categoricity as an Illocutionary force
Here is why categoricity is an illocutionary force:
You are allowed to ask: "yes, but what's that to me?"

You must take the A Train.
 - Why?
 - It's the fastest way to get to Sugar Hill.
 - *Oh, but I don't want to go to Sugar Hill*
 - Okay, never mind then.

You ought to treat your workers with more respect.
 - *Oh, but I don't care about them.*
 - Okay, never mind then... no, wait!

Another similarity is that its hard to imagine someone forcing a moral requirement in an unmoral way; edicts of etiquette are seldom mentioned with an unetiquette way. Maybe Chesterson's fence is at play here...

Maybe the social contract: if you are going to eat at my restaurant, you should follow my rules. 

### Motivating Reasons

Original definition of what a "reason" is:
A's reason to F: A's desire to X + A's belief that Fing will X.

But we can have a desire and a belief without a reason.
The guy drinking a petrol cocktail *thinks* he has a reason. We *don't think* he has a reason.

But we have another way to formulate it (Mark Shroeder):
A's reason to F: A's belief if true, would explain why Fing will X.

Complaint:
A's reason to F: A's belief that giving you the injection will prevent the (low probability) disease.

Similarly to "volunteer" notion of moralism: Owen Wingrave has no internal motivation to join the army. Owen's father maintains that he has a reason to join the army. He means this in an external sense. What is that sense? Williams says: there are no external reasons.

Reasons: reasons are explainers. 
Reason to live vs. Reason to go vegetarian.
If something can be a reason for an action, then it could be someone's reason for acting on a particular occasion, and it would then figure in an explanation of that action.

To vindicate morality, requirements must derive from our reasons.
But, all of our TRUE reasons are internal.
The contents of a person's S are contingent and not necessarily optional.
So any substantive requirement of morality can have rational authority only for those in Foot's "volunteer army".

But wait a minute. Humeans doubt the power of practical reason to produce moral motivation. Humeans believe that two things can be true or false: empirical results, and knowledge about the relation of ideas. Reasoning can't produce motivation. Or, reasoning can't produce *moral* motivation.

IPython uses a shared copyright model. Each contributor maintains copyright

Development Team. If individual contributors want to maintain a record of what

their copyright in the commit message of the change, when they commit the

title: grammar310
subject: nlp
contentHtml:
# Grammar for Python

# NOTE WELL: You should also follow all the steps listed at
# https://devguide.python.org/grammar/

# Start symbols for the grammar:
#       single_input is a single interactive statement;
#       file_input is a module or sequence of commands read from an input file;
#       eval_input is the input for the eval() functions.
# NB: compound_stmt in single_input is followed by extra NEWLINE!
single_input: NEWLINE | simple_stmt | compound_stmt NEWLINE
file_input: stmt* ENDMARKER
eval_input: testlist NEWLINE* ENDMARKER

decorator: '@' namedexpr_test NEWLINE
decorators: decorator+
decorated: decorators (classdef | funcdef | async_funcdef)

async_funcdef: 'async' funcdef
funcdef: 'def' NAME parameters ['->' test] ':' suite

parameters: '(' [typedargslist] ')'
typedargslist: (
  (tfpdef ['=' test] (',' tfpdef ['=' test])* ',' '/' [',' [ tfpdef ['=' test] (
        ',' tfpdef ['=' test])* ([',' [
        '*' [tfpdef] (',' tfpdef ['=' test])* [',' ['**' tfpdef [',']]]
      | '**' tfpdef [',']]])
  | '*' [tfpdef] (',' tfpdef ['=' test])* ([',' ['**' tfpdef [',']]])
  | '**' tfpdef [',']]] )
|  (tfpdef ['=' test] (',' tfpdef ['=' test])* [',' [
        '*' [tfpdef] (',' tfpdef ['=' test])* [',' ['**' tfpdef [',']]]
      | '**' tfpdef [',']]]
  | '*' [tfpdef] (',' tfpdef ['=' test])* [',' ['**' tfpdef [',']]]
  | '**' tfpdef [','])
)
tfpdef: NAME [':' test]
varargslist: vfpdef ['=' test ](',' vfpdef ['=' test])* ',' '/' [',' [ (vfpdef ['=' test] (',' vfpdef ['=' test])* [',' [
        '*' [vfpdef] (',' vfpdef ['=' test])* [',' ['**' vfpdef [',']]]
      | '**' vfpdef [',']]]
  | '*' [vfpdef] (',' vfpdef ['=' test])* [',' ['**' vfpdef [',']]]
  | '**' vfpdef [',']) ]] | (vfpdef ['=' test] (',' vfpdef ['=' test])* [',' [
        '*' [vfpdef] (',' vfpdef ['=' test])* [',' ['**' vfpdef [',']]]
      | '**' vfpdef [',']]]
  | '*' [vfpdef] (',' vfpdef ['=' test])* [',' ['**' vfpdef [',']]]
  | '**' vfpdef [',']
)
vfpdef: NAME

stmt: simple_stmt | compound_stmt | NEWLINE
simple_stmt: small_stmt (';' small_stmt)* [';'] NEWLINE
small_stmt: (expr_stmt | del_stmt | pass_stmt | flow_stmt |
             import_stmt | global_stmt | nonlocal_stmt | assert_stmt)
expr_stmt: testlist_star_expr (annassign | augassign (yield_expr|testlist) |
                     ('=' (yield_expr|testlist_star_expr))*)
annassign: ':' test ['=' (yield_expr|testlist_star_expr)]
testlist_star_expr: (test|star_expr) (',' (test|star_expr))* [',']
augassign: ('+=' | '-=' | '*=' | '@=' | '/=' | '%=' | '&=' | '|=' | '^=' |
            '<<=' | '>>=' | '**=' | '//=')
# For normal and annotated assignments, additional restrictions enforced by the interpreter
del_stmt: 'del' exprlist
pass_stmt: 'pass'
flow_stmt: break_stmt | continue_stmt | return_stmt | raise_stmt | yield_stmt
break_stmt: 'break'
continue_stmt: 'continue'
return_stmt: 'return' [testlist_star_expr]
yield_stmt: yield_expr
raise_stmt: 'raise' [test ['from' test]]
import_stmt: import_name | import_from
import_name: 'import' dotted_as_names
# note below: the ('.' | '...') is necessary because '...' is tokenized as ELLIPSIS
import_from: ('from' (('.' | '...')* dotted_name | ('.' | '...')+)
              'import' ('*' | '(' import_as_names ')' | import_as_names))
import_as_name: NAME ['as' NAME]
dotted_as_name: dotted_name ['as' NAME]
import_as_names: import_as_name (',' import_as_name)* [',']
dotted_as_names: dotted_as_name (',' dotted_as_name)*
dotted_name: NAME ('.' NAME)*
global_stmt: 'global' NAME (',' NAME)*
nonlocal_stmt: 'nonlocal' NAME (',' NAME)*
assert_stmt: 'assert' test [',' test]

compound_stmt: if_stmt | while_stmt | for_stmt | try_stmt | with_stmt | funcdef | classdef | decorated | async_stmt
async_stmt: 'async' (funcdef | with_stmt | for_stmt)
if_stmt: 'if' namedexpr_test ':' suite ('elif' namedexpr_test ':' suite)* ['else' ':' suite]
while_stmt: 'while' namedexpr_test ':' suite ['else' ':' suite]
for_stmt: 'for' exprlist 'in' testlist ':' suite ['else' ':' suite]
try_stmt: ('try' ':' suite
           ((except_clause ':' suite)+
            ['else' ':' suite]
            ['finally' ':' suite] |
           'finally' ':' suite))
with_stmt: 'with' with_item (',' with_item)*  ':' suite
with_item: test ['as' expr]
# NB compile.c makes sure that the default except clause is last
except_clause: 'except' [test ['as' NAME]]
suite: simple_stmt | NEWLINE INDENT stmt+ DEDENT

namedexpr_test: test [':=' test]
test: or_test ['if' or_test 'else' test] | lambdef
lambdef: 'lambda' [varargslist] ':' test
or_test: and_test ('or' and_test)*
and_test: not_test ('and' not_test)*
not_test: 'not' not_test | comparison
comparison: expr (comp_op expr)*
# <> isn't actually a valid comparison operator in Python. It's here for the
# sake of a __future__ import described in PEP 401 (which really works :-)
comp_op: '<'|'>'|'=='|'>='|'<='|'<>'|'!='|'in'|'not' 'in'|'is'|'is' 'not'
star_expr: '*' expr
expr: xor_expr ('|' xor_expr)*
xor_expr: and_expr ('^' and_expr)*
and_expr: shift_expr ('&' shift_expr)*
shift_expr: arith_expr (('<<'|'>>') arith_expr)*
arith_expr: term (('+'|'-') term)*
term: factor (('*'|'@'|'/'|'%'|'//') factor)*
factor: ('+'|'-'|'~') factor | power
power: atom_expr ['**' factor]
atom_expr: ['await'] atom trailer*
atom: ('(' [yield_expr|testlist_comp] ')' |
       '[' [testlist_comp] ']' |
       '{' [dictorsetmaker] '}' |
       NAME | NUMBER | strings | '...' | 'None' | 'True' | 'False')
testlist_comp: (namedexpr_test|star_expr) ( comp_for | (',' (namedexpr_test|star_expr))* [','] )
trailer: '(' [arglist] ')' | '[' subscriptlist ']' | '.' NAME
subscriptlist: subscript (',' subscript)* [',']
subscript: test [':=' test] | [test] ':' [test] [sliceop]
sliceop: ':' [test]
exprlist: (expr|star_expr) (',' (expr|star_expr))* [',']
testlist: test (',' test)* [',']
dictorsetmaker: ( ((test ':' test | '**' expr)
                   (comp_for | (',' (test ':' test | '**' expr))* [','])) |
                  ((test [':=' test] | star_expr)
                   (comp_for | (',' (test [':=' test] | star_expr))* [','])) )

classdef: 'class' NAME ['(' [arglist] ')'] ':' suite

arglist: argument (',' argument)*  [',']

# The reason that keywords are test nodes instead of NAME is that using NAME
# results in an ambiguity. ast.c makes sure it's a NAME.
# "test '=' test" is really "keyword '=' test", but we have no such token.
# These need to be in a single rule to avoid grammar that is ambiguous
# to our LL(1) parser. Even though 'test' includes '*expr' in star_expr,
# we explicitly match '*' here, too, to give it proper precedence.
# Illegal combinations and orderings are blocked in ast.c:
# multiple (test comp_for) arguments are blocked; keyword unpackings
# that precede iterable unpackings are blocked; etc.
argument: ( test [comp_for] |
            test ':=' test |
            test '=' test |
            '**' test |
            '*' test )

comp_iter: comp_for | comp_if
sync_comp_for: 'for' exprlist 'in' or_test [comp_iter]
comp_for: ['async'] sync_comp_for
comp_if: 'if' or_test [comp_iter]

# not used in grammar, but may appear in "node" passed from Parser to Compiler
encoding_decl: NAME

yield_expr: 'yield' [yield_arg]
yield_arg: 'from' test | testlist_star_expr

strings: (STRING | fstring)+
fstring: FSTRING_START fstring_content* FSTRING_END
fstring_content: FSTRING_STRING | fstring_expr
fstring_conversion: '!' NAME
fstring_expr: '{' (testlist_comp | yield_expr) ['='] [ fstring_conversion ] [ fstring_format_spec ] '}'
fstring_format_spec: ':' fstring_content*
preview: # Grammar for Python # NOTE WELL: You should also

continue_stmt: 'continue'
return_stmt: 'return' [testlist_star_expr]
yield_stmt: yield_expr
raise_stmt: 'raise' [test ['from' test]]

# note below: the ('.' | '...') is necessary because '...' is tokenized as ELLIPSIS

              'import' ('*' | '(' import_as_names ')' | import_as_names))

dotted_as_name: dotted_name ['as' NAME]
import_as_names: import_as_name (',' import_as_name)* [',']
dotted_as_names: dotted_as_name (',' dotted_as_name)*
dotted_name: NAME ('.' NAME)*
global_stmt: 'global' NAME (',' NAME)*
nonlocal_stmt: 'nonlocal' NAME (',' NAME)*
assert_stmt: 'assert' test [',' test]

async_stmt: 'async' (funcdef | with_stmt | for_stmt)

while_stmt: 'while' namedexpr_test ':' suite ['else' ':' suite]
for_stmt: 'for' exprlist 'in' testlist ':' suite ['else' ':' suite]
try_stmt: ('try' ':' suite
           ((except_clause ':' suite)+
            ['else' ':' suite]
            ['finally' ':' suite] |
           'finally' ':' suite))
with_stmt: 'with' with_item (',' with_item)*  ':' suite
with_item: test ['as' expr]
# NB compile.c makes sure that the default except clause is last
except_clause: 'except' [test ['as' NAME]]
suite: simple_stmt | NEWLINE INDENT stmt+ DEDENT

namedexpr_test: test [':=' test]
test: or_test ['if' or_test 'else' test] | lambdef
lambdef: 'lambda' [varargslist] ':' test
or_test: and_test ('or' and_test)*
and_test: not_test ('and' not_test)*
not_test: 'not' not_test | comparison
comparison: expr (comp_op expr)*

# sake of a __future__ import described in PEP 401 (which really works :-)

star_expr: '*' expr
expr: xor_expr ('|' xor_expr)*
xor_expr: and_expr ('^' and_expr)*
and_expr: shift_expr ('&' shift_expr)*
shift_expr: arith_expr (('<<'|'>>') arith_expr)*
arith_expr: term (('+'|'-') term)*
term: factor (('*'|'@'|'/'|'%'|'//') factor)*

atom_expr: ['await'] atom trailer*
atom: ('(' [yield_expr|testlist_comp] ')' |
       '[' [testlist_comp] ']' |

file_input: stmt* ENDMARKER
eval_input: testlist NEWLINE* ENDMARKER

decorator: '@' namedexpr_test NEWLINE
decorators: decorator+
decorated: decorators (classdef | funcdef | async_funcdef)

async_funcdef: 'async' funcdef
funcdef: 'def' NAME parameters ['->' test] ':' suite

parameters: '(' [typedargslist] ')'
typedargslist: (
  (tfpdef ['=' test] (',' tfpdef ['=' test])* ',' '/' [',' [ tfpdef ['=' test] (
        ',' tfpdef ['=' test])* ([',' [
        '*' [tfpdef] (',' tfpdef ['=' test])* [',' ['**' tfpdef [',']]]
      | '**' tfpdef [',']]])
  | '*' [tfpdef] (',' tfpdef ['=' test])* ([',' ['**' tfpdef [',']]])
  | '**' tfpdef [',']]] )
|  (tfpdef ['=' test] (',' tfpdef ['=' test])* [',' [
        '*' [tfpdef] (',' tfpdef ['=' test])* [',' ['**' tfpdef [',']]]
      | '**' tfpdef [',']]]
  | '*' [tfpdef] (',' tfpdef ['=' test])* [',' ['**' tfpdef [',']]]
  | '**' tfpdef [','])
)
tfpdef: NAME [':' test]
varargslist: vfpdef ['=' test ](',' vfpdef ['=' test])* ',' '/' [',' [ (vfpdef ['=' test] (',' vfpdef ['=' test])* [',' [
        '*' [vfpdef] (',' vfpdef ['=' test])* [',' ['**' vfpdef [',']]]
      | '**' vfpdef [',']]]
  | '*' [vfpdef] (',' vfpdef ['=' test])* [',' ['**' vfpdef [',']]]
  | '**' vfpdef [',']) ]] | (vfpdef ['=' test] (',' vfpdef ['=' test])* [',' [
        '*' [vfpdef] (',' vfpdef ['=' test])* [',' ['**' vfpdef [',']]]

)

            '<<=' | '>>=' | '**=' | '//=')

del_stmt: 'del' exprlist
pass_stmt: 'pass'

continue_stmt: 'continue'
return_stmt: 'return' [testlist]
yield_stmt: yield_expr

import_from: ('from' (('.' | '...')* dotted_name | ('.' | '...')+)
              'import' ('*' | '(' import_as_names ')' | import_as_names))

dotted_name: NAME ('.' NAME)*
global_stmt: 'global' NAME (',' NAME)*
nonlocal_stmt: 'nonlocal' NAME (',' NAME)*

async_stmt: 'async' (funcdef | with_stmt | for_stmt)
if_stmt: 'if' test ':' suite ('elif' test ':' suite)* ['else' ':' suite]
while_stmt: 'while' test ':' suite ['else' ':' suite]
for_stmt: 'for' exprlist 'in' testlist ':' suite ['else' ':' suite]

            ['else' ':' suite]

with_stmt: 'with' with_item (',' with_item)*  ':' suite

comparison: expr (comp_op expr)*
# <> isn't actually a valid comparison operator in Python. It's here for the

testlist: test (',' test)* [',']

            test '=' test |
            '**' test |
            '*' test )

comp_iter: comp_for | comp_if
sync_comp_for: 'for' exprlist 'in' or_test [comp_iter]
comp_for: ['async'] sync_comp_for
comp_if: 'if' test_nocond [comp_iter]

# not used in grammar, but may appear in "node" passed from Parser to Compiler
encoding_decl: NAME

yield_expr: 'yield' [yield_arg]
yield_arg: 'from' test | testlist

fstring: FSTRING_START fstring_content* FSTRING_END
fstring_content: FSTRING_STRING | fstring_expr

augassign: ('+=' | '-=' | '*=' | '@=' | '/=' | '%=' | '&=' | '|=' | '^=' |
            '<<=' | '>>=' | '**=' | '//=')
# For normal and annotated assignments, additional restrictions enforced by the interpreter

pass_stmt: 'pass'
flow_stmt: break_stmt | continue_stmt | return_stmt | raise_stmt | yield_stmt
break_stmt: 'break'
continue_stmt: 'continue'
return_stmt: 'return' [testlist]
yield_stmt: yield_expr

import_name: 'import' dotted_as_names
# note below: the ('.' | '...') is necessary because '...' is tokenized as ELLIPSIS

              'import' ('*' | '(' import_as_names ')' | import_as_names))

dotted_as_name: dotted_name ['as' NAME]
import_as_names: import_as_name (',' import_as_name)* [',']
dotted_as_names: dotted_as_name (',' dotted_as_name)*
dotted_name: NAME ('.' NAME)*
global_stmt: 'global' NAME (',' NAME)*
nonlocal_stmt: 'nonlocal' NAME (',' NAME)*
assert_stmt: 'assert' test [',' test]

while_stmt: 'while' test ':' suite ['else' ':' suite]
for_stmt: 'for' exprlist 'in' testlist ':' suite ['else' ':' suite]
try_stmt: ('try' ':' suite
           ((except_clause ':' suite)+
            ['else' ':' suite]
            ['finally' ':' suite] |
           'finally' ':' suite))
with_stmt: 'with' with_item (',' with_item)*  ':' suite
with_item: test ['as' expr]
# NB compile.c makes sure that the default except clause is last
except_clause: 'except' [test ['as' NAME]]
suite: simple_stmt | NEWLINE INDENT stmt+ DEDENT

test: or_test ['if' or_test 'else' test] | lambdef
test_nocond: or_test | lambdef_nocond
lambdef: 'lambda' [varargslist] ':' test
lambdef_nocond: 'lambda' [varargslist] ':' test_nocond
or_test: and_test ('or' and_test)*
and_test: not_test ('and' not_test)*
not_test: 'not' not_test | comparison
comparison: expr (comp_op expr)*

# sake of a __future__ import described in PEP 401 (which really works :-)
comp_op: '<'|'>'|'=='|'>='|'<='|'<>'|'!='|'in'|'not' 'in'|'is'|'is' 'not'
star_expr: '*' expr
expr: xor_expr ('|' xor_expr)*
xor_expr: and_expr ('^' and_expr)*
and_expr: shift_expr ('&' shift_expr)*
shift_expr: arith_expr (('<<'|'>>') arith_expr)*
arith_expr: term (('+'|'-') term)*
term: factor (('*'|'@'|'/'|'%'|'//') factor)*

power: atom_expr ['**' factor]
atom_expr: ['await'] atom trailer*

       '[' [testlist_comp] ']' |
       '{' [dictorsetmaker] '}' |

sliceop: ':' [test]

comp_if: 'if' test_nocond [comp_iter]

yield_expr: 'yield' [yield_arg]
yield_arg: 'from' test | testlist

strings: (STRING | fstring)+
fstring: FSTRING_START fstring_content* FSTRING_END
fstring_content: FSTRING_STRING | fstring_expr
fstring_conversion: '!' NAME
fstring_expr: '{' (testlist_comp | yield_expr) [ fstring_conversion ] [ fstring_format_spec ] '}'

# Grammar for Python

single_input: NEWLINE | simple_stmt | compound_stmt NEWLINE
file_input: stmt* ENDMARKER
eval_input: testlist NEWLINE* ENDMARKER

decorator: '@' dotted_name [ '(' [arglist] ')' ] NEWLINE
decorators: decorator+
decorated: decorators (classdef | funcdef | async_funcdef)

async_funcdef: 'async' funcdef
funcdef: 'def' NAME parameters ['->' test] ':' suite

parameters: '(' [typedargslist] ')'
typedargslist: (
  (tfpdef ['=' test] (',' tfpdef ['=' test])* ',' '/' [',' [ tfpdef ['=' test] (
        ',' tfpdef ['=' test])* ([',' [
        '*' [tfpdef] (',' tfpdef ['=' test])* [',' ['**' tfpdef [',']]]
      | '**' tfpdef [',']]])
  | '*' [tfpdef] (',' tfpdef ['=' test])* ([',' ['**' tfpdef [',']]])
  | '**' tfpdef [',']]] )
|  (tfpdef ['=' test] (',' tfpdef ['=' test])* [',' [
        '*' [tfpdef] (',' tfpdef ['=' test])* [',' ['**' tfpdef [',']]]
      | '**' tfpdef [',']]]
  | '*' [tfpdef] (',' tfpdef ['=' test])* [',' ['**' tfpdef [',']]]
  | '**' tfpdef [','])
)
tfpdef: NAME [':' test]
varargslist: vfpdef ['=' test ](',' vfpdef ['=' test])* ',' '/' [',' [ (vfpdef ['=' test] (',' vfpdef ['=' test])* [',' [
        '*' [vfpdef] (',' vfpdef ['=' test])* [',' ['**' vfpdef [',']]]
      | '**' vfpdef [',']]]
  | '*' [vfpdef] (',' vfpdef ['=' test])* [',' ['**' vfpdef [',']]]
  | '**' vfpdef [',']) ]] | (vfpdef ['=' test] (',' vfpdef ['=' test])* [',' [
        '*' [vfpdef] (',' vfpdef ['=' test])* [',' ['**' vfpdef [',']]]
      | '**' vfpdef [',']]]
  | '*' [vfpdef] (',' vfpdef ['=' test])* [',' ['**' vfpdef [',']]]

            '<<=' | '>>=' | '**=' | '//=')
# For normal and annotated assignments, additional restrictions enforced by the interpreter

continue_stmt: 'continue'

raise_stmt: 'raise' [test ['from' test]]

import_name: 'import' dotted_as_names

dotted_as_names: dotted_as_name (',' dotted_as_name)*
dotted_name: NAME ('.' NAME)*

title: README
subject: nlp
contentHtml:
This folder contains vendored dependencies of the debugger.

Right now this means the 'bytecode' library (MIT license).

To update the version remove the bytecode* contents from this folder and then use:

pip install bytecode --target .

or from master (if needed for some early bugfix):

python -m pip install https://github.com/MatthieuDartiailh/bytecode/archive/main.zip --target .

Then run 'pydevd_fix_code.py' to fix the imports on the vendored file, run its tests (to see
if things are still ok) and commit.

Then, to finish, apply the patch to add the offset to the instructions (bcb8a28669e9178f96f5d71af7259e0674acc47c)

Note: commit the egg-info as a note of the license (force if needed).
preview: This folder contains vendored dependencies of the debugger. Right now

title: README
subject: nlp
contentHtml:
This folder contains the utilities to attach a target process to the pydev debugger.

The main module to be called for the attach is:

attach_pydevd.py

it should be called as;

python attach_pydevd.py --port 5678 --pid 1234

Note that the client is responsible for having a remote debugger alive in the given port for the attach to work.


The binaries are now compiled at:
- https://github.com/fabioz/PyDev.Debugger.binaries/actions
(after generation the binaries are copied to this repo)


To copy:
cd /D X:\PyDev.Debugger
"C:\Program Files\7-Zip\7z" e C:\Users\fabio\Downloads\win_binaries.zip -oX:\PyDev.Debugger\pydevd_attach_to_process * -r -y
"C:\Program Files\7-Zip\7z" e C:\Users\fabio\Downloads\linux_binaries.zip -oX:\PyDev.Debugger\pydevd_attach_to_process * -r -y
"C:\Program Files\7-Zip\7z" e C:\Users\fabio\Downloads\mac_binaries.zip -oX:\PyDev.Debugger\pydevd_attach_to_process * -r -y
git add *.exe
git add *.dll
git add *.dylib
git add *.so
preview: This folder contains the utilities to attach a target process

title: README
subject: nlp
contentHtml:
Extensions allow extending the debugger without modifying the debugger code. This is implemented with explicit namespace
packages.

To implement your own extension:

1. Ensure that the root folder of your extension is in sys.path (add it to PYTHONPATH) 
2. Ensure that your module follows the directory structure below
3. The ``__init__.py`` files inside the pydevd_plugin and extension folder must contain the preamble below,
and nothing else.
Preamble: 
```python
try:
    __import__('pkg_resources').declare_namespace(__name__)
except ImportError:
    import pkgutil
    __path__ = pkgutil.extend_path(__path__, __name__)
```
4. Your plugin name inside the extensions folder must start with `"pydevd_plugin"`
5. Implement one or more of the abstract base classes defined in `_pydevd_bundle.pydevd_extension_api`. This can be done
by either inheriting from them or registering with the abstract base class.

* Directory structure:
```
|--  root_directory-> must be on python path
|    |-- pydevd_plugins
|    |   |-- __init__.py -> must contain preamble
|    |   |-- extensions
|    |   |   |-- __init__.py -> must contain preamble
|    |   |   |-- pydevd_plugin_plugin_name.py
```
preview: Extensions allow extending the debugger without modifying the debugger code.

title: README
subject: nlp
contentHtml:
=======================================================
 Nose plugin with IPython and extension module support
=======================================================

This directory provides the key functionality for test support that IPython
needs as a nose plugin, which can be installed for use in projects other than
IPython.

The presence of a Makefile here is mostly for development and debugging
purposes as it only provides a few shorthand commands.  You can manually
install the plugin by using standard Python procedures (``setup.py install``
with appropriate arguments).

To install the plugin using the Makefile, edit its first line to reflect where
you'd like the installation.

Once you've set the prefix, simply build/install the plugin with::

  make

and run the tests with::

  make test

You should see output similar to::

  maqroll[plugin]> make test
  nosetests -s --with-ipdoctest --doctest-tests dtexample.py
  ..
  ----------------------------------------------------------------------
  Ran 2 tests in 0.016s

  OK

title: test_combo
subject: nlp
contentHtml:
=======================
 Combo testing example
=======================

This is a simple example that mixes ipython doctests::

    In [1]: import code

    In [2]: 2**12
    Out[2]: 4096

with command-line example information that does *not* get executed::

    $ mpirun -n 4 ipengine --controller-port=10000 --controller-ip=host0

and with literal examples of Python source code::

    controller = dict(host='myhost',
		      engine_port=None, # default is 10105
		      control_port=None,
		      )

    # keys are hostnames, values are the number of engine on that host
    engines = dict(node1=2,
		   node2=2,
		   node3=2,
		   node3=2,
		   )

    # Force failure to detect that this test is being run.
    1/0

These source code examples are executed but no output is compared at all.  An
error or failure is reported only if an exception is raised.

NOTE: the execution of pure python blocks is not yet working!
preview: ======================= Combo testing example ======================= This is a simple example

title: test_example
subject: nlp
contentHtml:
=====================================
 Tests in example form - pure python
=====================================

This file contains doctest examples embedded as code blocks, using normal
Python prompts.  See the accompanying file for similar examples using IPython
prompts (you can't mix both types within one file). The following will be run
as a test::

    >>> 1+1
    2
    >>> print ("hello")
    hello

More than one example works::

    >>> s="Hello World"

    >>> s.upper()
    'HELLO WORLD'

but you should note that the *entire* test file is considered to be a single
test.  Individual code blocks that fail are printed separately as ``example
failures``, but the whole file is still counted and reported as one test.

title: test_exampleip
subject: nlp
contentHtml:
=================================
 Tests in example form - IPython
=================================

You can write text files with examples that use IPython prompts (as long as you
use the nose ipython doctest plugin), but you can not mix and match prompt
styles in a single file.  That is, you either use all ``>>>`` prompts or all
IPython-style prompts.  Your test suite *can* have both types, you just need to
put each type of example in a separate. Using IPython prompts, you can paste
directly from your session::

    In [5]: s="Hello World"

    In [6]: s.upper()
    Out[6]: 'HELLO WORLD'

Another example::

    In [8]: 1+3
    Out[8]: 4

Just like in IPython docstrings, you can use all IPython syntax and features::

    In [9]: !echo hello
    hello

    In [10]: a='hi'

    In [11]: !echo $a
    hi

title: to_be_pursuedness
subject: linguistics
contentHtml:
### The intrinsic justification for being bad (de re) 

Thrasymachus believes that it is like this:

$\exists$ x : x is bad, Thrasymachus wants [to have x].

(de dicto)
Well, actually, it's like this:

Thrasymus wants [to have $x$], x cannot be bad.

### Mackie's (modus tollens) argument from to-be-doneness

Judgement of what is morally good motivates without external help.
But, there are no properties judegment about which motivates without external help. There are no intrinsically motivating properties!
Then, there is no moral good!

### internalisms

perceptual internalism: if S knows that x is good, then S is motivated to pursue S <-> utiliarianism


Okay, there are clear counterexamples to "everyone pursues the good". For example, sociopaths! Us, when we are akratic (well, you did have some motivation to go.)

but...
sociopaths care about themselves! they do pursue the good.

Well, then good is an existence argument; if it exists, then it should be good.

pursuing the good 'in so far as the person is rational'
'is motivated' = 'has some motivation'
motivation can become inverted.. anomie. akratic.

okay, giving money to charity is "good". It's not good. Converse: okay, people think that something isn't "wrong" but it is wrong. 

okay, so what if guilt is learned instinctually and constitutes the "real" moral goodness?

### marquis de sade

(reactionary) i think that if there were a god, there would be less evil on this earth. i believe that if evil exists here below, then either it was willed by God or it was beyond His powers to prevent it. Now, I cannot bring myself to fear a God who is either spiteful or weak. I defy Him without fear and care not a fig for his thunderbolts... He's actually good OMG

wanting conflates state of posession and possession itself.

The kinds of things that lead somebody to gain pleasure at witnessing pain ... has to a product of some deficiency in someone's growth. personal good is not a universal good. NURTURE OVER NATURE!

To continue defending to-be-doneness, we need to say that the perception of sadists is distorted.

Paradise Lost Milton:

All hope excluded thus, behold, instead
of us, outcast, exiled, his new delight,
mankind, created, and for him this World!
So farewell hope, and, with hope, farewell fear,
Farewell remorse! All good to me is lost;
Evil, be thou my Good.
    
is there a difference between utilitarian and deontological good?
This guy says he *ought* to push the green one, but he pushes the red one. How can you tell that someone lost all of their motivation? Well, there are many cases in which someone goes I ought to do something but doesn't. Why? because they are overcommitted, or uncertain, or something else. The "I ought to" doesn't imply motivation, only that your future self would be happier if you do something. (Rationality argument)

title: twin_earth
subject: linguistics
contentHtml:
Twin Earth is exactly like Earth. (It is another planet on our world).
On this planet the liquid called "water" is "XYZ". It is not H20, but a very long and complicated formula.

On this planet they have this other color der. They call everything red this color der...

If a spaceship from Earth ever visits Twin Earth, they will say:
- On twin earth, the word 'water' means XYZ (not an important difference?)

But if they had visited in 1750, then they would have said "water" is "water".

Meaning ain't in the head. 
The reference of natural kind terms are not determined completely by what is inside the speaker.

If the hypothesis that moral properties are derived from natural properties is true, then imagine a moral twin earth exactly like our own, except where everyone is a consequentialist. Now imagine an otherwise perfectly healthy appendectomy patient and five people each missing a vital organ. The consequentialists want to sacrifice them; we would not.

The hypothesis would say that we are not really in disagreement, because we have exactly the same natural environment. But clearly, we are. Thus, moral properties cannot be contingent on natural ones.

There is the argument that this metaphysical experiment is metaethically insignificant. Because the difference between the way Twin Earthers speak to one another is empirically going to be different than our own, even if our intuitions tell us otherwise.

Also, for differences in our moral environment to exist, then there must be some natural difference as well, right?

title: GISP
subject: linguistics
contentHtml:
## Understanding Embeddings GISP

This group independent study course will review recent and ongoing work in vector embeddings (focusing primarily on language, but depending on interests and available material also images, music, and commercial/social applications). It will focus on interpretability, theoretical implications and limitations, and further research directions.

If the course had to pose a question, it would be:

"How can embeddings help us understand ourselves and others better? In what ways can we capture implicit understanding?"

This course is unique because (1): it will focus on embeddings alone, and (2): as much as possible, it will have both technical and theoretical depth. There are a few courses that cover similar content, such as Language Processing in Humans and Machines, or Computational Linguistics. However, unlike most discussion-based courses, students will perform experiments and attempt to implement results. Unlike CSCI 1460, this course focuses primarily on embeddings and their theoretical grounding.

The course may involve reimplementing key papers and results.

Some relevant topics (primarily focusing on language):
Platonic theory of forms, the problem of universals
Compositional and lexical semantics 
Principal Component Analysis
Attention is all you need: https://arxiv.org/pdf/1706.03762.pdf
Word2Vec: https://arxiv.org/pdf/1301.3781.pdf
BERT: https://arxiv.org/pdf/1810.04805.pdf
Bringing Machine Learning and Compositional Semantics together: https://www.annualreviews.org/doi/pdf/10.1146/annurev-linguist-030514-125312


my language sport: https://www.word.golf/
that other language sport: https://semantle.com/
DAT creativity: https://www.datcreativity.com/

title: stats
subject: nlp
contentHtml:
## Expected Value and Convergence
#### Convergence of Discrete CDFs

Let X be an discrete RV with CDF 
$$F_X(x) = \sum_{k = 0}^K p_k \cdot \mathbb{1}_{[x_k, +\infty)}(x)$$


If $\sum_{k = 0}^K p_k \cdot \mathbb{1}_{[x_k, +\infty)}(x) < +\infty$, then the expected value $\mathbb{E}X$ is 
$$\mathbb{E}X = \sum_{k=0}^{K}x_kp_k$$

If $\sum_{k = 0}^K p_k \cdot \mathbb{1}_{[x_k, +\infty)}(x) = +\infty$, then the expected value $\mathbb{E}X$ does not exist.

The mean of $x_kp_k$ is $$\sum_{k=0}^{+\infty}x_k p_k = x_0p_0 + x_1p_1 + x_2p_2 \ldots$$

But by our intuition, $x_0, x_1, x_2 \ldots$ are created equal! The order in which we add them doesn't matter. We should have

$$\sum_{k=0}^{+\infty}x_k p_k = \sum_{k=0}^{+\infty}x_{\sigma(k)} p_{\sigma(k)}$$
so that our sum is permutation invariant. In order to guarantee this equation above is true, we have two concepts:

- If $\sum_{k=0}^{+\infty}|x_k| p_k < +\infty$, then the permutation invariant is true. This also means that the series $\sum_{k=0}^{+\infty}x_k p_k$ is absolutely convergent.

- If $\sum_{k=0}^{+\infty}|x_k| p_k = +\infty$, then it is not permutation-invariant.


#### Convergence of Continuous CDFs

Let X be a continuous RV with PDF $p_X(x)$. 

1. If $\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}|x|p_X(x) < +\infty$, then the expected value is $$\mathbb{E}X = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}xp_X(x)$$.
2. If $\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}|x|p_X(x) = +\infty$, then the expected value $\mathbb{E}X$ does not exist.

**Remark**: The condition " $\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}|x|p_X(x) < +\infty$" invovles something more subtle... "Lebesque integrals".

**Example**: Let X be continuous RV with the following PDF

$$p_X(x) = \frac{1}{\pi(1+x^2)}, x \in \mathbb{R}$$

The expected value does not exist. (to be proved in HW5)

## Transformations of RVs

$$\Omega = \{ \text {students taking APMA 1655}  \} (\omega \in \Omega: \text{is a student})$$

$X(\omega) = \text{ the score } \omega \text{ gets in the final exam }$

The professor wants to curve the score like this:
$$Y(\omega) = \min\{X(\omega)^2, 100\}$$

If $g(x) = \min\{x^2, 100\}$, we have $Y(\omega) = g(Y(\omega))$.
Suppose we are given
- RV $X: \Omega \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$
- a function $g: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$

$$\Omega \rightarrow \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$$
$$\omega \rightarrow X(\omega) \rightarrow g(X(\omega))$$

We know how to calculate $\mathbb{E}X$. To compute $\mathbb{E}[g(X)]$ we need a new definition. 

#### Expected value of an transformed RV
Definition: Suppose $X$ and $g$ are given. 
1. Suppose X is discrete and has CDF $\sum_{k=0}^K p_k \cdot \mathbb{1}_{[x_k, +\infty)}(x)$. 
If $\sum_{k=0}^K|g(x_k)| p_k < +\infty$ then $$\mathbb{E}[g(X)] = \sum_{k=0}^K g(x_k)\cdot p_k.$$
2. Suppose X is continuous and has PDF $p_X(x)$. If $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}|g(x)|p_X(x)dx < +\infty$, then $\mathbb{E}[g(x)] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}g(x)p_X(x)dx$. Otherwise, $\mathbb{E}[g(x)]$ does not exist!

This definition is actually a theorem in graduate-level probability theory course, called "Law of the unconscious statistician".

### Expected value of any RV
For a random variable who's CDF can be decomposed in this way... (all of them)
Definition: Let $X$ be a RV with the following CDF:
$$F_X(x) = p \cdot F_Z(x) + (1 - p)F_W(x)$$
where 
$0 \leq p \leq 1$, 
$Z$ is a discrete RV with CDF $F_Z$
$W$ is a continuous RV with CDF $F_W$.j

We define $\mathbb{E}X = p \cdot \mathbb{E}Z + (1 - p) \cdot \mathbb{E}W$, if $\mathbb{E}Z$ and $\mathbb{E}W$ exist.

## Variance

Let $X$ be a random variable from some distribution. 

The distribution generates numbers $X_1, X_2, ... X_n$.

For example, if we use Bernoulli we will get 0 1 0 1 0 0 1 ...

Well, for this sequence, we can take the average $\bar{X_n} = \frac{X_1 + X_2 + ... + X_n}{n} = \mathbb{E}X$. But this is not exactly equal to $\mathbb{E}X$ for $n = 10$ or $100$...
For $n$ numbers, we have error as:
$$e_n = |\bar{X_n} - \mathbb{E}X|$$

What does the error $e_n$ depend on?

- $n$. For less $n$, the average is usually worse. That is, the average is not the true expected value for a distribution. 
  
- The distribution, of course!

### Simulation Study: Discrete

We can relate the error $e_n$ to the discrete distribution as follows.

Generate $X_1, X_2, \ldots X_n$ from Bernoulli($p$).

Claim: $e_n = |\bar{X_n} - p|$ is likely to be smaller than $$\sqrt{p \cdot (1-p) \cdot \frac{2\log(\log(n))}{n}}$$

$V$ here is $p(1-p)$.

### Simulation Study: Continuous

We have a similar error $e_n$ for continuous distributions.

Generate $X_1, X_2, \ldots X_n$ from Poisson($\lambda$).

Claim: $e_n = |\bar{X_n} - \lambda|$ is likely to be smaller than $$\sqrt{\lambda \cdot \frac{2\log(\log(n))}{n}}$$

$V$ here is $\lambda$.

> What does *likely* mean in this context? If we run a simulation $\infty$ times, then the number of simulations under the curve over the total number approaches 1.


### General conjecture

Assume that the expected value exists.
Generate $X_1, X_2, \ldots X_n$ from a distribution.

$e_n = |\bar{X_n} - \mathbb{E}X|$ is likely to be smaller than $$\sqrt{V \cdot \frac{2\log(\log(n))}{n}}$$

$V$ is the variance of $X$.

This is the [law of the iterated logarithm](https://www.wikiwand.com/en/Law_of_the_iterated_logarithm).

### Definition of Variance

Okay, now we go to a a result. The proof isn't related to anything above, only it's application.

Let X be a RV, whose expected value $\mathbb{E}X$ exists.

We define a function $g(x) = (x - \mathbb{E}X)^2$

We call $\mathbb{E}[g(X)]$ as the variance of $X$ if $\mathbb{E}[g(X)]$ exists. That is,

$$\text{V} (X) = \mathbb{E}[(X - \mathbb{E}X)^2]$$

Variance is nothing but the expected expected squared deviation from the $\mathbb{E}X$. That is, for every $\mathbb{E}X$ we will have an expected squared deviation. In turn, we have a squared deviation, $g(x)$ for each value of $X$.

### Concept Review 


#### 0 is not impossible
E is an event: $E \in \Omega$.

"E is impossible", $E = \emptyset$ means that $\mathbb{P}(E) = 0$.
But $\mathbb{P}(E) = 0$ does not mean that E is impossible!

#### 1 is not inevitable 
Similarly, 

"E is inevitable", $E = \Omega$ means that $\mathbb{P}(E) = 1$.
But $\mathbb{P}(E) = 1$ does not mean that E is inevitable!

#### constant (uniform) distributions

$X(\omega) = c \text{ for all } \omega \in \Omega$
$\text{Var}(X) = 0$

If $\text{Var}(X) = 0$, then there must $ \exists c$
$$\mathbb{P}(\{\omega \in \Omega: X(\omega) = c\}) = 1$$


### Law of Large Numbers (LLN)
A theorem that describes the result of performing an experiment a large number of times. 

Let $X \sim$ a distribution.
The distribution generates random numbers $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$.

$$\bar{X}_n = \frac{X_1 + X_2 +\ldots + X_n}{n} \approx \mathbb{E}X$$

For example:

Bernoulli($p$) has $\mathbb{E}X = p$.
Pois($\lambda$) has $\mathbb{E}X = \lambda$.

Okay. So what does $\approx$ really mean?

And what other conditions do we need for this to be true?

#### RVs i.i.d.

Let $\{X_i\}_{i=1}^{\infty}$ be an infinitely long sequence of RVs defined on $(\Omega, \mathbb{P})$. As we know, we say $X_1, X_2, \ldots $ are independent if 
$$\mathbb{P}(\{\omega \in \Omega: X_i(\omega) \in A_i \text{ for all } i = 1,2,\ldots, n \})$$

Is equal to 
$$\Pi_{i=1}^n \mathbb{P}(\{\omega \in \Omega: X_i(\omega) \in A_i\})$$

They are not just pairwise independent. They are independent!

Definition: Let $\{X_i\}_{i=1}^{\infty}$ be an infinitely long sequence of RVs defined on $(\Omega, \mathbb{P})$. We say $X_1, X_2, \ldots$ are *independently and identically distributed* if 
1. $X_1, X_2, \ldots$ are independent.
2. $X_1, X_2, \ldots$ share the same CDF, i.e. $F_{X_1} = F_{X_2} = F_{X_3} \ldots $

Example: Mike Meng flips a coin. Taylor Swift flips a coin. The outcomes of these coin tosses are two random variables, are independent and share the same CDF.

### back to the LLN

Heuristic: 
$$\bar{X}_n = \frac{X_1 + X_2 +\ldots + X_n}{n} \approx \mathbb{E}X_1$$

Theorem: Let $\{X_i\}_{i=1}^{\infty}$ be an infinitely long sequence of RVs defined on $(\Omega, \mathbb{P})$. 

Suppose that $X_1, X_2, \ldots $ are independently and identically distributed.

Suppose that $\mathbb{E}X$ exists.

As we know, we can take the mean $\bar{X}_n $. Then we know that we have

$$A = \{\omega \in \Omega: \lim_{n \rightarrow \infty} \bar{X}_n(\omega) = \mathbb{E}X_1\}$$
This is the event that involves doing each of these RVs $X_1, X_2, \ldots$ in an infinite sequence once (getting some outcome for each) and then taking the average of them. 
Then $$\mathbb{P}(A) = 1$$

This does not mean that $A = \Omega$ (that A is inevitable).

$$\frac{X_1(\omega) + X_2(\omega) + \ldots}{n} \rightarrow \mathbb{E}X_1$$
The sample average converges to the population average with probability one. This convergence is not inevitable. For example, we have an experiment where we flip a coin an infinitely many number of times, one outcome of which $\omega'$ is all tails.

### infinite coin flips and all tails 
We flip a coin $\infty$ times.

$X_1 = 0$
$X_2 = 0$
...
$X_n = 0$
...
 An outcome is an infinitely long sequence of H and T
$$\omega = (H, H, T, H, T, \ldots)$$

$$\Omega = \{\omega = (\omega^1, \omega^2, \ldots \omega^n)\}$$

Each $w^i$ is either H or T.

$$A = \{ \omega \in \Omega: \lim_{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{X_1 + X_2 +\ldots + X_n}{n} = \frac{1}{2} \}$$

We always get tails. This is possible!

$\omega = (T, T, T, \ldots)$
$$\lim_{n \rightarrow \infty}  \frac{X_1(\omega') + X_2(\omega') +\ldots + X_n(\omega')}{n} = 0 $$

Each $w$ is an infinite number of coin flips. The sample space has an infinite number of $\omega$, and an infinite number of those $\omega$ have an average of $\frac{1}{2}$. The law of large numbers says that $\mathbb{P}(A) = 1$.

If $\mathbb{E}X_1$ does not exist, the LLN is not necessarily true. 

If $X_1, X_2, \ldots$ are not independent, the LLN is not necessarily true. For example, if we set all of the random variables to a single random variable, the value will be 0 or 1, not $\frac{1}{2}$.

For each $i = 1,2, \ldots$

$$X_i(w) = X(w) \text{ for all } \omega \in \Omega$$
$$X_1 = X_2 = X_3 = \ldots = X$$
$$A = \{\omega \in \Omega: \lim_{n \rightarrow \infty}  \frac{X_1(\omega) + X_2(\omega) +\ldots + X_n(\omega)}{n} = \frac{1}{2}\}$$

The LLN anticipates $\mathbb{P}(A) = 1$.
However, $\mathbb{P}(A) = \mathbb{P}(X = \frac{1}{2}) = \mathbb{P}(\emptyset) = 0$


### Monte Carlo Integration

Let $X$ be a continuous RV with PDF $p_X(x)$ and $g(x)$ as a real valued function. Then we have

$$\mathbb{E}[g(X)] = \int_{-\infty}^\infty g(x) p_X(x) dx $$

$$\text{Var} [g(X)] = \int_{-\infty}^\infty (g(x) - \mathbb{E}[p_X(x)])^2 p_X(x) dx $$

For example, let $X$ ~ Unif(0,1) and $$g(x) = \arccos(\frac{\cos\frac{\pi}{2}x}{1 + 2\cos(\frac{\pi}{2}x)})$$

Then we have $\mathbb{E}[g(X)] = \int_{-\infty}^\infty g(x) \mathbf{1}_{(0,1)}(x)dx = \int_{0}^{1} g(x) dx$ = $\frac{5\pi}{2
}$. This can be found by doing $X$ many times and doing $g(x)$ of it.


### Generalized LLN

Suppose $X_1, X_2, \ldots$ be an infinite sequence of random variables. They are independently and identically distributed, and $g(x)$ is a continuous function. Furthermore that $\mathbb{E}[g(X_1)]$ exists. 

Define 

$$A = \{\omega \in \Omega: \lim_{n \rightarrow \infty}  \frac{g(X_1(\omega)) + g(X_2(\omega)) +\ldots + g(X_n(\omega))}{n} = \mathbb{E}[g(X_1)]\}$$

Then $\mathbb{P}(A) = 1$. More intuitively: the number of infinite sequences in $\Omega$, that have an expected value of $\mathbb{E}[g(X_1)]$, approaches all of the infinite series. 

The error $|e_n(w)|$ (the difference between $\bar X_n$ and $E[g(X)]$) is likely to be smaller than 

$$\sqrt{\text{Var}[g(X_1)] \frac{2\ln(\ln(n))}{n}}$$

This is known as the law of the iterated logarithm.

### Review of the LLN

Let $X_1, X_2, \ldots $ be i.i.d. and their expected values exist. When $n$ is large, 

$$\bar X_n(\omega) = \frac{X_1(\omega) + \ldots + X_n(\omega)}{n} \approx \mathbb{E}X_1 = \mathbb{E}X_2 = \ldots$$

Unless you are extremely unlucky.

### Law of the Iterated Logarithm

The law of the iterated logarithm is a result about a logarithm function involving $Var(X_1)$, stating that it is a good bound for the error.

Let $X_1, X_2, X_3, \ldots$ be RVs defined on $(\Omega, \mathbb{P})$. Suppose

- $X_1, X_2, X_3, \ldots$ are independently and identically distributed.
- $\mathbb{E}X_1$ and $Var(X_1)$ exist.

Intuitively, when $n$ is large, 
$$|e_n(\omega)| \leq \sqrt{Var(X_1) \frac{2\log(\log(n))}{n}}$$
is true for almost all $\omega$ in $\Omega$, so that $\mathbb{P}$ of these $\omega$ approaches 1.

Intuitively, when $n$ is large, 
$$|e_n(\omega)| > \sqrt{Var(X_1) \frac{2\log(\log(n))}{n}}$$
is true for almost no $\omega$ in $\Omega$, so that $\mathbb{P}$ of these $\omega$ approach 0.

### Law of the Iterated Logarithm (rigorous)

$$\lim_{m \rightarrow \infty}[\sup_{n \geq m}(\frac{|e_n(\omega)|}{\sqrt{Var(X_1) \frac{2\log(\log(n))}{n}}})] = 1$$

is true for almost all $w$ in $\Omega$, so that $\mathbb{P}$ of these $\omega$ approaches 1.

### An example: quantifying the error of an Monte Carlo integration

$U_1, U_2, U_3, \ldots  \sim $ Unif(0,1)

$$g(x) = \arccos(\frac{\cos\frac{\pi}{2}x}{1 + 2\cos(\frac{\pi}{2}x)})$$

$$\frac{g(U_1(\omega)) + \ldots + g(U_n(\omega))}{n} \approx \mathbb{E}[g(U)] = \int_{0}^{1} \arccos(\frac{\cos\frac{\pi}{2}x}{1 + 2\cos(\frac{\pi}{2}x)}) dx $$

$X_i(\omega) = g(U_i(\omega))$ for all $i$ and $\omega$

So that we have

$$\bar{X_n}(\omega) = \frac{X_1(\omega) + \ldots + X_n(\omega)}{n} \approx \mathbb{E}X_1 = \mathbb{E}[g(U)]$$

$$e_n(\omega) = \bar{X_n}(\omega) - \mathbb{E}X_1$$

Let's say that we want the error to be no higher than $10^{-5}$. Then we would have

$$|e_n(\omega)| \leq \sqrt{Var(X_1) \frac{2\log(\log(n))}{n}}\leq 10^{-5}$$

$$\sqrt{0.007556 \frac{2\log(\log(n))}{n}}\leq 10^{-5}$$

For error of $10^{-3}$ this would be n = 1.5 million!

### Random Walks

We have 
$$\bar{X_n}(\omega) = \frac{X_1(\omega) + \ldots + X_n(\omega)}{n}$$

But we could also look at 
$$\bar{S_n}(\omega) = X_1(\omega) + \ldots + X_n(\omega)$$

This is a random walk!

Let $\{X_i\}_{i=1}^{\infty}$ be a sequence of RV defined on $(\Omega, \mathbb{P})$ and with $X_i$ i.i.d. For each positive $n$, we define $S_n(\omega) = X_1(\omega) + \ldots + X_n(\omega)$. The sequence $\{X_i\}_{i=1}^{\infty}$ is the random walk.

Example 1: 
$$\mathbb{P}(X_1 = -1) = \mathbb{P}(X_1 = 1) = \frac{1}{2}$$

Then $\{X_i\}_{i=1}^{\infty}$ is called the 1-dim simple RW.


### Central Limit Theorem: prelude

Review:

Let $\{X_i\}_{i=1}^{\infty}$ be a sequence of i.i.d. RV on $(\Omega, \mathbb{P})$. We have a sum 
$$ S_n(\omega) = X_1(\omega) + X_2(\omega)  + \ldots + X_n(\omega)$$

So $S_n(\omega)$ is a random walk. If we go one step further, we can take
$$\bar{X_k}(\omega) = \frac{S_n(\omega)}{n} \approx \mathbb{E}X_1$$
The average is only equal to the expected value $\mathbb{E}X$ when $n$ is large.

Additionally, we have the law of the iterated logarithm.

Now we will say the central limit theorem.

$$e_n = |\bar{X_n} - \mathbb{E}X|$$

$$\sqrt{n} \cdot e_n(\omega) \sim N(0, \text{Var } X_1)$$

when $n$ is large.

Potential applications of CLT:

- It's a different way of quantifying $e_n(\omega)$
- formulation of "hypothesis testing" and "confidence intervals" in statistics.

Okay, now we will define it in full.

### Central Limit Theorem

Let $\{X_i\}_{i=1}^{\infty}$ be a sequence of i.i.d. RVs on $(\Omega, \mathbb{P})$. Suppose $\mathbb{E}X_1$ and $\text{Var }X$ exist. We define a sequence of RVs $\{G_n(\omega)\}_{n=1}^{\infty}$ by 

$$G_n(\omega) = \sqrt{n} \cdot e_n(\omega) = \sqrt{n} (\bar{X_n} - \mathbb{E}X)$$

Heuristic version: when $n$ is large, $G_n$ looks like a RV following $N(0, \text{Var}X_1)$. 

Rigorous version: The CDF of $G_n$ converges to the CDF of $N(0, \text{Var}X_1)$ as $n \rightarrow \infty$:
$$\lim_{n \rightarrow \infty} \mathbb{P}(\{\omega \in \Omega: G_n(\omega) \leq x\}) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \cdot \text{Var} X_1}} \cdot \exp(-\frac{t^2}{2VarX_1}) dt$$

Under the conditions of the CLT, we have the following.
Heuristic version: 

$\frac{G_n}{\sqrt{\text{Var }{X_1}}}$ looks like a RV following $N(0,1)$ when $n$ is large.


Rigorous version: the CDF of $\frac{G_n(\omega)}{\text{Var }X_1}$ converges to the CDF of $N(0, 1)$.

This is as follows:

$$ \lim_{n \rightarrow \infty} \mathbb{P}(\{\omega \in \Omega: \frac{G_n(\omega)}{\sqrt{\text{Var } X_1}} \leq x \}) = \int_{-\infty}^x \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \cdot \exp(-\frac{t^2}{2})dt $$

### Review of CLT

Let $\{X_i\}_{i=1}^{\infty}$ be a sequence of independent and identically distributed RVs,a nd the expected values and variances exist. We have

$$\sqrt{n}(\bar{X_n} - \mathbb{E}X_1) \sim N(0,\text{Var }X)$$
$$\sqrt{n}\frac{(\bar{X_n} - \mathbb{E}X_1)}{\sqrt{\text{Var }X_1}} \sim N(0,1)$$

When $n$ is large.

### LLN Approximation Error, from the CLT Viewpoint

We have two quantities, the average $\bar{X_n}(\omega)$ and the expected value $\mathbb{E}X_1$. 
We have

$$e_n(\omega) = \bar{X_n}(\omega) - \mathbb{E}X_1$$

We also have

$$e_n(\omega) = \frac{\sqrt{\text{Var }X_1}}{\sqrt{n}} Z_n(\omega)$$

For any $\delta \geq 0$:

$$\mathbb{P}(\{\omega \in \Omega: | e_n(\omega) | \leq \delta \frac{\sqrt{\text{Var }X_1}}{\sqrt{n}}\})$$
$$ = \mathbb{P}(\{\omega \in \Omega: | Z_n(\omega) | \leq \delta \})$$
$$ = F_{Z_n}(\delta) - F_{Z_n}(-\delta) + \mathbb{P}(Z_n = -\delta)$$
$$ = \mathbb{P}( -\delta \leq Z_n \leq -\delta) + \mathbb{P}(Z_n = -\delta)$$

We have $\Phi(x)$ to be the CDF of $N(0,1)$:
$$\lim_{x \rightarrow \infty} F_{Z_n}(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp(-\frac{t^2}{2}) dt = \Phi(x)$$

We have the following simplification:
$$= F_{Z_n}(\delta) - F_{Z_n}(-\delta) + \mathbb{P}(Z_n = -\delta)$$
$$\approx \Phi(\delta) - \Phi(-\delta)$$
$$\approx 2 \Phi(\delta) - 1$$

If we have $\Phi(\delta^{*}) = 0.975$, then we have $e_n(\omega) = 0.95$.
$$\delta^{*} = 1.96$$
This requires lots of computation.

$$\mathbb{P}(\{\omega \in \Omega: | e_n(\omega) | \leq \delta^{*} \frac{\sqrt{\text{Var }X_1}}{\sqrt{n}}\}) \approx 2\Phi(\delta) - 1 = 0.95$$

With 95% confidence, the approximation error $| e_n(\omega) |$ is smaller than or equal to $$| e_n(\omega) | \leq \delta^{*} \frac{\sqrt{\text{Var }X_1}}{\sqrt{n}}$$

Law of the iterated logarithm: we had
$$\mathbb{P}(\{\omega \in \Omega: |e_n(\omega)| \leq \sqrt{2\log\log n} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\text{ Var } X_1}}{\sqrt{n}} \}) \approx 1$$

With 100% confidence we have
$$|e_n(\omega)| \leq \sqrt{2\log\log n} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\text{ Var } X_1}}{\sqrt{n}}$$ 
When $n$ is large! 

When n > 1000, $\sqrt{2\log\log n} > 1.96 \approx \delta^{*}$

We can get a perfect upper bound. However, this upper bound is too large. However, with the central limit theorem, with the cost of 5% confidence, we can get a lower upper bound.

### Proof of Central Limit Theorem

Let $G_1, G_2, \ldots$ be a sequence of RVs.
We say $G_n$ converges weakly to a continuous RV $G$ if

$$\lim_{n \rightarrow \infty} F_{G_n}(x) = F_{G}(x)$$

for all real numbers $x$.

This is briefly denoted as $G_n \rightarrow_{w} G$.

Let $X_1, X_2, \ldots$ be i.i.d. RVs whose expected values and variances exist. We can define a error-like function called $G_n$ that is within a normal distribution:

- $G_n(\omega) = \sqrt{n}(\bar{X_n}(\omega) - \mathbb{E}X_1)$
- $G \sim N(0, Var X_1)$

$$\lim_{n \rightarrow \infty} F_{G_n}(x) = \text{the CDF of N}(0, Var X_1) = F_{G}(x)$$

### Moment-generating Functions

Used to describe a probability function.

Let $X$ be a RV.

We define a moment-generating function as 

$$M_X(t) = \mathbb{E}[e^{t\cdot X}]$$

provided that the expected value exists for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$.

$$e^{tX} = 1 + tX + \frac{t^2 X^2}{2!} + \frac{t^3 X^3}{3!} \ldots$$

We have the expected value of a sum involving a random variable to be the sum of terms involving an expected value of a random variable:

$$\mathbb{E}[\sum_{j=1}^{J}c_jg_j(X)] = \sum_{j=1}^{J}c_j \mathbb{E}[g_j(X)]$$

Applying this to our M_X^{t} we have
$$M_X^{t} = \mathbb{E}[e^{t\cdot X}] = 1 + t \mathbb{E}X + \frac{t^2 \mathbb{E}[X^2]}{2!} + \frac{t^3 \mathbb{E}[X^3]}{3!} \ldots $$

So then our derivative is 
$$ \frac{d}{dt} M_X(t) = \mathbb{E}X + t\mathbb{E}[X^2] + \frac{t^2}{2!}\mathbb{E}[X^3] + \ldots $$

At t = 0, our first moment is 
$$ \frac{d}{dt} M_X(t) = \mathbb{E}X $$

Our second moment is 
$$ \frac{d^2}{dt^2} M_X(t) = \mathbb{E}[X^2] $$

Our $k^{th}$ moment is 
$$ \frac{d^k}{dt^k} M_X(t) = \mathbb{E}[X^k] $$

### Theorem

Let $G, G_1, G_2, \ldots $ be a sequence of RVs.

Recall that 
$$M_{G_n}(t) = \mathbb{E}[e^{t G_n}]$$
$$\mathbb{E}[e^{t G_n}] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{tx}p_X(x) dx$$

If $\lim_{n \rightarrow \infty} M_{G_n}(t) = M_G(t)$:

Then $$G_n \rightarrow_{w} G$$
If we have a convergence of the moment functions, then we have a convergence of the random variables!

### Theorem 

Let $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ be RVs.
$$S_n(\omega) = X_1(\omega) + X_2(\omega) + \ldots + X_n(\omega) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}X_i(w)$$

If $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ are independent:

$$M_{S_n(t)} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} M_{X_i}(t)$$

Furthermore, if $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ are independently distributed:

$$M_{S_n}(t) = (M_{X_1}(t))^n$$

### Two more lemmas

Lemma 1: Let $\{c_n\}_{n=1}^{\infty}$ be a sequence of real numbers satisfying $\lim_{n \rightarrow \infty} c_n$ = 0. If $\lim_{n \rightarrow \infty}c_n = \lambda$, then 
$$ \lim_{n \rightarrow \infty} (1 + c_n) = ?$$

Lemma 2:

If $G \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$. Then $$M_G(t) = e^{\frac{t^2\sigma^2}{2}}$$


## Proof of CLT

$$G_n = \sqrt{n}[(\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n X_1) - \mathbb{E}X_1]$$

We move the average to the outside of the sum:

$$G_n = \frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}[\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i - \mathbb{E}X_1)
]$$

Given this fact, we can replace $G_n$ in our moment:

$$M_{G_n(t)} = \mathbb{E}[e^{tG_n}]$$
$$M_{G_n(t)} = \mathbb{E}[e^{\frac{t}{\sqrt{n}} \sum_{i=1}^n (X_i - \mathbb{E}X_1)}]$$

Because our random variables are i.i.d:

$$M_{G_n(t)} = \mathbb{E}[e^{\sum_{i=1}^n \frac{t}{\sqrt{n}} (X_i - \mathbb{E}X_1)}]$$

$$M_{G_n(t)} = (\mathbb{E}[e^{\frac{t}{\sqrt{n}} (X_i - \mathbb{E}X_1)}])^n$$

$$M_{G_n(t)} = ((\mathbb{E}[1 + \frac{t}{\sqrt{n}}(X_1 - \mathbb{E}X) + \frac{t^2 (X_1 - \mathbb{E}X)^2}{2n} + \ldots + \sum)^n$$

$$M_{G_n(t)} = (1 + \frac{t}{\sqrt{n}}(0) + \frac{t^2 (X_1 - \mathbb{E}X)^2}{2n} + \ldots + \sum)^n$$

$$\approx (1 + \frac{t^2}{2n} \text{Var }X_1)^n$$
$$\approx (1 + c_n)^n$$

$$M_{G_n(t)} \approx (1 + c_n)^n \rightarrow e^{\frac{t^2}{2} \text{Var }X_1}$$

This is the Moment generating function for $N(0, \text{Var }(X_1))$.

Thus, our function converges:

$$\lim_{n \rightarrow \infty} F_{G_n}(x) = \text{the CDF of N}(0, Var X_1) = F_{G}(x)$$

## Random Vectors

We have a vector $\bar{X}$ = $(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n )^T$ as a *random vector* defined on $(\Omega, \mathbb{P})$ if each of its components is a RV defined on 
$(\Omega, \mathbb{P})$. 

The CDF of $\bar{X}$ is an n-variable function
$$F_{\bar{X}}(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n) = \mathbb{P}(\{\omega \in \Omega: X_1(\omega) \leq x_1, \ldots, X_1(\omega) \leq x_n\})$$
$$= \mathbb{P}(\cap_{i=1}^n \{\omega \in \Omega: X_i(\omega) \leq x_i\})$$

$\bar{X}$ = $(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n )$ is said to be a continuous vector (continuous random variable, if length is 1) if its CDF $F_{\bar{x}}$ is differentiable (the derivative $p_X(x)$ exists everywhere on cumulative distribution function for $\bar{x}$)

The PDF of $\bar{X}$ is defined by
$$p_{\bar{X}}(x_1, \ldots, x_n) = \frac{\partial}{\partial x_1} \frac{\partial}{\partial x_2} \ldots \frac{\partial}{\partial x_n} F_{\bar{X}}(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$$

for $n = 2$:

$$p_{\bar{x}}(x_1, x_2) = \frac{\partial}{\partial x_1}\frac{\partial}{\partial x_2} F_{\bar{x}}(x_1, x_2) $$

The CDF for a continuous vector is hard to derive. The PDF is easier. Here is an example.

$$p_X(x_1, x_2) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \exp\{\frac{x_1^2 + x_2^2}{2}\}$$

Let $\bar{X} = (X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n)$ be a continuous random vector with PDF $p_{\bar{x}}(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$. Suppose $g(x_1, \ldots, x_n)$ is an n-variable function. 

$$\mathbb{E}[g(\bar{X})] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} g(x_1, \ldots, x_n) \cdot p_{\bar{x}}(x_1, \ldots, x_n)dx_1 dx_2 \ldots dx_n$$

If 

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} |g(x_1, \ldots, x_n)| \cdot p_{\bar{x}}(x_1, \ldots, x_n)dx_1 dx_2 \ldots dx_n < +\infty$$

We may consider the CDFs independently. Let $\bar{X} = (X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n)$ be a random vector. If $X_1, X_2, \ldots X_n$ are independent, then

(i) $$F_{\bar{X}}(x_1, \ldots, x_n) = \prod_{i}^n F_{X_i}(x_i)$$

So for a set of independent criteria on a population, the probability is the same as the product of each criteria!

Furthermore, if $\bar{X}$ is a continuous random vector, we can just go ahead and sum the individual probabilities!

(ii)$$p_{\bar{X}}(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n) = \prod_{i=1}^n p_{X_i}(x_i)$$

Here is a proof for (ii) based on (i):

$$p_{\bar{X}}(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n) = \frac{\partial}{\partial x_1} \frac{\partial}{\partial x_2} \ldots \frac{\partial}{\partial x_n} \prod_{i}^n F_{X_i}(x_i)$$

Because we hold every other $x$ constant when doing one derivative we have
$$= \prod_{i=1}^n \frac{\partial}{\partial x_i} F_{x_i}(x) = \prod_{i=1}^n p_{X_i}(x_i)$$

### Theorem
$X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ are i.i.d. RVs.

Define $S_n = \sum_{i=1}^nX_i$.

Then $M_{S_n}(t) = (M_{X_1}(t))^n$

Proof: To simplify the proof, we assume $\bar{X}$ is continuous. 
By definition:
$$M_{S_n}(t) = \mathbb{E}[e^{tS_n}]$$
$$ = \mathbb{E}[e^{tX_1 + tX_2 + \ldots + tX_n}]$$
Because addition in exponents is multiplication:
$$ = \mathbb{E}[\prod_{i=1}^{n}e^{tX_i}]$$

By definition of expected value:

$$ = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (\prod_{i=1}^{n}e^{tX_i})(\prod_{i=1}^{n}p_{X_i}(x_i)) dx_1 \ldots dx_n$$

$$ = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (\prod_{i=1}^{n}e^{tX_i} p_{X_i}(x_i)) dx_1 \ldots dx_n $$

$$ = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \ldots \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (\prod_{i=1}^{n}e^{tX_i} p_{X_i}(x_1)) dx_1) dx_2 \ldots dx_n$$

By definition of expected value:

$$ = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \ldots \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \mathbb{E}[e^{tX_1}] dx_2 \ldots dx_n$$

By definiton of moment:

$$ = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \ldots \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} M_{X_1}(t) dx_2 \ldots dx_n$$

$$ = M_{X_n}(t) \ldots M_{X_2}(t) M_{X_1}(t)$$

But all $M_{X_i} = M_{X_1}$, so we have

$$(M_{X_1(t)})^n$$


## Data, Models, and Inference

## Data
Before performing the experiment, we do not know the outcome. So we consider
$$\Omega = \{ \text{all possible outcomes} \}$$

Example: flip an unfair coin $n$ times. 
Then we have 
$$\Omega = \{\omega = (\omega^{(i)}, \ldots, \omega^{(n)})\}$$

Each $w^{(i)}$ is either $H$ or $T$. 

$X_1, X_2, \ldots X_n$ are functions defined on $\Omega$, and we know these functions. For example

$$X_i(\omega) = 1 \text { if } \omega^{(i)} = H$$
$$X_i(\omega) = 0 \text { if } \omega^{(i)} = T$$

Performing the experiment is equivalent to picking and fixing $\omega^* \in \Omega$. That is a fixed sequence like $(H,H,T, \ldots)$.

For the fixed $\omega^*$ we can define a collection $\{x_i\}_{i=1}^n$ of deterministic numbers by 
$$x_i = X_i(\omega^*)$$
for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$. This is like a single outcome.

The collection $\{x_i\}_{i=1}^{n}$ is called sample (data), and the $n$ is called the sample size. For example

$$\omega^{*} = (H, H, T, H, \ldots, T)$$
$$x^{*} = (1, 1, 0, 1, \ldots, 0)$$

## Models
Let $\mathcal{F} = \{F_\theta\}_\theta \in H$ be a family of real-valued functions satisfying the "CDF properties". That is, F(x) is non-decreasing. The limit of F(x) as x goes to 0 is 0, the limit of F(x) as x goes to infinity is 1, and F(x) is right-continuous.

The $\mathcal{F}$-based model is an assumption:

$\exist \theta^* \in \Theta$ and a probability $\mathbb{P}$ defined on $\Omega$ such that $$X_1, X_2, \ldots X_n \sim F_{\theta^*}(x) = \mathbb{P}(\{\omega \in \Omega: X_1(\omega) \leq x \}$$

If $\Theta$ is a subset of a finite-dimensional space, then the model is called a parametric model.

If $\Theta$ is a subset of an infinite-dimensional space, the model is called a nonparametric model.

One example. If $\Theta$ is the functions $\theta$ on (0, 1) such that 
$$\int_0^1 |\theta(x)|^2 dx < +\infty$$
$$\int_0^1 |\theta(x)'|^2 dx < +\infty$$

Smoothing splines.

## Statistic Inference

$\theta^*$ is unknown.

title: proofwriting_journal_10
subject: algorithms
contentHtml:
The most important things I've learned is how to see proof structures and sets more viscerally. I heavily rely on visual intuition and while it is not essential for a proof, and it reinforces my ability to manipulate something when it is drawn in some form on the page. This might be because my spatial intuition is worse than many people, so that I need to actually see it to work with it. 

Overall, I feel comfortable with employing direct proof, contrapositive, and proof by contradiction. I think there will still be some challenge in knowing when to use each of these in new domains of math, but I think I will improve as time goes on. 

I have generally found feedback to be more helpful while I am still working on problem sets than after I have written it. This is because I can defend myself and realize my own mistakes. I have gotten somewhat contradictory responses from peer reviewers, but I still try to reread my proof and correct mistakes where possible. 

I think I have gotten a lot better at proofs by definition and built a lot more intuition around induction. I've been very happy with the speed at which I can typeset in LaTex; I write my statistics notes in LaTex during lecture now.

title: instrumental
subject: linguistics
contentHtml:
Some things are desirable because of what they will bring -- that is, they are instrumental.
- Money
- Living on the East Coast
- A high-speed internet connection

Other things are desirable in-and-of-themselves: that is, there is something implicit to them that is important

- Happiness
- Respect of colleagues
- Preventing suffering

Wanting de dicto and de re
is it: Mikayla wants to meet the world's best cellist.

- de re: x is the world's best cellist, Mikayla wants to meet x
- de dicto: Mikayla wants: (x is the world's best cellist and Mikayla meets x). This is more superficial.

- The direct path to happiness

Example of why de re makes more sense than de dicto: Tom Sawyer decided to do what was right de re by (emotional ?)motivation, but his conception of what was wrong was entirely de dicto. Maybe this bridges the gap between natural and moral properties?

Maybe the law is meant to be a way of enforcing rational behavior that is also good (?). Maybe if you follow the law de dicto you are doing the right thing, most of the time.

Good for a person: we can look at their subjective interests. Where we are authoritative, but not omniscient. Once you imagine what it is like...

Subjective interests: the things which you believe are good for you. 

Objective interests: the things that are actually good for you.

peter: think fair is waste of time
peter: doesn't want to go
peter+: knows fair is informative
peter+: doesn't want to go
peter+: wants peter to go
