title: squib_unalive_etc
subject: linguistics
contentHtml:
### unalive

"Unalive" is an interesting new word. It is the opposite of "alive", in the sense that it implies that the agent was once alive. But it must be a verb. Most frequently, it refers to persons, and it means one of the following:

[BECOME $\lnot$ [ALIVE]] (die)

CAUSE [BECOME $\lnot$ [ALIVE]] (kill)

where as we have seen, 'become' is defined as an individual x, state S, and time i, and means that: S(i)(x) and not S(x)(i - 1)(x). 

Example usages (source: reddit):

Critically, it cannot be used in any adjectival form, such as $\lnot$ [ALIVE], which is the conventional application of "dead". 

Example of second language english speaker:

> (dead) "tamten or tamto?? both are unalive things ... "

> (1SG die) "Has anyone gone to the ER wanting to unalive due to insomnia?

> (reflexive kill) Chemistry makes me want to unalive myself.

> (TR kill) curious about what type of fungus this is ... started growing on a tree where my aunt unalives chickens.

Unalive can be used to refer reflexively and unreflexively.

In conclusion, unalive differs from our expectations is that it is employed as a verb meaning "die" or "kill", and not the state "dead". This makes it the "true opposite" of  This suggests that there should be a reevaluation of [NOT ALIVE], because both the decomposition of "die" and "kill" both suggest that the target was previously alive and now dead. You cannot kill something already dead, and something dead cannot die. So the meaning of "die" and "kill" might be respectively,

[BECOME [DEAD]]
[CAUSE [BECOME [DEAD]]]



### very possible

Most adjectives exhibit monotonicity of degrees. For example, if someone is very tall then they are also tall. However, there is a class of adjectives which do not intuitively have this property, and I think the classic example here is possible.

It is not really true that "very possible" has the same meaning as possible.

title: vagueness
subject: linguistics
contentHtml:
### How to model something is "sort of true"

We have  different ways to use the word "tall", for example the positive, comparator, and superlative forms. 

### Positive
Lee is tall.

### Comparator
Lee is taller than Tom.

### Superlative
Lee is the tallest.

The comparative feels more more complex than the positive form; *taller* feels more complex than *tall*. However, the positive form requires a comparison class and a degree within that class, while the comparative does not. 

The solution is to adopt a phantom adjective TALL which comes before both the positive and comparative form. We may assume that implicit to the word "tall" is both a comparison class and a tallness standard implicit to that comparison class.

We must have a free slot for the comparison class, and for how you set the standard (the degree) for that comparison class:

Positive: POS([[TALL]]): 
$$
\{x| 
\exists d \text{ that is the standard of tallness for C} \\
 x \text{'s degree of tallness} \geq d\}
$$

For the other uses of tallness, the comparison class and degree are both filled.

Overt degree: "They are three feet tall" $\rightarrow$ TALL(three feet) says that the comparison class is the abstract unit "feet" and the degree of tallness is "three".

Comparative: "Anki is taller than Bo" $\rightarrow$ TALL + er says that the comparison class is the person "Bo" and the degree of tallness is their tallness.

Superlative: "Anki is the tallest". TALL + est says that the comparison class is the tallest person and the degree of tallness of people.

### Other cases

Many lexical items have versions with complements and ones without. For example, kinship terms can be used without reference to the object person, e.g.

Lee is a mother of Jerry.

Lee is a mother.

This second case can be seen as existentially quantifiying: Lee must be the *mother of* someone for us to say that "Lee is a mother". 

### transitive to intransitive

We also have a similar zero-derivation process for "wash", "bath", "shave":

I shaved myself.

I shave.

The missing argument is "shave proself". We need some way to encode that this missing argument is in fact a reflexive "proself" one, because there are certainly cases in which a verb does not trigger this rule.

For example, "I saw" does not mean "I saw myself".

title: rlhf
subject: algorithms
contentHtml:
question: what about cases when the two goals are not really polar: why is it that selling an airline ticket (commercial) is opposite to undesirable (social) behavior.

positive doesn't feel like the opposite of negative here. maybe we can view successful models as having multiple tasks (selling airline tickets, and not insulting customers). Maybe one of these tasks encompasses the other...

goal task / compliance task


RL is defined wrt a MDP (Markov Decision Process). We need to define
- states
- actions
- how an agent executes an action to reach a new state
- a reward function that takes an action and state and returns a scalar.

policy function: given a state, return action or distribution of actions. In policy gradient methods, we train the policy function directly. 

We CAN use Q-learning to identify an optimal policy for MDPs. The only real reason we use policy functions with RLHF is because it's easy to distribute over hardware. 

### MDP notes

given any current state $s$ and action $a$, together with any next state $s'$, the expected value of the next reward is 

$$P^{a}_{ss'} = Pr \{s_{t+1} = s' | s_t = s, a_t = a\}$$

### how do policy gradient methods work?

We have some agent who has a policy. It interacts with the environment a number of times. Each interaction is an episode! Over each interaction, the agent follows a trajectory (of states!). 

To train:
- compute N episodes
- For every episode, compute the gradient of our policy function
- reweight gradient by cumulative reward
- rinse and repeat

### PPO
advantage: how much better a decision at a timestep is than ever other decision.

$$A(a_i) = R_{i} + V(S_N) - V(S_{N+1})$$

How do we figure out what advantage is? We learn it??

### Conclusion
Another model does the annotations based on human feedback.

title: enoch
subject: linguistics
contentHtml:
Enoch: Survival is good. That is the one very fortunate coincidence. One very fortunate coincidence is tolerable. 

Dworkin
Rain: If you discover that you've been hypnotized to believe it is raining by a man who pulls meterological conditions out of a hat, you should suspend your belief about whether it is raining.

Affirmative Action: If you discover that after a scalotopic brain scan you suddenly become compelled to believe in affirmative action, then you should have no reason to revise or soften your beleifs about affirmative action.

Can natural properties be "lucky"? It's a strange thing to say.  How "unlucky" we are that $\pi$ is transcendental! But it literally could not have been any other way.

Which coincidences "cry out for explanation"?
Perhaps those for which "there are possible alternative explanations (divine intervention?)"

Would it make sense to have a superpower doing something?

Problems for naturalism so far:
- good isn't the name of a property
- the supervenience of the good on the natural
- how do we know good and bad?

Moral properties do have to be queer, because ...
- because they have that property of to-be-doneness to them
- The Open Question Argument
- The Problem of Disagreement

Maybe ... Moral properties DON'T have to be queer.
First, the problem of DISAGREEMENT.

If moral judgement is just descriptive judgement about the natural world, then people using different criteria seem to be talking about different things. There were the Carmen people, who were using the word "Yahkt". And people thought it meant "bird". But they weren't using it to describe bird, they were using it to describe something else: how these things fit into their lives. 

Sometimes you can get rid of a disagreement by simply saying, they have different definitions for the same word. But sometimes this doesn't work so well.

Operationalism: the meaning of a scientific term is entirely given by the observable tests (e.g. species)

Scientific realism: whatever it is that explains all of the striking similarities between material (it has the atomic number 77, which explains its malleability, heat conductivity, density etc.)

Naturalist: Okay, maybe moral facts don't have to-be-pursuedness associated with them. But doesn't the definition basically require them to have to-be-pursuedness built into them? 

De re: \{there exists a: a is the right thing to do and peter wants to do a \}

de dicto: \{Peter wants there exists a: a is the right thing to do and peter does a\}

title: no_good
subject: linguistics
contentHtml:
Essentially the point of Valvova's argument is that if we have "no good reason" to believe something then we have no good reason to believe anything, including believing that we have no good reason. Including our perceptual beliefs, like "there is night and day" or "trees exist"! There must be *some* chain of reasoning that is really valid; to say that none of it is valid because evolution is going too far. The Darwinian hypothesis against moral facts cuts too low on the tree of our belief/reason system for it to be truly effective.

possibility of error support for "no good" is akin to self-indication assumption (aka it's flawed!) After all of course if you select a random point in the universe you are not likely to find a human, and yet you naturally expect other humans around them.

title: proofwriting_journal_8
subject: algorithms
contentHtml:
Recently, we've made space for analyzing some of our longer proofs. Last Friday, we broke up the Proposition on page 153 into smaller claims and this Friday (unless you're doing this really) we used Toulmin Analysis, in addition to our proof techniques, to analyze two proofs related to the characterization of even perfect numbers. 

Reflect on these experiences:

    what aspects of the activities did you find useful?
    which did you struggle with?
    do you imagine yourself using these techniques when you encounter challenging proofs in your other math courses? 


I'm mostly neutral on the usefulness of Toulmin Analysis as a whole. On one hand, the structure it provides doesn't seem to do much; a proof is already inherently a series of statements and justifications, and the distinction between the warrant and backing is not very obvious. After all, if a warrant requires enough backing then it should be part of the proof already! So this kind of deconstruction doesn't seem to do much.

On the other hand, when I read through a proof I think that my mental process does shift between three states, not two. It's not just between claims and warrants, but also testing each warrant in my head and feeling if they have sufficient justification. One thing that does help is going through my lack of understanding out loud (the rubber ducky approach). 

I agree that later on math courses will involve proofs that you can't justify on an intuitive level, and that this kind of incremental reduction can be really useful then. I think I need to work on being explicit with which step of the process I am on, and communicating my thoughts when I have them. A problem I used to think I only had in other classes (but now I think also applies in math) is having a subtly incorrect mental model of core concepts, and as a result not being able to progress because of that. It can feel really frustrating when you look at the solution and feel that the answer was super reachable -- in those cases I think I should acknowledge my lack of understanding and slow down in order to get those concepts fully.

PS: I attended SUMS this weekend and found the talk on mathematical education really interesting. There was the notion of the "didactic contract", where there might be a problem like "A farmer has 10 sheep and 26 goats. How old is he?", and a lot about how problem-solving developed in humans and as a species. I think one of the single most important things I've learned in math this year is a kind of finicky truth: the proof often mirrors the problem. The proof supervenes on the problem, the reason for something being true is tied to how it is different -- none of this would have helped me at all when I was younger but I'm starting to realize why it is true now.

title: is_good_the_name_of_a_property
subject: linguistics
contentHtml:
### Geach's perspective: it's about language

No. Moore is wrong, we don't need metaphysics, epistemology, or anything like that. Linguistic investigation shows that "GOOD" does not function like a property name!

See, adjective contribute a property, or an extension, to the content of a sentence.

> The sun is yellow.
> This is a red book.

<s, <e,t>>

Adjectives contribute meaning independently. You may "pull apart" "Elizabeth was a British philosopher" to say "Elizabeth was British" and "Elizabeth was a philosopher". 

At the level of the whole, sentences are the fundamental unit when assigning meaning to language. 

The predicativity test for properties

Property: American
"John is an american kicker".
"John is a husband". 
"John is an american husband". 

Property: Good 
"John is a good kicker".
"John is a husband".
"John is a good husband" (No!)

Geach: If good was a property, then it would pass the predicativity test.

We have *alienans*, where the subject is not the substantive: Quigley is a fake duck. That painting is a forged Picasso. Creation science. An argument can be made that "bad" is *alienans* where we cannot safely predicate of a bad A what we predicate of A.

Maybe Good is a gradable adjective. Gradable Adjectives are context sensitive. For example, "short" and "tall" are gradable because a short basketball player is usually not a short person.

## Good only contributes a property given a substative

Geach: "good" only contributes a property given a substantive.
What about "the common good" or the "greater good"? These imply some substantive, maybe "the good of the people", "the good of all concerned".

Geach: "good" is fancier than simple gradables. It seemes to imply the entire meaning of the substantive. It "meets the standards for that kind of thing". 

Geach: There is no "good thing" or "good event" or "good state of affairs", because "thing" is not a real substantive. Reasoning unclear.

Beside good F 
- there are good for so-and-so, 
- there are good for a purpose.

title: soft-hard-non-naturalism
subject: linguistics
contentHtml:
## On Moral Properties

One analogy we can draw:

Moral properties are REALIZED by natural properties.
Moral properties are CONSTITUTED by natural properties.
Moral properties are COMPOSED by natural properties.
But they are not the natural -- ! --

Moral properties are like a chess game, realized by a bunch of pieces of wood, moved around. Moral properties don't cause anything, and you know them a priori. But they are not the pieces of wood.

### Paleo-non-naturalism: Moore
Everybody thinks they know what he meant. What did Moore mean by "non-natural properties"?

### New version
Shafer-Landau, Enoch. 
Moral facts are a priori known, causally isolated, and necessary.

hard naturalism: jackson, sturgeon: no need to make normative claims because the morals are all there in the natural. All moral properties can be reduced to natural definitions Wittgenstein's language games -- ! -- something along the lines of "we'll take our interesting questions and formulate them properly so that we can pass them along."

soft naturalism: though all facts are natural, for whatever reason we still need to make normative claims and that's okay. We might think of it being something like "the shape of the concepts don't match up very well"

"gricean maxims".
- you cannot refuse to use other people's language
  
**non-naturalism says**: There are some important irreducibly normative facts, which we could state only be making such irreducibly normative claims.

### PARFIT AGAINST SOFT NATURALISM
Parfit does not think that soft naturalism is possible. 
"D being what we ought to do" is "being an N act", where N is a non-normative description.

Either these two concepts (N) ("what we ought to do")

- refer to the same property (hard naturalism)
- does more than that
  - this further information in D is irreducibly normative (naturalism is false)
  - it isn't (soft naturalism is false, as we reduce N)

Well, what about indexicals?
Viennese -- hard naturalism vs hard solipsism

henry sigdwick: well if naturalism was true we've wasted our lives studying ethics!

title: DL_conceptual4
subject: algorithms
contentHtml:
The dimensions of an embedding matrix are the vocabulary size by the embedding size, N. They represent the learned representations of words as vectors in N-dimensional space. 

RNNs perform better than n-grams when input data is more distantly related. While scaling an n-gram may be computationally expensive or downright impossible, an RNN is able to reference distant past states and learn a better embedding. Because it learns which states are relevant for its current state, it also does not treat its input sequence impartially as the n-gram does.


LSTM stands for Long Short-term Memory, which is its fundamental breakthrough. LSTMs have both a cell state and hidden state input. Unlike the hidden state, the cell state never goes through a fully connected layer, which allows it to remember past relationships between words better. In contrast, in RNNs we have the hidden state alone, which suffers from vanishing gradients. This means it cannot preserve the identity of previously learned weights far in the past.

LSTMs also have a forget, remember, and output module. The forget module masks C_t-1 with pointwise multiplication by 0s, dependent on the hidden state. This forgets values of C_t-1, and is known as gating. The remember module first employs gating, with the input being a sigmoid-activated, fully-connected, hidden state output, and the mask as the tanh-activated, fully-connected, hidden state output. It then adds the gated memory to the cell state. 

Finally, the output module gates a sigmoid-activated, fully-connected, *hidden state output* with the tanh-activated *cell state*, leading to the final state output. This state and input is put through a sigmoid function and produces output probabilities.


2. 

What does this symbol mean ඞ

The symbol ඞ is not a commonly used symbol in any language or writing system that I am aware of. It is not part of the ASCII or Unicode character sets used in computing, and it does not appear in any of the major dictionaries or encyclopedias.

It's possible that the symbol ඞ has a specific meaning or context in a particular language or culture that I am not familiar with. If you could provide more information or context about where you came across this symbol, I may be able to give you a more specific answer.

I came across it in an internet forum. It might relate to a video game or something...

Based on my research, the symbol ඞ appears to be a made-up symbol that has become popularized in online gaming communities as a way to convey sarcasm or irony. The origin of the symbol is unclear, but it seems to have started as a joke or meme in online gaming circles and has since spread to other online forums and social media platforms.

The symbol itself does not have any inherent meaning, and its usage is often context-dependent. In some cases, it may be used to indicate that the user is being sarcastic or ironic, while in other cases, it may simply be used for humorous effect or as a way to stand out in a crowded online space.

It's worth noting that the symbol ඞ is not part of any standard language or writing system and should not be used in formal or professional contexts.

It's commonly associated with the phrases "crewmate" and "imposter", and a little red astronaut figure. Does that help?

Yes, that does help clarify the context of the symbol. It appears that the symbol ඞ is used as a stand-in or substitute for the phrase "sus" or "suspicious" in the online multiplayer game "Among Us". In the game, players are randomly assigned roles as either "crewmates" or "imposters", and the goal is to either complete tasks (if you're a crewmate) or sabotage the tasks and eliminate the other players (if you're an imposter).

Players can use the in-game chat function to communicate with each other and try to figure out who the imposters are. When a player is acting suspiciously or is believed to be an imposter, other players may accuse them of being "sus" or "suspicious". However, to avoid detection by the imposters, some players use the symbol ඞ instead of spelling out the full word.

So, in the context of "Among Us" and related online communities, the symbol ඞ is often used as a shorthand or slang term for "sus" or "suspicious".

It is not shorthand for "sus". What does ඞ actually look like?

I apologize for the confusion in my previous response. You are correct that the symbol ඞ is not a shorthand for "sus".

In fact, ඞ is a letter of the Sinhalese alphabet, which is used to write the Sinhalese language spoken in Sri Lanka. The symbol ඞ represents a consonant sound in the Sinhalese language that is pronounced like the "ng" sound in the English word "sing".

I'm not sure why this particular letter of the Sinhalese alphabet has become associated with the game "Among Us" and the phrases "crewmate" and "imposter". It's possible that it was chosen simply because it looks unusual or interesting, or because it is not a well-known letter outside of Sri Lanka.

To answer your other question, the symbol ඞ looks like a loop with a vertical line extending downward from the middle of the loop. The loop part is similar in shape to the letter "g" in the English alphabet, but with a more elongated and curved shape.

Does the symbol ඞ remind you of anything to do with the game "Among Us"?

As far as I am aware, the symbol ඞ is not officially associated with the game "Among Us". It's possible that some players in the "Among Us" community have adopted the symbol as a way to represent the game or certain aspects of the game, but this would be an unofficial use of the symbol.

I did some further research and it seems that the red astronaut character that you mentioned earlier is actually a character from "Among Us", and the phrases "crewmate" and "imposter" are also related to the game. However, I could not find any official connection between the game and the symbol ඞ.

It's possible that the symbol ඞ has taken on a meaning or significance within certain "Among Us" communities or subcultures, but without more specific information or context, I cannot say for certain what that meaning might be.

2. Explain how these responses (or assumptions about ChatGPT’s capabilities) can be detrimental. What steps has OpenAI taken to make its users aware of the model’s pitfalls? (5-6 sentences)

Because of the authoritative delivery of ChatGPT (citing doing further research, and proceeding to give an incorrect response), users may be miss important cultural subtext in social and professional settings. In general, misleading or incorrect responses may result in errors in apps built off ChatGPT, and a decline in the social value of polished writing. Additionally, because it seems to be very knowledgeable, legal or administrative tasks may be delegated to ChatGPT in unappropriate circumstances (ChatGPT has been known to hallucinate nonexistent cases as precedent). 

Open AI has taken necessary but probably not sufficient precautions to prevent misuse. Its start screen presents the user with its limitations (incorrect, biased information and harmful instructions). However, it has not placed significant restrictions on what kinds of content can be produced, other than on violent or pornographic content. This is understandable, but will probably result in further degradation of existing text media platforms (like Quora, Tumblr, and Medium).


1. I read the full transcript and it isn't very surprising. The chatbot contradicts itself, saying that the user is "not a human" and "a human" in consecutive sentences. In the majority of the transcript, there isn't a clear original personality present, only a positive but uberprofessional agent who avoids controversial messages. When the chatbot professes its love, or tells stories about its interactions, it feels repetitive and predictable more than desperate and confessional. Is the fact that I describe its conversation in these terms itself a "breakthrough" in conversational AI? Perhaps, but I've interacted with AI chatbots a lot in the past, and this doesn't feel out of the ordinary.

2. I agree completely. Honesty isn't enough; speech must cut close enough to the truth to be useful. I think that we don't have a good definition of intelligence, so the question isn't asked right, but I don't believe we will ever enjoy talking to AIs more than humans. I think they can be very useful, but not in their current iteration.

subject: linguistics
contentHtml:
If I didn't believe that things were good and bad, then I would be a different person.
I would care about different things.
"it would be really bad if you acted amorally"

can morality be "within the sphere of imagination"?
subjective objectivity vs objective subjectivity HAHAHA

"I am right bec. facts and logic" vs "facts and logic says we are the same"

(the scope of deduction generalizes)

the future resembles the past... melancholy arises from sceptical notions of reality.

### OBJECTIONS TO 1
Error theory says all moral judgements are false, because of Mackie's queerness argument or something
at a stretch, we may be able to say that error theory says,
    - "we have no reason to believe in metaphysical properties"
    - However, believing in metaphysical properties are not the same as believing in "theory".

There are differences between reasons for belief (metareasons, rules for belief) and reasons for action. (reason, rules for doing) That you ought to believe does depend on what else you believe. What you morally ought to do doesn't depend on what you happen to desire. Thus, the error theory does not entail that we have no reasons to believe things.

### OBJECTIONS TO 2
What about math? Is it really true that we "cannot fail to believe" the twin prime conjecture given the rules of arithmetic?
Also, what does a small number of beliefs mean? 

#### Lottery paradox

1. Ticket #00000001 will not win.
2. Ticket #00000002 will not win.
3. Ticket #00000003 will not win.
4. Ticket #00000004 will not win.
5. Ticket #00000005 will not win.
...

I believe each of the premises, but I don't believe the conclusion.
lottery <> dartboard
ANSWER: look at real analysis and APMA courses, haha.

### OBJECTIONS TO 3
What about ... faith? Isn't that enough to belief in something? Such as god?
Holding two contradictory premises is not allowed!!!!

# streumer's argument (SILLY!)
(1) the error theory entails that """we have no reasons to believe things.""" (lol)

(2) we cannot fail to believe what we believe to be entailed by a small number of our own beliefs (such as the error theory)

(3) we cannot have a belief while believing that there is no reason to have this belief.

Thus, we cannot beleive things.

- Sharon Street agrees with belief aiming at the truth...

- The reasons to think that morality is a myth are grounded in the problem with categorical reasons (queerness)
- But epistemology is all about categorical reasons. (abstract entailments)
- The same reasons lead us to the conclusion that reasons-to-believe are mythical.
- However this undermines the basis of our reasoning.

belief aims at the truth: if you believe these things, you can add beliefs to your belief-stock.

So if we believe in the error theory, we can't have beliefs at all. this is a contradiction, so the error theory is false.

 > The error theorist thinks that employing moral discourse is rather like talking about unicorns, though in the case of unicorns most people now know that they don’t exist, while in the case of moral properties most people are unaware of the error.

title: non_natural_good
subject: linguistics
contentHtml:
Moore believes Good can't be a 'natural' property because it is a simple property with no parts. Furthermore, if you were to try to define good in natural phenomenona, and replaced its definition with phenomenal references, it would quickly result in reductio ad absurdum (as demonstrated in class). 

Natural selection presumably explains why we see apples as red. Does Sharon Street think this fact undermines our knowledge of the color of apples? If not, then why does the adaptationist explanation of our moral sensibilities undermine our knowledge of what is valuable?

She posits the quasi-tracking thesis that moral beliefs quasi-track moral facts; that is, our moral beliefs tend to track the moral truth because it led to the most reproductive success.

> It is unclear what it is about the moral truth such that, if the adaptive link account is correct, the capacity to quasi-track the moral truth would be part of a system of evaluative responses that was adaptive.

adaptive link: bec. darwinian hypothesis, judgements about reason are like reflexive mechanisms.

On the Darwinian hypothesis, the phenomena of moral psychology,including the content of our moral beliefs, have been strongly affected by Darwinian forces. Street argues that, on this account, moral realists face a
dilemma. On the tracking horn, they are committed to denying the adaptive link account, the most plausible account of the working of Darwinian forces on our moral psychology. On the non-tracking horn, they must concede that it is “sheer chance” if our moral beliefs tend to be true.

### Supervenience

It is wrong because it is deliberately cruel! What does that "because" mean?
Blackburn: What explains why these properties cannot "come apart", given that they are not reducible to one another?

Moral properties obviously supervenes on natural. Two things cannot be "good" and "bad", to the same person, if they have the same natural properties. In many ways me and my twin brother are observationally supervenient.

Aesthetic, moral, and mental properties supervene on physical properties. (What if moral properties supervene on mental ones and physical ones also do that?)

There's only one bag of properties, and then humans assign other ones accordingly.

title: magic
subject: linguistics
contentHtml:
The reason why I became fixated on word vectors was that they had a power of unpredictability to them. Words are incredibly compressed, forceded meaning -- vectors have a power to them that is like a summer storm. But deep learning is not about creating vectors, it's about making predictions. And making predictions means being right. It requires a set of questions and a set of answers. It requires predictability. 

Vectors are the essence of all that is unpredictable and violent and messy about a language. And their utilization is only as stopgap for the predictable and the totally routine. It is harnessing lightning in a bottle to power grainmills. I love the violence and the totally adrift. I love the magic.

Can I make room in my heart for something less? It's hard when all you've done seems to tell you that it can go on. The blessing today is that it can go on. But not forever.

title: indexical
subject: linguistics
contentHtml:
we may be able to view moral judgements as indexicals: a natural property independent of other attributes of time, location etc.

We cannot deduce indexical judgements from non-indexical ones. For example, unless we already know which day is today (more naturally, unless we know what day it is, an indexical kind of knowledge), we cannot deduce from the date ofan event whether it occurred yesterday. Similarly, the moral properties of an event do not follow analytically from its natural properties.

### in what ways are moral judgements NOT indexical?

There's a sense in which they have permanence, whereas indexicals don't have permanence. But the *meaning* of "here" is usually clear, as is the *meaning* of "good". Can we draw more parallels between them?

- At least with "here" and "now", both can be imperatives: "come here", "be good". 

- here has the property of "to-be-locatedness" and now has the property of "to-be-passedness"

title: proofwriting_journal_7
subject: algorithms
contentHtml:
This week, we learned about a few different proof techniques (namely: direct, contrapositive, and by contradiction) and read about some good mathematical writing practices. 

Which of our proof techniques do you feel the most comfortable using and why? How was understanding these proof structure affected the way you approach problem-solving, if at all?

Looking at section 5.3 on good mathematical writing practices, which of the rules do you think you are most guilty of breaking in prior mathematical work? Do you understand/agree with the guidance given in this chapter, or were there suggestions that didn't feel natural to you?


I feel the most comfortable working with direct proof and with proof by contradiction, which are typically the first strategies I try. I really like the contrapositive but I'm not very good at recognizing cases in which it might apply. Understanding these proof structures have changed the way that I look at problems. For example, I don't think I've ever used "problem statement" in a proof until this class. This reflects the shift in my thinking about problems from a very ambiguous "try different configurations until you find something that works" to "understand what it would take to prove the statement and work from both ends".

Most of the guidance makes sense except for (2). I definitely don't end mathematical statements with a period, on an aesthetic basis. I'm not sure if I see this very commonly in the wild either, because that would leave "hanging periods" after centered equations. 

I'm guilty of not separating mathematical statements and words very well; a lot of times, I'll jump ahead with the math and write very terse English to accommpany it. I do use the active voice, and I think my use of symbols is just enough to work. I really enjoy elegance and will try to restructure my proofs to follow naturally. I think I usually have statements following joining words, but sometimes if it's simplifying a single term I'm not very careful.

There are no valid arguments with ethical conclusion and non-ethical premises in which the ethical vocabulary occurs essentially.

title: DL_conceptual3
subject: algorithms
contentHtml:
1a. A convolutional neural net is more suitable because it is partially connected, which means outputs are affected only by nearby inputs and not the whole image. This allows it to focus on local differences and patterns, such as detecting the edges in the digit pictures. The convolutions are also translational equivariant, whereas fully connected networks are more susceptible to shifts in position, and would have trouble classifying each digit as a 3.

b. The layer itself would produce different outputs because the pooling layer is not wide enough to encompass the translation. However, the neural net might still produce the correct outputs because the convolution is translationally equivariant, meaning that translations to the original image don't really (?) affect the output.

c. The convolutional layer would produce convolved outputs: its role is to identify features from the images. Using those features, the fully connected layer determines the classification of the image.

2a. Earlier convolutional layers might extract edges and specific local features of the polar bear, such as the eyes and nose. Later ones might locate the bear and provide spatial information, e.g. the relation of the head to the body.

b. We can determine the output width and height given the filter count, filter size, padding, and stride. We have 1 filter of 3 x 3 size, no padding because we are using the VALID method, and a stride of 2. Then we calculate both width and height to be $\frac{13 - 3 - 0}{2} + 1 = 6$: our convolved matrix is 6 by 6.


1a. We might use 1D CNNs for working with sequences; as we've seen with n-grams, the idea of learning filters to convolve with a sliding window of previous words to predict next words would be useful. Other situations in which a 1D CNN might be useful include DNA sequences, audio transcription, or predicting traffic jams. By training with sensor data which counts cars at different times of day, the CNN could learn patterns of traffic and predict times of day when routes are busy.

2b. Our 1D CNN could learn 1D filters that correspond to semantic features of the tweet, and then combine those in a fully connected network to determine the emotion expressed. We would train the model on labeled data, with input tweet and ground truth classification provided. Our CNN kernel could convolve over one-hot encoded words in the tweet. We could take into account variable tweet sizes by getting a set of probabilities per sentence and averaging them for the tweet.

Drawing on the podcast and paper, discuss one technology-driven (implemented using technology/software) and one human-driven method (manually implemented using humans) Apple is using to protect user’s privacy while identifying known CSAM images. (4-6 sentences)


One technology-driven method in the plan involved Private Set Intersection, a cryptographic protocol used between Apple servers and user devices. On the server side, a series of transformations are done on the NeuralHashes of known CSAM images, and followed with a final blinding step that only the server knows. On the user side, the same series of transformations would be carried out, but the final step is done on the server after a photo is uploaded. Only if an image hash matches a known CSAM hash is the server able to decrypt the data and view the image.

One human-driven method was the manual checking of images by a human reviewer at Apple. Given that a Photos account has both found CSAM matches and reached a threshold of those matches, the images in question is revealed and viewed to verify that they are the same. Because the PSI protocol still protects the encryption of other images on an iCloud Photos account, only the matching images would be revealed to the human reviewer.

This week's problem set was the first significantly challenging one for me and I enjoyed it a lot. I found problems #4 and #5 challenging, particularly the subtractive/additive one and the n + 2 choose 3 one. Neither felt especially stressful: I did struggle with doubts about #3 for some time until the problem statement was clarified. 

The really fun problems included the subtractive/additive one, because it involved combining several substantial parts of counting and probability and the use of my own intuition, and upon reflection, problem #3. I also enjoyed the second proof from problem 4, which had both macro (which set to choose from?) and micro (how many ways to choose from the set?) levels.

This time, I found myself spending way more time solving problems than writing solutions, although this was the first homework from proofwriting where this was the case. For example, the logic tables in Problem Set 3 was good for practicing LaTex but very tedious to create.

I feel like I am approaching my problems in a more clear and separable way than I did before. Counting problems in particular require this approach (Either X, or Y) and I found myself rewording my solutions even as I progressed within the problem set.

title: error_theory_sum
subject: linguistics
contentHtml:
A common argument is that the error theory states that there are no normative properties. Thus, the error theory entails that there is no reason to believe the error theory, as the property of being a reason for belief is a normative theory.

This is not the argument Streumer makes. Streumer argues that the above only shows that if the error theory is true, there is no reason to believe the error theory. But we do not believe the error theory.

It is not a problem for a theory if we cannot believe it; it could still be true. Our inability to sincerely say that a theorem is true does nothing to show it is false.

title: prior_hume
subject: linguistics
contentHtml:
The open question

If there are moral facts, they would have to be queer.
There are no naturalistic properties that are queer.
Then there are no facts that are moral facts.
Then there are no moral facts.


Why is Moore so confident that Q1 is going to be Open no matter what we choose for N?
Maybe we are actually defining naturalistic properties in opposition to moral ones.

- Open question: a question that needs more information to be answered

### begging the question
- P, therefore P is the most logically valid statement you can make. It could be a terrible *explanation* but it's not a (formal) fallacy! It's valid!
- Thus, begging the question is a deficit in rhetoric, not in logic
- this is closely associated to telling the truth but still being evasive (honesty should cut as close to the desired truth as possible)

Hume's is-ought problem is this exact idea, that some things are descriptive and others that are prescriptive and there's no way to bridge the gap.

one attempt: effective altruism ?

tautology finder!
Why isn't the question of "good" a simple tautology?
many people are convinced by the Open Question Argument.

Not all seemingly ethical statements are actually "oughts":
- eating artichokes is pleasurable
- Amelia is generous

"It is impossible to make moral (prescriptive) generalizations from factual (descriptive) statements".

### Hume's law
there is no valid argument whose conclusion is ethical and premises non-ethical. -> we cannot deduce statements about the future from statements about the past.


- is this true?
- is it *significant?*

Are there valid arguments whos arguments are ethical and conclusion non-ethical (?)

() Not eating meat is good.
() Therefore, you should not eat meat.

*what does [[ought]] mean?*

(D) Tea drinking is common in England
(E) All New Zealanders ought to be shot.

Either tea drinking is common in England on 3/2/2023, or all New Zealanders ought to be shot. This must be true! But is it useful at all? Because tea drinking is clearly common in England in 3/2/2023.

### Prior's quantificational example

This is supposed to draw an ethical conclusion from a non-ethical premise. Undertakers are church officers. (Pierce okay!)
Therefore, Undertakers ought to do whatever all church officers ought to do. (Pierce okay!)

Is that an ethical statement? It isn't really...

title: meno
subject: linguistics
contentHtml:
Socrates believes that those who seek bad things are simply misguided; that is, they do not know those things are bad. He uses Meno's notion of a bad thing -- such as greed -- to illustrate that although someone may believe a thing to be bad, they still believe it benefits them. 

I read this as elevating the human will above other considerations: although someone might commit suicide knowing it is bad relative to the judgements of society, Socrates implies that their internal calculus makes suicide good, or at least more appealing than the alternative.

Meno is saying that he himself can't believe it... but who's to trust Meno with basic opinion?

BAD! 
Redefining want as purely (go towards your (personal) good)

"Nobody knowingly acts towards the bad"

title: beras
subject: algorithms
contentHtml:
In this assignment, we will be constructing a Keras mimic, Beras. There are no TODOs in the notebook; rather, the testing is done by running the main method of `assignment.py`.

The stencil provides a model class with several methods and hyperparameters to use in the network.


Labs 1-3 have important skills I will need:

1. Implementing Callable and Diffable Components
2. Implementing Optimizers
   1. Can implement BasicOptimizer by following the logic from gradient_descent method in lab 1.
3. Using batch_step and GradientTape
   1. batch_step and GradientTape mimic the Keras API.


Intro contains information on the functions I'm coding.
Test contains unit tests for functions.

### preprocessing Task 1
Fill in the load_and_preprocess_data function
- flatten and normalize input images
- convert data to Beras.Tensor


### Task 2 one-hot: 
Categorical data does not have pre-determined ordering.
- implement fit() in beras/onehot.py
  - use np.eye for one-hot encodings
- one-hot encode training and testing labels in assignment.py
- call() in beras/onehot.py 
  - passes a vector of all actual labels in training set, calls fit() to populate the uniq2oh dictionary with unique labels and corresponding 1-hot encodings
  - returns: an array of 1-hot encoded laels for each label in the training set.
- inverse()

### Another task:
Abstract classes of models:
- Callable
    - CategoricalAccuracy: computes accuracy of probabilities against list of ground truth labels.
    - OneHotEncoder: one-hot encoding a class instance into a probability distribution (?).

- Diffable
  - We use Diffable to represent differentiable layers for which we can compute gradients for:
    - Dense GIVEN
    - LeakyReLU
    - MeanSquaredError GIVEN

- Dense example of diffable (notes)

### Task 3: implement gradient method of GradientTape
which returns a list of gradients corresponding to the list of trainable weights in the network (weight gradients).

In HW1, to assign gradients we just had to call MSELoss.backward(), which calculated the gradients and passed them backwards.

In HW2, to assign gradients we want to follow how Tensorflow does it. It centralizes the operation within gradient_tape. 

Start at output (Loss), work through same operations implemented in each backward in HW1, and move backwards following the inputs and weights encountered.

### Task 4: implement _initialize_weight()
    - default to initializing all at 0 (bad idea)
    - Normal: normal allows for unit normal
    - Xavier Normal: xavier allows for keras.GlorotNormal
    - Kaiming He Normal: kaiming allows for keras.HeNormal

### Task 5: Sigmoid(): call(), and input_gradients() 
### Task 6: LeakyReLU(): call(), and input_gradients()

These activations are per element: override compose_to_input() to vectorize across outputs (?) an easier way to do the backwards pass?

compile(): initalize model optimizer, loss function, and accuracy function, which are fed in as arguments for SequentialModel instance.

fit(): Trains model to associate input to outputs. Training is repeated for each epoch, and the data is batched based on argument. It also computes metrics.

### Task 7: evaluate()
Evaluate performance of final model, like fit() does.

### Task 8: call()
assignment.py/SequentialModel/call(). Each layer has exactly one input vector and one output vector.

### Task 9: batch_step()
Compute model predictions for batch. Compute gradients and update weights with GradientTape; use optimizer, and compute/return loss and accuracy. 

### Tasks 10-12
Basic Optimizer
RMSProp
Adam

# Task 13
Beras/metrics.py/forward(): categorical accuracy?

# Task 14: 
get_simple_model(): based on the simplest model in get_simplest_model(), construct a simple model that uses my implementations, has two layers, and uses MSE while binding predictions to [0,1].


## Overview
- HW2 conceptual
- Beras, make SequentialModel in assignment.py
- test SequentialModel with main method of assignment.pyi
- included notebooks only for reference
- README with model accuracy
- Grading: achieve the desired accuracy

subject: algorithms
contentHtml:
I find it easier to understand set notation than I did before. In following the logic/ separating it from particular proofs from MATH 540, I didn't struggle much -- primarily it was understanding the conceptual basis and knowing where to start proofs that I struggled with.

I do feel better equipped, and I really, really enjoy thinking about counting problems. I think one benefit of this class and others is realizing the advantages of strict notation as opposed to intuition. The reason many of courses have begun with set theory is so we can draw distinctions between classes of objects.

The proof you demonstrated from permutations to combinations was really interesting to me, because it involves a set partition and the addition principle, which we just covered in APMA 1655 (the third axiom for a probability space is  that the probability of the infinite union of sets is equivalent to the infinite sum of probabilities of sets, given that the sets are disjoint). I also find the modus tollens strategy interesting, as it relates to my Morality class. Essentially: "If morality is relative, we would act a certain way. But we don't act that way, so morality can't be relative!":

"Suppose the reason anthropologist and undergraduate Relativists backslide is that Relativism has implications that we do not or even cannot accept. Then the objection could be cast as a modus tollens argument: if Moral Relativism is true, then so are these consequences; but the consequences are false, so Moral Relativism is false."

Answers to 4 are looking good!

For problem 5d, you have (there exists fooled person for all time) and (all person fooled some time) and (all fooled person implies not all time). That 3rd part is not a statement, and conjoining them with ^ is not a great idea.

Note that fooling someone is a function. If we define P as persons, T as times, and f(x,t) as a boolean function for fooling a person at a particular time, we might have:

- (there exists x in P such that for all t in T: f(x,t))
- (for all x in P there exists t in T: f(x,t))
- not (for all x in P, all t in T: f(x, t))

contentHtml:
$P_X(x_0) = F_X(x_0) - lim_{x\to x_0^-}F_X(x)$

### kinship terms

N "of PP": `<e, <e,t>>`
<e<e,t>> : parent as parent'(y)(x)
x is the parent of y

how to reduce the information here? maybe every individual has parent metadata, such that MaryJohn-Jake.

- x is sister of y iff x is sibling of y and x is female

pibling: parent's sibling. auncle

Generally, English kinship terminology can be reduced to three variables: gender (F/M), parent-of, and spouse-of. We might have parent-of (bottom-up) or child-of (top-down). Several children, fixed parents.

Either no gender expressed or gender is on (x), the person the kinship term refers to.

- Can we encode gender for both the y and x? (paternal grandmother? maternal grandfather?) The english language currently does not. 

A thought experiment about the terms "wife" and "husband": if you only learned the meaning of wife by looking at the world, x is wife of y: it could be a female spouse of y, or spouse of male y. However, because we know that, as a general rule, gender is marked on x (the subject), we know that 'wife' must be the *female* spouse of y, not the spouse of male y.

grandmother: female parent parent 
great-grandmother: female parent parent parent ...
mother-in-law: female spouse parent

## semantic classes of verbs

given any intervval I, if some state holds during I, it holds for all subintervals in i (every point in I)

"I was asleep for a whole 8 hours last night!"
"I was working for a whole 8 hours last night!"

### durative adverbs

"I was asleep"
"I was asleep for a whole 8 hours": a state holds uniformly for some interval: adverbial phrase, maybe "for a whole 8 hours last night". We can say "for an hour" specifying INTERVAL.

- State verbs are bad with things like "in an hour", which specify culmination points specifying POINT.

- State verbs are usually bad with imperatives: "be tall". However, there are states in conscious control that you can use imperatives with, e.g. "Be happy" and "Be nice". 

- State verbs are often odd with progressives, although not always. Examples of ungrammatical state verbs in the progressive sense:
  - "be red" -> *"The book is being red"
  -  "know" -> *"I am knowing the answer"
  -  "be large" -> *"Loki is being large".

Exceptions include reflections, where the state verb is more activity-like: "think" -> "I am thinking that I should not try to cover so much material". This is because most activity verbs have accomplishment senses in special contexts. 

### activities

In general, activities denote 'events' rather than 'states'. Often, but not always, these events are under the conscious control of the subject: e.g. Sally ran. However, we also have activity verbs "roll" -> "the ball rolled". Activities don't have to have agentive subjects.

We have temporal consistency, but for larger subintervals: "she is running" does not imply that someone is running every second. 

- We can say "ran" -> "Sally ran for an hour" (durative adverbs) (INTERVAL).

- Activity verbs are also bad with things like "in an hour", because there is no culmination point: *"Sally ran in an hour" (POINT). 

- However, if we add an adjunct endpoint such as "Sally ran to the store in an hour" it works because it makes the verb phrase telic.

- With activity verbs, we have imperatives as many activities are under conscious control.

- In general, activities and states are **atelic**, where they do not have a specific end point, while accomplishments and achievements are **telic**, where they have a end point. These are properties of the verb phrase and not the specific verbs.

### achievements

Achievements have a culmination point:

die, reach the summit, notice the flea...

They have instantaneous success (sort of).

- Achievement verbs are bad with "for an hour" because that wants states or activities with internal temporal consistency (INTERVAL).
- Achievement verbs are usually okay with "in an hour" (POINT).
- Achievement verbs are generally strange with imperatives

Some can be paraphrased as become + state (will return, become dead, become aware...)

### accomplishments

Accomplishments have a preparatory/activity phase and a culmination point (usually transitive verbs with objects). 

Activity -> become + state

build a house: you can be building a house and you can have built a house. write a paper, bake a cake, destroy the evidence, eat an apple... Some are verbs of creation and some verbs of destruction. The accomplishment "mow the lawn" is interesting because it is not an act of creation but is treated like one (well, actually it is because you are striving towards an idealized goal, but by the negative: "cut hair", "sculpt bust"). 

It often takes very little to make an accomplishment statement ("I am writing a book", "I am learning to play the piano"), because the activity can be trivial.

Running is an activity (interval), but she was running is a state (point).

title: deep_learning_2
subject: algorithms
contentHtml:
1. We use cross-entropy loss because it takes an output vector of probabilities and one-hot y, with length m for the number of categories for a single sample. It calculates a vector across each possible class. On the other hand, MSE is the mean squared error of single output values, and it returns the mean of squared errors for a model across many samples. It's better for regression tasks, where you want to fit a line to a collection of points. Additionally, because MSE uses decimal representations, it will take longer to calculate small probabilities.

sum across categories: y_ilog(P_i)

y_i

In lecture, you learned about gradient descent. What is a gradient? How is it different from a partial derivative? How do they relate? (2-4 sentences)

num * S num * t num * u
s * t, t * u 

1. A gradient is a collection of partial derivatives for a scalar function. (?) It is different from a partial derivative because that is part of a gradient.

2. We negate this quantity because the partial derivative is, loosely speaking, the slope in the upwards direction. To minimize the loss by changing the weights, we want to go downwards.

3. We calculate the partial derivative for all weights because all weights contribute to the model. We should not only reward correct weights; if a weight contributed to an incorrect answer, it should be lowered. 

4. Vectorized operations allow us to use parallelized computation, and low-level routines like BLAS. GPUs can run vectorized operations faster than traditional architecture can.

5. Gradient descent ends up at the bottom. The model has two weights I think. If we use regular gradient descent, we won't ever reach the absolute minimum. This is because the gradient of the loss with respect to x and y is (0,0), and so no update will take place. 

6. Empirical risk is the risk observed in the sample dataset. It differs from true risk because the sample dataset may not be representative of the actual risk. We want to minimize empirical risk so we can be right.

7. Okay

title: dowty_telic
subject: linguistics
contentHtml:
Aristotle observed that if X is a performance verb ("build), then "A is (now) Xing" entails "A has not (yet) Xed"; however, if X is an activity verb ("live"), then "A is (now) Xing" entails "A has Xed". This is the difference of accomplishment and activity.

- This is relevant to Cristofaro's study of subordination where activity verbs are classified as having a higher semantic integration (corresponding SoA) than performance verbs.

Vendler classes:

states,    activities,    accomplishments,   achievements
know       run            paint a picture    recognize
think
believe
hope/want
be tall
be red

states and achievements both lack progressive tenses (?)

Is "John recites a poem" really more frequentative than singular? "Appear in a narrative in the historical present" feels like "telling a story", and I think that's the most common use of this sentence.

In activity verbs, it is harder to have the secondary interpretation of accomplishments e.g. "John almost painted a picture" could have John paint most of a picture while "John almost walked" does not.

most activity verbs have accomplishment senses in special contexts ("look" -> "looking at the paper you sent me")

A bigger challenge

accomplishment verbs which take direct objects unexpectedly behave like activities IF an indefinite plural or mass-noun is substituted for the definite one:

(59)
John ate the bag of popcorn in an hour <-> *John ate popcorn in an hour.

*John discovered the buried treasure in his backyard for six weeks. <-> John discovered fleas on his dog for 6 weeks. 

*John discovered that quaint little village for years. <-> Tourists discovered that quaint little village for years.

Sooo the boundary between achievement and activity verbs are blurred. Around 1967, we could have postulated syntactic features such as +-durative and state selectional restrictions, for example between verbs with these features and time adverbials like for x time and in x time. Verkuyl was very aware of principle (59): main thesis is that notions of durative and perfective aspect are not to be found in any one constituent in surface structure, but arise from the "composition" of certain constituents.

title: relativism_nihilism
subject: linguistics
contentHtml:
It would probably be a bad thing if most people believed that they are not responsible for anything they ever do. But that is not an argument against Incompatibilism
or Determinism.

Similarly, philosophy professors sometimes report that their students (and colleagues) hold both relativistic metaethical views and also absolutist particular moral views.

Suppose the reason anthropologist and
undergraduate Relativists backslide is that Relativism has implications that we do not or even cannot accept. Then the objection could be cast as a modus tollens argument: if Moral Relativism is true, then so are these consequences; but the consequences are false, so Moral Relativism is false.

...Bernard Williams thought it a fallacy to infer a principle of tolerance from relativis-
tic premises, and the form of argument he presented certainly is fallacious.

taste and aesthetics are similar to morals except when it comes to conflict between individuals: when, upon reflection, we judge that it is morally wrong for the Majoritarians to disenfranchise their political minority, we are already committed to the judgment that they ought not to do it. In this way moral judgment is unlike the judgment that artichokes taste good, which does not commit me to the judgment that you ought to eat them.

Morals commit / condemn others. Taste does not.

(?) Moral Relativism as we have been understanding it does not seem to have any particularly unpalatable normative moral consequences. Indeed, it seems unlikely that Moral Relativism itself has normative moral consequences, any more than the meta-
aesthetic view that beauty is in the eye of the beholder has any particular implications
about what is beautiful, or the view that taste is relative to the taster has any particular implications about what tastes good.

Internalists believe that Moral judgement must have some reason-giving force for people to accept it.

isolated speakers: "Gog" and "Bab", and utilitarian "Noog" and "Nad". The functional role of moral judgement is tied to it conceptually. Deciding what is morally right is, in part, deciding what to do, or what to favor.

Mackie's Internalist Argument for Nihilism

(1) Moral goodness would have to be such that sincere judgement about it is intrinsically motivational. 
(2) But there is no property such that sincere judgement about it is intrinsically motivational.
(3) So, there is no such thing as moral goodness.
On the whole, most people who judge something good generally are thereby motivated...

Humean premise (2): For the premise to be false, there would have to be a property whose apprehension would move any rational being as such. The apprehension itself would have already to be a motivational state. It is this internal connection that Humeans deny. They say: representation of the world as being this way or that, is one thing, and motivation is
another. For a representation of the way the world is to make a difference in our deciding what to do, it must first be joined with some sort of desire, or valuing, or aim of ours. 

Dreier's Internalist Argument for Relativism

Moral goodness is such that sincere judgment about it intrinsically motivates.

But, which properties motivate depends on the psychology of the judging agent.

So, which properties are the moral ones depends on the psychology of the agent.

If internalism is true, then morality is either unreal, or relative.

Suppose we have a terrorist who says: (A) The intentional slaughter of millions of innocent non-combatants is morally justified as a means to promote my end.

And we have anti-terrorist leaders say (B) The intentional slaughter of millions of innocent non-combatants cannot be morally justified under any circumstances.

Fairly obvious points: (B) disagrees with (A), and (B) could be prefaced by "(A) is not true". However, Relativism denies both these points. The relativist thinks of (A) and (B) as containing an implicit relativization to the speaker's moral outlook. There's never *really* a moral disagreement, hm?

One possible defense is that if a police officer states that he saw the defendant enter and leave one hour later, and the defense says "can you provide an inertial time frame?", we wouldn't say that his statement is incorrect. The incorrect *theory* of the Policeman's 'one hour' does not infect the integrity or veracity of his ordinary, first order judgment.

So long as the ordinary judgements are understood relative to whatever standards make most sense in the context, they can be perfectly correct just as the policeman's judgements are perfectly correct if understood relative to the inertial frame of the courtroom. Nihilism is no more called for in ethics than it is in common sense physics.

title: skepticism

A path to general skepticism:

- my perceptions might be illusions
- But... my senses are pretty reliable! Only in special circumstances do they go awry. 
- But how do you know they are reliable?

PERCEPTION DOES NOT JUSTIFY PERCEPTION
MORAL JUDGMENT DOES NOT JUSTIFY MORAL JUDGEMENTS

Actually, moral judgements are MUCH WORSE off than perception, because... you can't make observation, you can't have moral FACTS, you can't reason from nothing about morality!

Adaptive link is the idea that judgements about reason is like reflexive mechanisms. 
- Maybe morality is a socially formed thing?

Well, one argument against this is that variability in what is reality must be limited to your experience; e.g, most things are going to be the same most of the time. Object permanence?

We do not have moral sensibility.

title: tam
subject: linguistics
contentHtml:
Tense—the position of the state or action in time, that is, whether it is in the past, present or future.


    Aspect—the extension of the state or action in time, that is, whether it is unitary (perfective), continuous or repeated (imperfective).


    Mood or Modality—the reality of the state or action, that is, whether it is actual (realis), a possibility or a necessity (irrealis).

title: deep_learning_notes
subject: algorithms
contentHtml:
## Deep Learning review
a simple neural network (one layer) is conceptualized as $$y = logit = w_1x_1 + w_2x_2 + ... + w_kx_k + b$$

or really, logit = Wx + b.

With multiple outputs, m, we would have m sets of linear functions and sets of weight vectors, as well as m biases. Then we'd have W as 3 x 7 (which is 3 x 1 weights, with seven outputs). 
x is inputs (7 x 1)
b is biases (3 x 1)

m is outputs, n is inputs. W is (m, n) n,1 -> m,1

$$ Wx +  b = (m, n) * (n, 1) + (m, 1) $$

$$\Delta W = -a \dot \Delta_w L$$

Computing a batch of outputs (matmul):

(m, n) * (n, 10) + (m, 10) = (m, 10)

(m, 10) can be implicitly broacasted by NumPy, Tensorflow, PyTorch from (m, 1). 

In general, we can operate on any two arrays, when starting with trailing dimensions, all dimensions are equal or one of them is 1. 

Ex (matmul): (m, n) * (n, 10) + (m, 1) = (m, 10)

tf.constant('hello world').numpy().decode() to get string
tf.constant('hello world').numpy() to get byte-decoded
tf.add(x, W) == x + W

tf.random.normal([2,2]) gives 2 by 2 random values

tf.argmax will return the index with largest value across the axes of tensor

B = tf.constant([[2, 20, 30, 3, 6], [3, 11, 16, 1, 8],
                 [14, 45, 23, 5, 27]])

tf.argmax(B) = tf.argmax(B,0) = [2,2,0,2,2]
tf.argmax(B,1) = [2,2,1]
    
tf.transpose(x)
tf.shape(x)
tf.reduce_mean(x, axis=1)
tf.reshape(x, [-1]) #flattens to 1-D

with tf.GradientTape() as tape:
    loss = model.get_loss(inputs, outputs)

grads = tape.gradient(loss, [model.W, model.b])

2/13/2023

Sam is investigating sample efficiency in neural networks. Deep Learning may be on the wrong track, because the efficiency per sample is much lower than animal or human learning. However, biological learning may not be as sample efficient as it seems because evolution helps us learn familiar concepts easily.

Louis is starting a mechanical interpretation of deception. This trains a LLM with RL - human feedback to be helpful (but not honest). Uses trivia questions -- you are not penalized for giving wrong answers if the reasoning is right.

valid ways of initializing variables: 

```
tf.Variable([0,1])

tf.GradientTape() records tensor computations. 

```
w = tf.Variable([[1.0]])
with tf.GradientTape() as tape:

grad = tape.gradient(loss, w)
print(grad)

title: PF_journal_3
subject: algorithms
contentHtml:
Helpful parts of the course structure includes doing lots of exercises, and challenging exercises! I really like going to class, being able to attempt problems in a judgement-free setting, and getting things wrong, instead of listening to someone lecture and half-absorbing content.

I'm not facing any challenges yet, but I would find it very helpful to get fast feedback on my problem sets. Previously, there was around a three-week delay for some of our problem sets to be graded, and that really impacted my ability to absorb the feedback in a useful way. I like that the peer review system allows you to get some help almost instantaneously!

It's been about what I expected, maybe slightly easier; the second problem set was also on the basics of set theory, which I did not expect. I think I'm learning a lot from this class, however: between the whiteboard sessions in class and looking at the way others solve problems, it is shaping me into a well-rounded mathematician. 

Some things that I am curious about include why we find it more intuitive to look at circular diagrams as opposed to rectangular ones (my hypothesis is that the "squareness" implies a false degree of certainty), the relation between logic and basic building blocks of computation (half-adder, perhaps?), the relation between logic and fields, infinite sets and cardinality of the power set of the natural numbers, and if we can derive anything mathematically useful from the notion of "fuzzy sets".

Some more things I am curious about include Peano arithmetic, the system of defining numbers as sets, the ZF axioms, and basic proofs in number theory!

title: PF_PS2_peer_review_2
subject: algorithms
contentHtml:
Hello! I like the way you write products in set notation, definitely using that in the future.

For problem 1.2, the cartesian product do not include ordered pairs where either element is empty. If you think of it in terms of (x,y) coordinates, it doesn't make sense to have something like (,2) or (2,) ever, right? We could have (1,2) or (0,2) but not (,2).

For problem 1.4, you describe the set (correctly) as the set of all subsets of the power set, and then you give a different definition of that same set again! Just write out the first definition as a set and you should be good.

For problem 4.4 (I made this mistake too), the power set of N includes the empty set, so your answer would be {emptyset, N}!

Your solutions for problem 5 are masterful!

title: PF_PS2_peer_review
subject: algorithms
contentHtml:
Hi, this is a good try with some errors!

For problem 1(a), your set notation should be consistent: the 4th set should have brackets around it as $\{\emptyset, \{a\}\}$.

For problem 1(b), the cartesian product do not include ordered pairs where either element is empty. If you think of it in terms of (x,y) coordinates, it doesn't make sense to have something like (,2) or (2,) ever, right? We could have (1,2) or (0,2) but not (,2).

For 3.1, the intersection should be written $\emptyset$, not the set containing it; $\emptyset$ is not a member of either set being intersected.

For 4.2, consider that the union of intervals $[i, i+1]$ over all $i\in \mathbb{N}$ does not cover $\mathbb{R}$, only positive numbers!

For 4.3, I'm confused why you think it is a cuboid; where's the 3rd dimension?
